| Commercial Ethylene Dose for Ripening | $100 \text{ – } 150 \; \mu\text{L/L}$ for 24–48 h at 18–22 °C | µL/L = ppm | For banana, mango, tomato |
| 1-MCP Dose (Ethylene Blocker) | $0.5 \text{ – } 1.0 \; \mu\text{L/L}$ for 12–24 h | µL/L | Binds irreversibly to ETR1 receptors |

### 2.4 The Ethylene Receptors and Signal Transduction Pathway

Ethylene is perceived by a family of **membrane-bound receptors (ETR1, ETR2, ERS1, ERS2, EIN4)** located on the **endoplasmic reticulum (ER)** membrane. They contain a **copper cofactor (Cu⁺)** required for ethylene binding, supplied by the **RAN1 (Responsive to Antagonist 1)** copper transporter.

The simplified signal cascade is:

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 \rightarrow \text{ETR1/ERS1} \rightarrow \text{CTR1 (Raf-like kinase, OFF)} \rightarrow \text{EIN2 (ON)} \rightarrow \text{EIN3/EIL} \rightarrow \text{ERF1} \rightarrow \text{Target Genes (Ripening)}$$

* **CTR1** is a **negative regulator** — in the *absence* of ethylene, CTR1 is active and suppresses EIN2.
* Upon ethylene binding, **CTR1 is inactivated**, **EIN2 is cleaved**, and the **C-terminal fragment of EIN2 moves to the nucleus**, activating **EIN3/EIL transcription factors**, which in turn activate **Ethylene Response Factors (ERFs)** that drive ripening-related gene expression.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation of Ethylene Biosynthesis

Below is the **complete, line-by-line derivation** of the Yang Cycle. This is the type of answer that fetches full 7 marks in KTU valuation.

---

**Step 1: Activation of Methionine**
The sulfur atom of the amino acid **L-Methionine** is activated by ATP in a reaction catalysed by the enzyme **Methionine Adenosyltransferase (MAT)**. The product is **S-Adenosyl-L-Methionine (SAM)**, which is the universal biological methyl donor.

$$\text{L-Methionine} + \text{ATP} \xrightarrow{\text{MAT}} \text{S-Adenosyl-L-Methionine (SAM)} + \text{PP}_i + \text{P}_i$$

**Why this step?** Methionine itself is chemically inert for ethylene synthesis. Activation by ATP raises its free energy and creates a high-energy sulfonium centre that enables subsequent bond cleavage.

---

**Step 2: Conversion of SAM to ACC (The Rate-Limiting Step)**
The enzyme **ACC Synthase (ACS)** — a pyridoxal-5′-phosphate (PLP)-dependent enzyme — cleaves SAM between the **5′-methylthio group** and the **ribose**, releasing **5′-Methylthioadenosine (MTA)** and forming the novel cyclopropane ring compound **1-Aminocyclopropane-1-Carboxylic Acid (ACC)**.

$$\text{SAM} \xrightarrow[\text{(PLP cofactor)}]{\text{ACC Synthase (ACS)}} \text{ACC} + 5'\text{-Methylthioadenosine (MTA)}$$

**Why this is rate-limiting:**
* ACS is encoded by a **multigene family** (e.g., *ACS1, ACS2* in tomato) and is **highly regulated** at the transcriptional level.
* The half-life of ACS mRNA is only **~25 minutes**, making it the **primary control point** for ethylene biosynthesis.
* This is the **main target of 1-MCP (1-Methylcyclopropene)** and **AVG (Aminoethoxyvinylglycine)** inhibition strategies.

---

**Step 3: Oxidation of ACC to Ethylene**
The enzyme **ACC Oxidase (ACO)** — a member of the **non-heme iron (Fe²⁺), ascorbate-dependent dioxygenase** family — catalyses the oxidation of ACC using **molecular O₂** as a co-substrate and **ascorbate + Fe²⁺** as cofactors.

$$\text{ACC} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow[\text{Ascorbate, Fe}^{2+}]{\text{ACC Oxidase (ACO)}} \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{HCN} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Important note:** One of the products is **hydrogen cyanide (HCN)**, which is toxic. Plant cells immediately detoxify HCN via the enzyme **β-Cyanoalanine Synthase**, which converts it to **β-cyanoalanine** using cysteine.

---

**Step 4: Recycling of MTA back to Methionine (The Yang Cycle Closure)**
The **5′-Methylthioadenosine (MTA)** produced in Step 2 is recycled in five enzymatic steps back to **Methionine**, ensuring that the cell does not deplete its sulfur pool. The intermediates are:
**MTA → 5-Methylthioribose (MTR) → 5-Methylthio-ribose-1-phosphate (MTR-1-P) → 2-Keto-4-methylthiobutyrate (KMB) → Methionine.**

$$\text{MTA} \xrightarrow{\text{5-step recycling}} \text{L-Methionine}$$

This closed-loop recycling means that **a single methionine molecule can theoretically sustain the production of many ethylene molecules**, making the pathway highly efficient.

---

**Step 5: Net Stoichiometry of One Turn of the Yang Cycle**

Combining all four steps and cancelling the internal intermediates (SAM, MTA), the **net reaction** simplifies to:

$$\text{L-Methionine} + \text{ATP} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{HCN} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{AMP} + \text{PP}_i + \text{P}_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Accounting for KTU:**
> One molecule of ethylene is produced at the cost of **1 ATP equivalent (ATP → AMP + PPᵢ + Pᵢ)**, which is the energetic price paid by the ripening fruit.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Conversion from ppm to µL/L for Ethylene Concentration

> [!NOTE]
> KTU numerical problems often ask: *"Express 150 ppm of ethylene in µL/L and in mol/m³ at 25 °C and 1 atm pressure."* Use the derivation below.

**Step 1: Define ppm (parts per million) for gases**
For gases, $1 \text{ ppm} = 1 \text{ part per million parts of air} = 1 \; \mu\text{L of C}_2\text{H}_4 \text{ per L of air}$.

$$150 \text{ ppm} = 150 \; \mu\text{L/L} = 150 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{L/L}$$

**Step 2: Convert volume to moles using Ideal Gas Law**
The molar volume of an ideal gas at 25 °C and 1 atm is:

$$V_m = 22.414 \; \text{L/mol} \times \dfrac{298.15 \text{ K}}{273.15 \text{ K}} \approx 24.47 \; \text{L/mol}$$

**Step 3: Calculate moles per litre of air**

$$n = \dfrac{150 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{L}}{24.47 \; \text{L/mol}} = 6.13 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{mol/L}$$

**Step 4: Convert to mol/m³**

$$[\text{C}_2\text{H}_4] = 6.13 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{mol/L} \times 1000 \; \text{L/m}^3 = 6.13 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mol/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{[\text{C}_2\text{H}_4] \approx 6.13 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mol/m}^3 \text{ at 25 °C, 1 atm}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Physiological Effects of Ethylene

| Stage / Process | Ethylene Effect | Molecular Basis | KTU High-Yield Point |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fruit Ripening | Triggers climacteric respiration & autocatalytic C₂H₄ | Activates PG, PE, amylase, carotenoid genes | Most important — repeated question |
| Pigment Change | Chlorophyll degradation + Carotenoid/Anthocyanin synthesis | ↑ Chlorophyllase, ↑ PSY (Phytoene Synthase) | Greening → Yellowing in banana |
| Sugar Accumulation | Starch → Glucose, Fructose, Sucrose | ↑ α-Amylase, ↑ Sucrose-Phosphate Synthase | Sweetness in banana, mango |
| Cell Wall Softening | Pectin breakdown → Texture softening | ↑ Polygalacturonase (PG), ↑ Pectin Methyl Esterase (PME) | Pulp softening in tomato |
| Aroma Volatiles | Production of esters, aldehydes, alcohols | ↑ LOX, ↑ ADH, ↑ AAT enzymes | Banana, mango aroma |
| Epinasty (Leaf Bending) | Causes downward curvature of leaves | Asymmetric cell expansion on adaxial side | Diagnostic test for C₂H₄ |
| Abscission | Leaf & fruit drop | ↑ Cellulase in abscission zone | Used in mechanical harvesting |
| Triple Response (Seedlings) | Shortened hypocotyl, thickened stem, horizontal growth | Classical bioassay for C₂H₄ | Dark-grown pea seedling test |
| Stress Response | Biotic/abiotic stress → C₂H₄ burst | Wounding, pathogen attack, chilling | Defence signalling |
| Sex Expression | Promotes female flowers in monoecious plants | In cucumbers, cucurbits | Horticultural use |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Yang Cycle — Ethylene Biosynthesis Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Methionine"] --> B["ATP Activation MAT Enzyme"]
    B --> C["S Adenosyl Methionine SAM"]
    C --> D["ACC Synthase ACS Rate Limiting Step"]
    D --> E["ACC 1 Aminocyclopropane Carboxylic Acid"]
    D --> F["MTA Byproduct 5 Methylthioadenosine"]
    F --> G["5 Step Recycling Pathway"]
    G --> A
    E --> H["ACC Oxidase ACO Uses O2 Ascorbate Fe2+"]
    H --> I["Ethylene C2H4 Gas"]
    H --> J["CO2 Released"]
    H --> K["HCN Toxic Byproduct"]
    K --> L["Detoxified by Beta Cyanoalanine Synthase"]
```

### 4.2 Climacteric vs Non-Climacteric Respiratory Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Climacteric[Climacteric Fruit Banana Mango Tomato]
        C1["Phase 1 Pre Climacteric"] --> C2["Phase 2 Climacteric Peak Sharp Rise in CO2 and C2H4"]
        C2 --> C3["Phase 3 Post Climacteric Senescence"]
    end
    subgraph NonClim[Non Climacteric Fruit Citrus Grape Strawberry]
        N1["Harvest"] --> N2["Steady Gradual Decline in Respiration"]
        N2 --> N3["No Ethylene Burst Senescence"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Ethylene Signal Transduction Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Ethylene C2H4 Gas Molecule"] --> P2["Binds to ETR1 ERS1 Receptors on ER Membrane"]
    P2 --> P3["Copper Cofactor Cu Plus Supplied by RAN1 Transporter"]
    P3 --> P4["CTR1 Negative Regulator Raf like Kinase Becomes INACTIVE"]
    P4 --> P5["EIN2 Cleaved C terminus Enters Nucleus"]
    P5 --> P6["Activates EIN3 EIL Transcription Factors"]
    P6 --> P7["ERF1 Ethylene Response Factor Binds to GCC Box Promoter"]
    P7 --> P8["Ripening Related Genes PG PME Amylase PSY Chlorophyllase"]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Ethylene Action in Post-Harvest Ripening

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input[Input Stage]
        M1["Mature Green Fruit Harvested"]
    end
    subgraph Biosynthesis[Ethylene Biosynthesis Yang Cycle]
        B1["Methionine"]
        B2["SAM"]
        B3["ACC via ACS Rate Limiting"]
        B4["C2H4 via ACO"]
    end
    subgraph Perception[Signal Perception]
        R1["Receptor ETR1 ERS1"]
        R2["CTR1 OFF EIN2 ON"]
        R3["Transcription Factor EIN3 ERF1"]
    end
    subgraph Output[Biochemical Outputs]
        O1["Colour Change Chlorophyll Degraded Carotenoids Formed"]
        O2["Texture Softening Pectin Breakdown by PG and PME"]
        O3["Sugar Conversion Starch to Glucose and Fructose"]
        O4["Aroma Volatiles Esters Aldehydes Alcohols"]
        O5["Climacteric Respiratory Peak CO2 and O2 Exchange"]
    end
    subgraph Regulation[Regulation Strategies]
        RS1["1 MCP Blocks Receptors"]
        RS2["AVG Inhibits ACC Synthase"]
        RS3["Low O2 High CO2 Storage"]
        RS4["Cold Storage 13 C for Banana"]
    end
    M1 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> R1 --> R2 --> R3
    R3 --> O1
    R3 --> O2
    R3 --> O3
    R3 --> O4
    R3 --> O5
    RS1 -.Blocks.-> R1
    RS2 -.Blocks.-> B3
    RS3 -.Suppresses.-> B4
    RS4 -.Slows.-> O1
    RS4 -.Slows.-> O2
    RS4 -.Slows.-> O3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Architecture:** The diagram shows the **input (mature fruit) → biosynthesis (Yang Cycle) → perception (ER receptors) → outputs (colour, texture, sugar, aroma, respiration) → regulation (chemical, physical, thermal interventions)**. This is exactly the order KTU examiners expect in a 14-mark descriptive answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define climacteric and non-climacteric fruits with two examples each. Why is this classification important in post-harvest technology?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
* **Climacteric fruits** are those that exhibit a **sharp rise in respiration rate (the climacteric peak)** accompanied by an **autocatalytic burst of ethylene production (≥ 0.1 µL·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹)** at the onset of ripening. They can ripen after harvest. *Examples: Banana, Mango, Tomato.*
* **Non-climacteric fruits** show a **steady decline in respiration rate** and produce negligible ethylene; they must ripen on the plant. *Examples: Grape, Citrus, Strawberry.*
* **Importance:** Determines **harvesting strategy, storage conditions, ripening protocols, and shelf-life extension** techniques. Climacteric fruits benefit from controlled-atmosphere and 1-MCP treatment; non-climacteric fruits require careful temperature management to slow senescence. *(3 marks)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Name the three enzymes involved in the Yang Cycle of ethylene biosynthesis. Which one is the rate-limiting enzyme?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The three enzymes of the Yang Cycle are:
1. **Methionine Adenosyltransferase (MAT)** — converts Methionine to SAM.
2. **ACC Synthase (ACS)** — converts SAM to ACC.
3. **ACC Oxidase (ACO)** — oxidizes ACC to ethylene.

**ACC Synthase (ACS)** is the **rate-limiting enzyme** because it is encoded by a multigene family, has a very short mRNA half-life (~25 min), and is the primary regulatory point of the entire pathway. It is also the target of the commercial inhibitor **AVG (Aminoethoxyvinylglycine)**. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> * Students commonly lose marks by **confusing ACC Synthase and ACC Oxidase**. ACS = SAM → ACC. ACO = ACC → C₂H₄. Memorize the direction of arrows.
> * Forgetting to mention the **rate-limiting step (ACS)** and its **mRNA half-life (25 min)** costs easy 2 marks.
> * Skipping the **recycling of MTA back to methionine** (the "Cycle" in Yang Cycle) loses another 1–2 marks.
> * In signal transduction questions, students forget that **CTR1 is a NEGATIVE regulator** — it is active when ethylene is ABSENT, and gets inactivated upon ethylene binding. This is the most common conceptual error.
> * Always mention the **cofactors**: MAT needs ATP, ACS needs **PLP (pyridoxal phosphate)**, ACO needs **O₂ + Ascorbate + Fe²⁺**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — "Ethylene Biosynthesis & Climacteric Respiration"**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**Q.A. (a)** Describe the Yang Cycle of ethylene biosynthesis in plants. Highlight the rate-limiting step and the key enzymes involved. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** The Yang Cycle, elucidated by Shang Fa Yang (1984), is the primary biochemical pathway for ethylene biosynthesis in higher plants. The precursor is the amino acid **L-Methionine**.

2. **Step 1 — Activation (1 mark):** L-Methionine is activated by ATP via **Methionine Adenosyltransferase (MAT)** to form **S-Adenosyl-L-Methionine (SAM)**, the universal methyl donor.

3. **Step 2 — ACC Formation / Rate-Limiting Step (2 marks):** The enzyme **ACC Synthase (ACS)**, a **PLP (pyridoxal-5′-phosphate)-dependent** enzyme, converts SAM to **1-Aminocyclopropane-1-Carboxylic Acid (ACC)** with the release of **5′-Methylthioadenosine (MTA)**. This is the **rate-limiting step** because ACS has a short mRNA half-life (~25 min), is encoded by a multigene family, and is the target of the inhibitor AVG.

4. **Step 3 — Ethylene Formation (1.5 marks):** **ACC Oxidase (ACO)**, a **non-heme Fe²⁺, ascorbate-dependent dioxygenase**, oxidises ACC in the presence of O₂ to produce **C₂H₄ + CO₂ + HCN + H₂O**. The toxic HCN is immediately detoxified by **β-cyanoalanine synthase**.

5. **Step 4 — Recycling (1 mark):** MTA is recycled back to methionine via a 5-step pathway (MTA → MTR → MTR-1-P → KMB → Methionine), forming a closed loop.

6. **Net Stoichiometry (0.5 mark):**
$$\text{Methionine} + \text{ATP} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{HCN} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{AMP} + \text{PP}_i + \text{P}_i$$

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

**Q.A. (b)** What is the climacteric peak in fruit ripening? Differentiate between climacteric and non-climacteric fruits with examples. Explain the concept of autocatalytic ethylene production. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Climacteric Peak — Definition (1.5 marks):** The **climacteric peak** is a **sharp, transient rise in the respiration rate (CO₂ evolution / O₂ uptake)** of certain fruits at the onset of ripening, coinciding with a **massive burst of autocatalytic ethylene production (C₂H₄ ≥ 0.1 µL·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹)**. It is a hallmark of climacteric fruits and represents the irreversible transition from maturation to senescence.

2. **Climacteric vs Non-Climacteric (3 marks):**
| Parameter | Climacteric | Non-Climacteric |
|---|---|---|
| Respiration Pattern | Sharp peak (2–3× rise) | Gradual decline |
| Ethylene Production | Autocatalytic burst (≥ 0.1 µL/kg/h) | Negligible (< 0.1 µL/kg/h) |
| Response to Exogenous C₂H₄ | Yes, accelerates ripening | No response |
| Ripening After Harvest | Yes | No (must ripen on plant) |
| Examples | Banana, Mango, Tomato, Apple, Papaya, Pear | Grape, Citrus, Pineapple, Strawberry, Pomegranate |

3. **Autocatalytic Ethylene Production (2.5 marks):** In climacteric fruits, ethylene **stimulates its own synthesis** — a positive feedback loop. Initial small amounts of ethylene (System-1, basal) trigger the ripening programme, which upregulates **ACS and ACO gene expression**, producing more ethylene. This second wave (System-2, autocatalytic) drives the climacteric peak and irreversible ripening. Once the autocatalytic cycle is initiated, it cannot be reversed without intervention (e.g., 1-MCP treatment).

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — "Ethylene Signal Transduction & Practical Applications"**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the ethylene signal transduction pathway in plants with a suitable diagram. Mention the role of CTR1 and EIN2. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Ethylene Perception at ER (2 marks):** Ethylene is perceived by a family of **five receptors (ETR1, ETR2, ERS1, ERS2, EIN4)** localised on the **endoplasmic reticulum (ER) membrane**. These are **histidine-kinase-like receptors** that require a **copper cofactor (Cu⁺)** for ethylene binding, supplied by the **RAN1 copper transporter**.

2. **CTR1 — The Negative Regulator (2 marks):** In the **absence of ethylene**, the receptors activate **CTR1 (Constitutive Triple Response 1)**, a **Raf-like serine/threonine kinase**, which **phosphorylates EIN2 and keeps it inactive**. CTR1 is thus a **negative regulator** of the ethylene response.

3. **Ethylene Binding → CTR1 OFF → EIN2 Cleavage (2 marks):** When ethylene binds to the receptors, **CTR1 is inactivated**, allowing the positive regulator **EIN2 (Ethylene Insensitive 2)** to be **cleaved**. The **C-terminal fragment of EIN2 (EIN2-C)** translocates from the ER to the **nucleus**.

4. **Nuclear Response (1 mark):** In the nucleus, EIN3/EIL transcription factors are stabilised, which activate **Ethylene Response Factors (ERF1)**. ERF1 binds to the **GCC-box** promoter element, switching on **ripening-related genes** (PG, PME, amylase, PSY, etc.).

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

**Q.B. (b)** Discuss the commercial applications of ethylene in post-harvest technology. How is ethylene action inhibited using 1-MCP? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Commercial Applications of Ethylene (3.5 marks):**
* **Controlled Ripening of Climacteric Fruits:** Bananas, mangoes, and tomatoes are ripened on demand using **exogenous ethylene at 100–150 ppm for 24–48 h at 18–22 °C** in commercial ripening chambers. This standardises ripening, allows uniform colour and texture, and reduces market losses.
* **Degreening of Citrus:** Citrus fruits are treated with ethylene (1–5 ppm) to **degrade chlorophyll and reveal the carotenoid colour**, improving market appeal without affecting internal quality.
* **Flower and Ornamental Industry:** Ethylene is used to synchronise flowering in pineapples and to induce female flowers in cucurbits (cucumber, pumpkin).
* **Harvesting Aid:** Ethylene-releasing compounds like **Ethephon (2-chloroethylphosphonic acid)** are sprayed on crops to promote **fruit loosening and abscission**, facilitating mechanical harvesting (e.g., cotton, cherry, grape).
* **Induction of Latex Flow:** Used in rubber plantations to enhance latex yield.

2. **Inhibition by 1-Methylcyclopropene (1-MCP) (3.5 marks):**
* **1-MCP** is a **gaseous cyclic olefin** that binds **irreversibly and covalently** to the **copper cofactor** of ethylene receptors (ETR1, ETR2, ERS1, ERS2, EIN4).
* Because the binding is **permanent**, the receptor is permanently blocked, and the fruit becomes **blind to ethylene** for the rest of its storage life.
* **Commercial dose:** 0.5–1.0 µL/L for 12–24 hours at 0–2 °C.
* **Applications:** Extends shelf life of apples (SmartFresh™), bananas, kiwifruit, tomatoes, mangoes. It is **non-toxic, odourless, and used at very low concentrations**, making it the most successful ethylene antagonist in the post-harvest industry.

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Role of Ethylene in Ripening**

* **Ethylene (C₂H₄)** is a **gaseous plant hormone** with MW = **28.05 g/mol**; threshold for biological action is **0.01–0.1 ppm**.
* **Yang Cycle (1984)** = the Methionine pathway of ethylene biosynthesis.
* **Three key enzymes:** **MAT → ACS (rate-limiting) → ACO**.
* **Rate-limiting step:** SAM → ACC by **ACC Synthase (ACS)**, requiring **PLP cofactor**, mRNA half-life ~25 min.
* **ACC Oxidase (ACO)** requires **O₂ + Ascorbate + Fe²⁺**; produces toxic **HCN** which is detoxified by **β-cyanoalanine synthase**.
* **MTA recycling** is what makes it a *cycle* — five enzymatic steps back to methionine.
* **Climacteric fruits:** Banana, Mango, Tomato, Apple, Papaya, Pear, Guava, Avocado, Kiwi, Sapota, Passion fruit. Ethylene ≥ **0.1 µL·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹**.
* **Non-climacteric fruits:** Citrus, Grape, Pineapple, Strawberry, Cherry, Pomegranate, Watermelon, Capsicum, Cucumber. Ethylene < **0.1 µL·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹**.
* **Autocatalytic ethylene** = self-amplifying positive feedback loop; **System-1 (basal)** → **System-2 (autocatalytic)**.
* **Receptors:** **ETR1, ETR2, ERS1, ERS2, EIN4** on the **ER membrane**; need **Cu⁺ from RAN1**.
* **CTR1** = **negative regulator (Raf-like kinase)**, active in absence of ethylene.
* **EIN2** = positive regulator; cleaved upon ethylene perception, **C-terminus enters nucleus**.
* **EIN3 / EIL** → **ERF1** → **GCC-box** → ripening genes (PG, PME, amylase, PSY, chlorophyllase).
* **1-MCP** = irreversible ethylene receptor blocker; 0.5–1.0 µL/L; commercial product **SmartFresh™**.
* **AVG (Aminoethoxyvinylglycine)** = inhibits ACC Synthase; used in orchards to delay pre-harvest drop.
* **Commercial ripening dose** = **100–150 ppm ethylene, 24–48 h, 18–22 °C**.
* **Triple response** of dark-grown seedlings (shortened hypocotyl, thickened stem, horizontal growth) = classical **ethylene bioassay**.
* **Effects of ethylene:** ripening, degreening, abscission, epinasty, sex expression, stress response, aroma production, starch-to-sugar conversion, pectin breakdown.
* **Anti-ethylene technologies:** Low O₂, High CO₂, cold storage (13 °C for banana), 1-MCP, AVG, modified atmosphere packaging (MAP), controlled atmosphere storage (CA).
* **One ATP is consumed per molecule of ethylene produced** — write this stoichiometry if asked.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Tip:** Always structure your 14-mark answer as **(i) Introduction → (ii) Pathway/Concept → (iii) Key enzymes/Steps → (iv) Diagram/Table → (v) Significance/Applications → (vi) Conclusion**. This order is what KTU examiners look for and is the **fastest way to secure full marks**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Climacteric and Non-Climacteric Fruits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT614 - POST HARVEST HANDLING OF FOOD MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Climacteric and Non-Climacteric Fruits ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Climacteric Fruits:** Fruits that exhibit a **sharp rise (peak) in the rate of respiration** accompanied by a **burst of ethylene (C₂H₄) production** at the onset of ripening. This respiratory peak is known as the **Climacteric Peak**, and the fruit continues to ripen even after being detached from the parent plant. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Non-Climacteric Fruits:** Fruits that show **no pronounced respiratory peak** and produce **very low (trace) levels of ethylene** during ripening. These fruits **do not ripen further** once harvested at maturity; they must be picked fully ripe from the plant. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 1 – The "Self-Ripening Banana":** > Imagine you buy **green bananas** from the market. Even though they are cut off from the tree, they turn yellow, soft, and sweet on your kitchen counter. This is because they are **climacteric** — they carry their own "ripening fuel" (ethylene) inside them and ripen autonomously. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 2 – The "Grapes That Don't Ripen":** > Imagine a bunch of **green grapes**. If you pick them early, they will stay sour and hard forever, even after weeks of storage. They will never sweeten on their own. This is because grapes are **non-climacteric** — they must ripen on the vine while still attached to the mother plant. ### 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard Values - **Climacteric Peak Respiration Rate:** $\approx 2\times$ to $3\times$ the pre-climacteric (basal) respiration rate. - **Critical Ethylene Threshold for Climacteric Fruits:** $\geq 0.1$ to $1.0$ ppm (parts per million). - **Critical Ethylene Threshold for Non-Climacteric Fruits:** $\leq 0.01$ to $0.05$ ppm (autocatalytic response absent). - **Standard Storage Temperature (Climacteric, e.g., Apple):** $0$ to $4^{\circ}\text{C}$. - **Standard Storage Temperature (Non-Climacteric, e.g., Citrus):** $5$ to $10^{\circ}\text{C}$. ### 1.4 Classification Snapshot | Category | Respiration Pattern | Ethylene Role | Ripening After Harvest | |---|---|---|---| | **Climacteric** | Sharp peak (rise and fall) | **Autocatalytic** driver | **Yes** (continues) | | **Non-Climacteric** | Gradual decline, no peak | Minimal / regulatory | **No** (must ripen on plant) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Physiological Basis of Climacteric Behavior The ripening process in climacteric fruits follows a well-defined biochemical cascade: - **Step 1 — Pre-Climacteric Phase (Quiescent):** The mature-green fruit has a low, steady respiration rate ($R_0$) and minimal ethylene output. Starch, organic acids, and chlorophyll dominate the tissue composition. - **Step 2 — Trigger Event:** A physiological "switch" (autocatalytic ethylene biosynthesis via **ACC synthase** converting $S\text{-adenosyl methionine (SAM)} \rightarrow \text{1-aminocyclopropane-1-carboxylic acid (ACC)}$, and **ACC oxidase** converting $ACC \rightarrow \text{ethylene}$) is activated. - **Step 3 — Climacteric Peak:** Respiration rate shoots up to $R_{peak}$ ($R_{peak} \approx 2R_0$ to $3R_0$). Ethylene concentration may reach **10 to 100 ppm** internally. - **Step 4 — Post-Climacteric / Senescence:** Respiration falls back, ethylene drops, tissues soften, cell walls degrade (via **polygalacturonase**, **pectin methylesterase**), pigments change (chlorophyll breakdown, carotenoid/anthocyanin synthesis). - **Step 5 — Ripening Completion:** Sugar accumulation peaks, acidity declines, aroma volatiles are emitted, and the fruit becomes edible. ### 2.2 The Physiological Basis of Non-Climacteric Behavior - Non-climacteric fruits **lack the autocatalytic ethylene feedback loop**. - Respiration follows a **monotonically declining curve** post-harvest — no peak. - Ripening is a **gradual, ethylene-independent** process tied to the developmental program of the plant. - They retain **harvest-time quality** — no further sugar conversion or softening occurs after picking. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The formulas below describe the respiration kinetics of harvested fruits and are **high-yield** for numerical/analytical KTU questions. | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | $R = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{m_{fruit} \cdot t}$ | Respiration rate ($R$), $\text{CO}_2$ mass ($m_{CO_2}$), fruit mass ($m_{fruit}$), time ($t$) | $\text{mg CO}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$ | Quantifies postharvest metabolic activity | | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\dfrac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Temperature coefficient — ratio of respiration rates at two temperatures differing by $10^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Dimensionless | Predicts shelf-life loss with temperature rise | | $R_{peak} = k \cdot R_0$ | Climacteric peak ($R_{peak}$), basal rate ($R_0$), constant $k$ where $k \approx 2$ to $3$ | $\text{mg CO}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$ | Quantifies climacteric rise magnitude | | $[C_2H_4]_{critical} \approx 0.1 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ ppm}$ | Threshold for autocatalytic ethylene response | ppm | Storage chamber design | | $t_{shelf} \propto \dfrac{1}{R \cdot T}$ | Inverse proportionality of shelf life to respiration and temperature | hours / days | Cold-chain design | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > - **Cold Storage Design:** Engineers size refrigeration loads based on respiration heat ($Q_{resp}$) of stored fruits. Climacteric fruits need **rapid pre-cooling** to delay the peak. > - **Controlled Atmosphere (CA) Storage:** Lowering $\text{O}_2$ to $2$ to $5\%$ and raising $\text{CO}_2$ to $3$ to $5\%$ suppresses the climacteric rise in apples and bananas. > - **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):** Ethylene scrubbers (e.g., potassium permanganate) are used in climacteric packaging. > - **Transport Logistics:** Non-climacteric fruits (e.g., strawberry, grapes) require faster farm-to-shelf transit because they cannot recover quality post-harvest. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Respiration Rate from Experimental Data **Problem Setup:** A $5\text{ kg}$ batch of harvested mangoes (climacteric) liberates $250\text{ mg}$ of $\text{CO}_2$ over $4\text{ hours}$ at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$. Compute the respiration rate $R$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Given: } & m_{CO_2} = 250 \text{ mg}, \quad m_{fruit} = 5 \text{ kg}, \quad t = 4 \text{ h} \\[6pt] \text{Step 1: Write the respiration rate formula.} & \quad R = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{m_{fruit} \cdot t} \\[6pt] \text{Step 2: Substitute numerical values.} & \quad R = \dfrac{250 \text{ mg}}{5 \text{ kg} \times 4 \text{ h}} \\[6pt] \text{Step 3: Simplify the denominator.} & \quad R = \dfrac{250 \text{ mg}}{20 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{h}} \\[6pt] \text{Step 4: Final result.} & \quad R = 12.5 \text{ mg CO}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** The mangoes are respiring at $R = 12.5 \text{ mg CO}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$, indicating active metabolic (likely climacteric) activity. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Temperature Coefficient ($Q_{10}$) Calculation **Problem Setup:** A fruit's respiration rate is $8 \text{ mg CO}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$ at $10^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $20 \text{ mg CO}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$ at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$. Compute the $Q_{10}$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Given: } & R_1 = 8 \text{ at } T_1 = 10^{\circ}\text{C}, \quad R_2 = 20 \text{ at } T_2 = 20^{\circ}\text{C} \\[6pt] \text{Step 1: Use the formula.} & \quad Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\dfrac{10}{T_2 - T_1}} \\[6pt] \text{Step 2: Compute the temperature difference and exponent.} & \quad T_2 - T_1 = 10^{\circ}\text{C}, \quad \dfrac{10}{10} = 1 \\[6pt] \text{Step 3: Compute the rate ratio.} & \quad \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{20}{8} = 2.5 \\[6pt] \text{Step 4: Final result.} & \quad Q_{10} = (2.5)^1 = 2.5 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** A $Q_{10}$ of **2.5** means that for every $10^{\circ}\text{C}$ rise in storage temperature, the respiration rate (and hence deterioration) increases by **2.5 times**. This validates cold-chain necessity for climacteric fruits. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Climacteric Peak Magnitude **Problem Setup:** A tomato batch has a basal respiration rate $R_0 = 10 \text{ mg CO}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$ at the pre-climacteric stage. If the climacteric constant $k = 3$, determine $R_{peak}$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Write the relationship.} & \quad R_{peak} = k \cdot R_0 \\[6pt] \text{Step 2: Substitute.} & \quad R_{peak} = 3 \times 10 \\[6pt] \text{Step 3: Final result.} & \quad R_{peak} = 30 \text{ mg CO}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** The climacteric peak respiration rate is $30 \text{ mg CO}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$, a **three-fold** rise over the basal rate. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Climacteric vs. Non-Climacteric Classification | Parameter | Climacteric Fruits | Non-Climacteric Fruits | |---|---|---| | **Respiration Pattern** | Sharp rise followed by decline (peak) | Steady gradual decline, **no peak** | | **Ethylene Production** | High burst (**autocatalytic**) | Very low / negligible | | **Response to External Ethylene** | Accelerated ripening | Minimal / nil | | **Ripening After Detachment** | **Yes — continues** | **No — must ripen on plant** | | **Examples** | Apple, Banana, Mango, Tomato, Papaya, Pear, Kiwi, Avocado | Grape, Citrus (orange/lemon), Pineapple, Strawberry, Cherry, Pomegranate, Watermelon | | **Storage Strategy** | Low temp + CA/MAP + ethylene scrubbers | Low temp + high humidity + rapid transit | | **Sensitivity to Chilling** | Variable (banana chilling injury at $<13^{\circ}\text{C}$) | Variable (pineapple chilling injury at $<10^{\circ}\text{C}$) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Respiration Curves — Climacteric vs. Non-Climacteric The following block diagram represents the **functional behavior** of respiration rate over time after harvest, which is the hallmark difference between the two classes. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Time After Harvest t = 0]) --> preClimA[Pre Climacteric Phase
Low steady respiration R0] preClimA --> triggerA{Internal Ethylene Trigger
ACC Synthase ACC Oxidase} triggerA -->|Yes| peakA[Climacteric Peak
Rpeak approx 2 to 3 times R0] peakA --> postClimA[Post Climacteric Senescence
Respiration declines] postClimA --> ripeA[Fully Ripe Edible Fruit] startB([Time After Harvest t = 0]) --> preClimB[Initial Respiration R0] preClimB --> gradualB[Gradual Decline No Peak
No autocatalytic ethylene] gradualB --> shelfB[Quality Stays at Harvest Level
No further ripening] classDef climFill fill:#FFD8A8,stroke:#D2691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef nonClimFill fill:#A8D8FF,stroke:#1E5AA8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class preClimA,triggerA,peakA,postClimA,ripeA climFill class preClimB,gradualB,shelfB nonClimFill ``` ### 4.2 Ethylene Biosynthesis Pathway (Climacteric Trigger Mechanism) ```mermaid flowchart LR methionine[Methionine
Amino Acid Precursor] --> sam[S Adenosyl Methionine SAM] sam --> acc[1 Aminocyclopropane
1 Carboxylic Acid ACC] sam -.Catalyzed by.-> accSyn[ACC Synthase Enzyme] acc --> ethylene[Ethylene C2H4 Gas
Ripening Hormone] acc -.Catalyzed by.-> accOx[ACC Oxidase Enzyme] ethylene --> feedback{Autocatalytic Positive Feedback
in Climacteric Fruits} feedback -->|Yes amplify| acc feedback -->|Absent minimal| nonClim[Non Climacteric Fruits
No feedback loop] ``` ### 4.3 Post-Harvest Decision Flow for Fruits ```mermaid flowchart TD harvest[Harvested Fruit] --> classify{Is the fruit
Climacteric or
Non Climacteric} classify -->|Climacteric| climPath[Apply Rapid Pre Cooling
Store at 0 to 4 degree C
Use Ethylene Scrubbers
CA or MAP environment] classify -->|Non Climacteric| nonClimPath[Maintain at Optimum Low Temp
Avoid external ethylene exposure
Ensure rapid farm to shelf transit
Do not expect further ripening] climPath --> monitor[Monitor Respiration Rate
and Ethylene Levels] nonClimPath --> monitor2[Monitor Visual Quality
and Microbial Spoilage] monitor --> market[Distribute to Market at Peak Quality] monitor2 --> market ``` ### 4.4 Engineering Architecture: Controlled Atmosphere Storage ```mermaid flowchart LR fruitLoad[Fruit Chamber
e.g. Apple Storage Room] --> tempCtrl[Temperature Control Unit
0 to 4 degree C] fruitLoad --> gasCtrl[Gas Composition Controller] gasCtrl --> o2Ctrl[O2 Reducer
Maintains 2 to 5 percent] gasCtrl --> co2Ctrl[CO2 Injector
Maintains 3 to 5 percent] fruitLoad --> ethScrub[Ethylene Scrubber
KMnO4 based] fruitLoad --> humidCtrl[Humidity Controller
90 to 95 percent RH] tempCtrl --> quality[Extended Shelf Life
and Delayed Climacteric Peak] gasCtrl --> quality ethScrub --> quality humidCtrl --> quality ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, 3 Marks]** > **Q:** Define climacteric fruits with two examples. > > **Model Answer:** > Climacteric fruits are those that exhibit a **sharp rise in respiration rate** (called the climacteric peak) accompanied by a **burst of ethylene production** at the onset of ripening. They continue to ripen even after being detached from the parent plant. **Examples:** Banana and Mango. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for respiration peak concept, 1 for examples)* > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, 3 Marks]** > **Q:** Why do non-climacteric fruits fail to ripen after harvest? > > **Model Answer:** > Non-climacteric fruits **lack the autocatalytic ethylene biosynthesis feedback loop** and do not show a respiratory climacteric peak. Their ripening is entirely dependent on **sugar and acid accumulation from the parent plant via the phloem**. Once detached, the supply of photosynthates is cut off, and since internal ethylene cannot trigger further ripening, the fruit remains at its harvest-time quality. **Example:** Grape. *(3 marks: 1 for autocatalytic absence, 1 for phloem dependence, 1 for example)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Classify fruits into climacteric and non-climacteric categories. Explain the respiration pattern and ethylene behavior of each with suitable graphical representation. **Model Answer:** Fruits are classified into two categories based on their **postharvest respiratory behavior** and **ethylene biosynthesis pattern**. **Climacteric Fruits:** These exhibit a characteristic **climacteric rise** in respiration rate (often $2\times$ to $3\times$ the basal rate) coupled with a **burst of endogenous ethylene production**. The ethylene produced is **autocatalytic**, meaning it triggers its own further synthesis via ACC synthase and ACC oxidase enzymes. Once the climacteric peak is reached, the fruit undergoes ripening — starch converts to sugar, chlorophyll degrades, carotenoids/anthocyanins form, cell wall softens, and aroma volatiles develop. Ripening **continues after harvest**. *Examples: Apple, Banana, Mango, Tomato, Papaya, Pear, Kiwi, Avocado.* **Non-Climacteric Fruits:** These do **not show any respiratory peak**. Their respiration rate **declines gradually and monotonically** after harvest. Ethylene production remains at **trace levels** with **no autocatalytic feedback**. Ripening-related changes (color, aroma, sugar) occur only while the fruit is attached to the plant. Once picked, the fruit **does not undergo any further quality improvement**. *Examples: Grape, Citrus, Pineapple, Strawberry, Cherry, Pomegranate, Watermelon.* **Graphical Representation:** A climacteric fruit's respiration curve resembles a **bell-shaped / peak-shaped** profile with a sharp rise and fall, whereas a non-climacteric fruit's curve is a **smooth declining curve** with no peak. *(7 marks: 2 for classification logic, 2 for respiration and ethylene behavior, 2 for examples, 1 for graphical comparison)* **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the engineering strategies used in the post-harvest handling of climacteric fruits to extend their shelf life. **Model Answer:** The following engineering strategies are employed to delay the climacteric peak and extend shelf life of climacteric fruits: 1. **Rapid Pre-Cooling:** Immediately after harvest, fruits are brought down to their **optimum storage temperature** (e.g., $0$ to $4^{\circ}\text{C}$ for apples, $13$ to $15^{\circ}\text{C}$ for bananas) to remove field heat and slow down respiration. 2. **Controlled Atmosphere (CA) Storage:** The $\text{O}_2$ level is reduced to **2 to 5%** and $\text{CO}_2$ raised to **3 to 5%**. Low $\text{O}_2$ suppresses ACC oxidase activity, reducing ethylene biosynthesis and delaying the climacteric peak. 3. **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):** Polymeric films (e.g., LDPE, HDPE) are used to create a passive modified atmosphere around the fruit inside the package, balancing $\text{O}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$, and humidity. 4. **Ethylene Scrubbers:** Potassium permanganate ($\text{KMnO}_4$) sachets or catalytic oxidizers are placed in storage chambers to **remove ethylene** from the environment. 5. **1-MCP Treatment (1-Methylcyclopropene):** A synthetic ethylene receptor blocker that **competitively inhibits ethylene binding**, delaying ripening by 5 to 14 days in apples and tomatoes. 6. **Edible Coatings:** Wax, chitosan, or alginate coatings reduce transpiration, gas exchange, and microbial load. 7. **Humidity Control:** Maintaining **85 to 95% RH** prevents shriveling and weight loss. Collectively, these interventions shift the climacteric peak to a later time, prolonging the marketable window. *(7 marks: 1 each for the 6 strategies + 1 for integrated conclusion)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the ethylene biosynthesis pathway in climacteric fruits and highlight the role of ACC synthase and ACC oxidase. **Model Answer:** Ethylene biosynthesis in climacteric fruits follows the **Yang Cycle** pathway: **Step 1:** The amino acid **Methionine** is converted to **S-Adenosyl Methionine (SAM)** by the enzyme SAM synthetase. **Step 2:** SAM is converted to **1-Aminocyclopropane-1-Carboxylic Acid (ACC)** by the rate-limiting enzyme **ACC Synthase**. **Step 3:** ACC is oxidized to **Ethylene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_4$)** by **ACC Oxidase**, with $\text{CO}_2$ and HCN as byproducts. **Step 4:** The ethylene produced **binds to ethylene receptors** (ETR1, EIN4) on the cell membrane and triggers a signal transduction cascade (EIN2 → EIN3 → ERF transcription factors) that activates ripening-related genes. **Step 5:** In **climacteric fruits**, ethylene **positively feeds back** to upregulate ACC synthase and ACC oxidase, producing a **self-amplifying autocatalytic burst** of ethylene that drives the climacteric rise. **Role of ACC Synthase:** It is the **rate-limiting enzyme** and primary regulatory control point. Its gene expression is dramatically upregulated at the onset of ripening. **Role of ACC Oxidase:** It catalyzes the **final step** of ethylene production. It is also **ethylene-inducible** in climacteric fruits (autocatalytic), making it a key target for shelf-life extension strategies (e.g., 1-MCP blocks the receptor, suppressing this feedback). The diagram should show: Methionine → SAM → ACC → Ethylene, with ACC Synthase and ACC Oxidase annotated as the two key regulatory enzymes. *(7 marks: 2 for pathway steps, 2 for ACC synthase role, 2 for ACC oxidase role, 1 for autocatalytic feedback concept)* **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast climacteric and non-climacteric fruits across at least six parameters and provide their respective examples. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Climacteric Fruits | Non-Climacteric Fruits | |---|---|---| | **Respiration Pattern** | Sharp rise and fall (climacteric peak) | Gradual decline, no peak | | **Ethylene Production** | High burst, autocatalytic | Trace, no autocatalysis | | **Ripening After Harvest** | Continues | Does not occur | | **Response to External Ethylene** | Accelerated ripening | Minimal / nil | | **Sugar Accumulation** | Starch → sugar conversion post-harvest | Sugar accumulation only on plant | | **Storage Strategy** | CA, MAP, ethylene scrubbers, 1-MCP | Low temperature, high humidity, fast transit | | **Examples** | Apple, Banana, Mango, Tomato, Pear, Kiwi, Avocado, Papaya | Grape, Orange, Lemon, Pineapple, Strawberry, Cherry, Pomegranate, Watermelon | *(7 marks: 1 mark each for the 6 parameters + 1 mark for example lists)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > - **Do NOT** list "Lemon" as a climacteric fruit. Lemons are **non-climacteric** citrus fruits. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. > - **Do NOT** confuse **climacteric peak** with **respiratory quotient (RQ)**. They are different concepts. > - **Do NOT** claim all fruits produce ethylene. Non-climacteric fruits produce **negligible** amounts; they are **not zero** in absolute terms but lack the burst. > - In numerical questions, **always include units** ($\text{mg CO}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$). Marks are deducted for unit omission. > - In pathway questions, **ACC synthase is rate-limiting** — this is the most-tested keyword. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Climacteric vs. Non-Climacteric Fruits:** - ✅ Climacteric fruits show a **respiratory peak + ethylene burst**; non-climacteric fruits show **neither**. - ✅ Climacteric ripening is **autocatalytic** (self-amplifying); non-climacteric ripening is **non-autocatalytic**. - ✅ Climacteric fruits **continue to ripen** after harvest; non-climacteric fruits **do not**. - ✅ The two key regulatory enzymes are **ACC synthase (rate-limiting)** and **ACC oxidase**. - ✅ Standard respiration unit: $\text{mg CO}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$. - ✅ $Q_{10}$ of fruits is typically **2 to 3** — every $10^{\circ}\text{C}$ rise accelerates deterioration by that factor. - ✅ **Climacteric examples to memorize:** Apple, Banana, Mango, Tomato, Pear, Kiwi, Avocado, Papaya. - ✅ **Non-climacteric examples to memorize:** Grape, Orange, Lemon, Pineapple, Strawberry, Cherry, Pomegranate, Watermelon. - ✅ Engineering interventions for climacteric shelf-life extension: **Pre-cooling, CA, MAP, ethylene scrubbers, 1-MCP, edible coatings, humidity control**. - ✅ **1-MCP** blocks ethylene receptors and is widely used commercially for apples and tomatoes. - ✅ Non-climacteric fruits require **rapid transit** — there is no recovery of quality post-harvest. - ✅ Climacteric peak respiration rate is typically **$2\times$ to $3\times$ the basal (pre-climacteric)** respiration rate.

topics.title: Ethylene Biosynthesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT614 - POST HARVEST HANDLING OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ethylene Biosynthesis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ethylene biosynthesis** is the tightly regulated biochemical pathway by which plant cells synthesize the gaseous phytohormone **ethylene (C₂H₄)** — the simplest unsaturated olefin — from the amino acid **L-methionine**. In the context of post-harvest technology, this pathway is of paramount importance because ethylene acts as the master regulator of **climacteric fruit ripening**, senescence, abscission, and stress responses in harvested horticultural commodities.

The universally accepted pathway, established through the pioneering work of **Yang and Hoffman (1984)**, proceeds through two committed enzymatic steps:

$$L\text{-Methionine} \xrightarrow{\text{SAM-Synthetase}} \text{SAM} \xrightarrow{\text{ACC-Synthase (ACS)}} \text{ACC} \xrightarrow{\text{ACC-Oxidase (ACO)}} \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{HCN}$$

where **SAM** = *S*-adenosyl-L-methionine and **ACC** = 1-aminocyclopropane-1-carboxylic acid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The ethylene biosynthesis pathway is a high-weightage topic in Module 2. Students must master the Yang Cycle, the two key enzymes (ACS and ACO), the role of methionine recycling, and the physiological triggers (climacteric vs. non-climacteric fruits).

> [!NOTE]
> **Ethylene (C₂H₄)** is a colourless, flammable gas with a faint sweet/musky odour. It is biologically active at concentrations as low as **0.01–1.0 ppm (parts per million)** — making it one of the most potent plant hormones known.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ripening Domino Chain"

Imagine a row of dominoes standing inside a fruit. The **first domino** is methionine. A small trigger (like a tap on the fruit, an injury, or natural maturation) flips it, which knocks over the second domino (SAM). This in turn knocks over the third domino (ACC, made by ACC synthase). Finally, the fourth domino (ACC oxidase) releases ethylene gas — the "knocked-over coin" that tumbles into the surrounding air and triggers ripening in **neighbouring fruits too**.

The beautiful part? Once ACC is converted, the leftover pieces of methionine are **recycled** (Yang cycle) to start the chain again — like re-setting the dominoes automatically after each fall. This is why a single ripe banana can ripen a whole bunch, and why cold storage / controlled atmosphere works by **slowing down the domino chain**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Range | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| Molecular formula of ethylene | **C₂H₄** | Simplest alkene |
| Molecular weight | **28.05 g/mol** | Lightest plant hormone |
| Boiling point | **−103.7 °C** | Always gaseous at room/storage temperature |
| Biologically active threshold | **0.01–1.0 ppm (10 ppb–1000 ppb)** | Extremely potent |
| Climacteric peak ethylene | **100–300 ppm** (internal) | Apples, bananas, tomatoes |
| Non-climacteric basal ethylene | **< 0.5 ppm** | Citrus, grapes, strawberries |
| Optimal ACO activity pH | **~7.5–8.0** | Slightly alkaline cytosolic |
| Cofactor for ACC oxidase | **Ascorbate + O₂ + Fe²⁺** | Vitamin C dependent |
| Km of ACC synthase (SAM) | **~13–20 µM** | High affinity |

---

### 1.4 Why Ethylene Biosynthesis Matters in Post-Harvest Handling

1. **Quality Control**: Knowing whether a fruit is climacteric or non-climacteric dictates the entire storage strategy.
2. **Cold Chain Management**: Low temperatures suppress ACS/ACO transcription and translation.
3. **Controlled Atmosphere (CA) Storage**: Elevated CO₂ and reduced O₂ inhibit ACC oxidase → slow ripening.
4. **1-MCP Treatment**: A competitive inhibitor of ethylene receptors that blocks perception (downstream of biosynthesis).
5. **Packaging Design**: Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) exploits the biosynthesis pathway's oxygen dependency.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Methionine / Yang Cycle — Step-by-Step Logic

The complete ethylene biosynthesis pathway is best understood as a **closed loop** that conserves the sulfur-containing methyl group of methionine. The cycle was elucidated by **Shang Fa Yang and Neal E. Hoffman (1984)** — therefore called the **Yang Cycle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember the cycle order**: **"M → SAM → ACC → C₂H₄, and MTA → MTR → KMB → M"** — "Many Students ACCept Cheerful Minds That Make Things Right Kindly Bring More".

#### Step 1 — Activation of Methionine
- Enzyme: **SAM synthetase** (also called *methionine adenosyltransferase*, MAT)
- Cofactor: **ATP + Mg²⁺**
- Reaction: Methionine condenses with ATP to form **S-adenosyl-L-methionine (SAM)**
- Why: SAM is a "high-energy" methyl donor; its activation energy barrier allows the cell to control flux.

#### Step 2 — Conversion of SAM to ACC (THE RATE-LIMITING STEP)
- Enzyme: **ACC synthase (ACS)** — EC 4.4.1.14
- Cofactor: **Pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP)** — vitamin B6 derivative
- Reaction: SAM → **ACC + 5'-methylthioadenosine (MTA)**
- Why: ACS is the **primary regulatory point**; its gene (*ACS*) is induced by:
  - Wounding / mechanical stress
  - Auxin (IAA)
  - Drought, chilling, pathogen attack
  - Fruit maturation
  - Exogenous ethylene (autocatalysis in climacteric fruits)

#### Step 3 — Oxidation of ACC to Ethylene
- Enzyme: **ACC oxidase (ACO)** — EC 1.14.17.4 (originally called "ethylene-forming enzyme, EFE")
- Cofactors: **Ascorbate (vitamin C)**, **O₂**, **Fe²⁺**, **bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻)**
- Reaction: ACC + O₂ + ascorbate → **C₂H₄ + CO₂ + HCN + dehydroascorbate + H₂O**
- Why: ACO is **membrane-associated** and **oxygen-dependent**; this is why low-O₂ storage blocks ripening.

#### Step 4 — Recycling of MTA back to Methionine
- MTA → **MTR (5-methylthioribose)** via MTA-nucleosidase
- MTR → **KMB (2-keto-4-methylthiobutyrate)** via MTR-kinase and dehydratase
- KMB → **Methionine** via transaminase
- Why: Recovers the **sulfur atom** and prevents depletion of the methionine pool — vital because plants cannot store large methionine reserves.

---

### 2.2 The Autocatalytic vs. Autoinhibitory Dichotomy

| Behaviour | Climacteric Fruits | Non-Climacteric Fruits |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Ethylene pattern | **Climacteric peak** (100–300 ppm) | **Constant low basal** (< 0.5 ppm) |
| Response to exogenous C₂H₄ | **Autocatalytic** — induces more ethylene | **Autoinhibitory** — suppresses further production |
| ACS regulation | System I → System II transition | Only System I operates |
| Examples | Banana, apple, tomato, mango, avocado, papaya | Citrus, grape, strawberry, cherry, pineapple |
| Post-harvest strategy | 1-MCP, cold storage, CA, ethylene scrubbing | Just cold storage + RH control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System I** = basal, low-rate ethylene production in pre-climacteric and non-climacteric tissues. **System II** = autocatalytic, high-rate production characteristic of climacteric fruits. The transition from System I → System II is the biochemical "switch" that triggers ripening.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Relation | Symbolic Form | Units | Application |
|--------------------|---------------|-------|-------------|
| Michaelis–Menten for ACS | $v = \dfrac{V_{max}[SAM]}{K_m + [SAM]}$ | mol·s⁻¹·mg⁻¹ | Quantifies SAM → ACC flux |
| Michaelis–Menten for ACO | $v = \dfrac{V_{max}[ACC][O_2]}{(K_m^{ACC}+[ACC])(K_m^{O_2}+[O_2])}$ | mol·s⁻¹·mg⁻¹ | O₂-dependent step |
| Activation energy (Clapeyron form) | $k = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | s⁻¹ | Effect of temperature on enzyme |
| Q₁₀ rule | $R = \left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{\!\!z}$ where $z = \dfrac{10}{\log_{10}Q_{10}}$ | dimensionless | Approx. 2–3× rate per 10 °C |
| Diffusion of ethylene through peel | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = \dfrac{D \cdot A}{\Delta x}\,(C_{int}-C_{ext})$ | mol·s⁻¹ | Mass transfer in storage rooms |
| ACC content → ethylene potential | $E_{pot} = n_{ACC} \times \dfrac{Y_{C_2H_4}}{n_{ACC}}$ (1:1 stoichiometry) | mol C₂H₄ | Predicts ripening burst |
| Hill equation for receptor binding | $\theta = \dfrac{[L]^n}{K_d^n+[L]^n}$ | dimensionless | 1-MCP competition with C₂H₄ |
| Avogadro's number for ppm conversion | $1\ \text{ppm} = \dfrac{P}{RT}\times 10^{-6}$ M ≈ **40 nmol/mol** at STP | mol/mol | Gas phase kinetics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical stoichiometric fact for KTU exams:** 1 mole of ACC yields **exactly 1 mole of ethylene**. This is why measuring tissue ACC content via HPLC directly predicts the maximum ethylene a fruit can produce.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Post-Harvest Engineering

- **1-MCP (SmartFresh™)**: A cyclic olefin that binds ethylene receptors (ETR1) irreversibly → blocks ripening despite active biosynthesis.
- **Controlled Atmosphere (CA)**: Reducing O₂ below **2–3%** and elevating CO₂ to **2–5%** suppresses ACO (Ki for O₂ ≈ 0.2%).
- **Ethylene Scrubbers**: Potassium permanganate (KMnO₄) oxidizes ambient C₂H₄ → CO₂ + MnO₂.
- **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)**: Selectively permeable films (e.g., LDPE) achieve internal atmospheres of 3–8% O₂ and 3–10% CO₂.
- **Edible Coatings**: Chitosan-based coatings create a diffusion barrier reducing O₂ reaching ACO.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Michaelis–Menten Equation for ACS (Rate-Limiting Step)

The **steady-state assumption** for the enzyme–substrate complex yields the standard Michaelis–Menten form. We will derive it rigorously because KTU examiners often test this for the rate-limiting enzyme.

Let $E$ = free enzyme, $S$ = SAM, $ES$ = enzyme-substrate complex, $P$ = ACC, $E'$ = ACC synthase.

**Step 1: Equilibrium and catalytic constants**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E + S &\;\underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; ES \;\xrightarrow{k_{cat}}\; E + P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Define the Michaelis constant**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_m &= \dfrac{k_{-1} + k_{cat}}{k_{1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the steady-state assumption** $\dfrac{d[ES]}{dt}=0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{1}[E][S] &= (k_{-1}+k_{cat})[ES]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Express total enzyme** $[E]_T = [E] + [ES]$

$$
\begin{aligned}
[E] &= [E]_T - [ES]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute and solve for [ES]**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{1}([E]_T - [ES])[S] &= (k_{-1}+k_{cat})[ES] \\[4pt]
k_{1}[E]_T[S] - k_{1}[ES][S] &= (k_{-1}+k_{cat})[ES] \\[4pt]
k_{1}[E]_T[S] &= [ES]\bigl(k_{-1}+k_{cat}+k_{1}[S]\bigr) \\[4pt]
[ES] &= \dfrac{k_{1}[E]_T[S]}{k_{-1}+k_{cat}+k_{1}[S]} = \dfrac{[E]_T[S]}{K_m + [S]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Initial velocity** $v_0 = k_{cat}[ES]$

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_0 &= \dfrac{k_{cat}[E]_T[S]}{K_m + [S]} \;\;\equiv\;\; \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $V_{max}=k_{cat}[E]_T$. **This is the Michaelis–Menten equation** used to model ACC synthase kinetics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Temperature Dependence (Arrhenius Form)

Storage temperature drastically alters ACS/ACO turnover. The Arrhenius equation gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln k &= \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For two storage temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) &= \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example** (commonly asked in KTU):
Given $E_a = 50$ kJ/mol for ACO in tomato, calculate the rate ratio when temperature drops from **25 °C (298 K)** to **5 °C (278 K)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_{278}}{k_{298}}\right) &= \dfrac{50{,}000}{8.314}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{298} - \dfrac{1}{278}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 6014.4 \times (0.003356 - 0.003597) \\[4pt]
&= 6014.4 \times (-2.41\times 10^{-4}) \\[4pt]
&= -1.450
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{k_{278}}{k_{298}} &= e^{-1.450} \approx 0.235
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Cooling tomatoes from 25 °C to 5 °C reduces ACO activity to **~23.5%** of the original — confirming why cold storage is so effective at slowing ripening.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Regulatory Hierarchy of Ethylene Biosynthesis

| Regulatory Level | Mechanism | Key Molecule | Time-Scale |
|------------------|-----------|--------------|------------|
| **Transcriptional** | *ACS* gene induction | Auxin, wounding, ethylene (positive feedback) | Hours |
| **Post-transcriptional** | mRNA stability | 5' UTR elements, miRNAs (e.g., miR-159) | Minutes–Hours |
| **Translational** | Ribosome loading on *ACS* mRNA | eIF4E availability | Minutes |
| **Post-translational (ACS stability)** | Ubiquitin–proteasome degradation | E3 ligases (ETP1/ETP2 in *Arabidopsis*) | Minutes |
| **Allosteric (ACO)** | O₂/CO₂ binding at active site | O₂, ascorbate regeneration | Instantaneous |
| **Compartmentalization** | ACO anchored to plasma membrane/ER; ACS in cytosol | Vesicle trafficking | Minutes |
| **Hormonal cross-talk** | Auxin ↑ ACS; ABA ↑ ACS; BR ↑ ACO; JA modulates | IAA, ABA, BR, JA | Hours |
| **Oxidative** | Ascorbate depletion inactivates ACO | ROS, AA pool | Seconds–Minutes |

---

### 3.4 Stoichiometric Mass-Balance Derivation of the Yang Cycle

Let us verify the closed-loop nature of the Yang cycle by tracing atoms through one complete turn.

**Step 1 — SAM formation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Methionine} + \text{ATP} \longrightarrow \text{SAM} + \text{PP}_i + \text{P}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — SAM to ACC + MTA:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAM} \longrightarrow \text{ACC} + \text{MTA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — ACC to ethylene:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ACC} + \text{O}_2 + \text{Asc} \longrightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{HCN} + \text{dAsc} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — MTA recycling back to methionine** (overall summary equation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTA} \longrightarrow \text{MTR} \longrightarrow \text{KMB} \longrightarrow \text{Methionine}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Overall net for one ethylene molecule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Methionine} + \text{ATP} + \text{O}_2 + \text{Asc} \longrightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{HCN} + \text{dAsc} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{PP}_i + \text{P}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Notice that **methionine is regenerated**, so the only stoichiometric consumption per C₂H₄ is **1 ATP** and the reducing equivalents (ascorbate). The HCN byproduct is detoxified by **β-cyanoalanine synthase** to β-cyanoalanine, preventing cyanide poisoning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HCN Detoxification Equation** (high-yield for KTU):
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{HCN} + \text{Cysteine} \xrightarrow{\beta\text{-cyanoalanine synthase}} \text{β-cyanoalanine} + \text{H}_2\text{S}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> Then β-cyanoalanine is hydrolysed by β-cyanoalanine hydratase to **asparagine + NH₃**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Yang Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Methionine Pool] -->|SAM Synthetase + ATP + Mg2+| B[SAM Activated]
```
