# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between enzymatic and non-enzymatic browning in stored food materials."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Enzymatic Browning | Non-Enzymatic Browning |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Catalyst** | Requires **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)** enzyme | No enzyme; chemical reaction |
| **Substrate** | Phenolic compounds (catechol, tyrosine) | Reducing sugars + amino acids (Maillard) or sugars alone (caramelization) |
| **O₂ Requirement** | **Essential** | Not required (Maillard can occur anaerobically) |
| **Temperature** | 20–40 °C optimum; inactivated by blanching | Accelerates at higher T (Maillard ↑ with T) |
| **Examples** | Cut apple, bruised banana, potato | Dried milk, bread crust, scorched sugar |
| **Control** | Blanching, acid dip, sulfites | Lower water activity, low T storage |

*(Distribution: 1 mark definition + 1 mark differentiation + 1 mark example)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"List the enzymes responsible for texture softening in stored fruits."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The major enzymes responsible for texture softening in stored fruits are:

1. **Polygalacturonase (PG)** – Hydrolyzes α-1,4 glycosidic bonds in pectic acid.
2. **Pectin Methyl Esterase (PME)** – Demethylates pectin, making it PG-susceptible.
3. **Cellulase** – Degrades cellulose microfibrils in cell wall.
4. **β-Galactosidase** – Removes galactose residues from pectin side chains.
5. **Pectate Lyase** – β-eliminative cleavage of pectate (especially in soft fruits like mango).

*(1 mark for naming + 1 mark for function + 1 mark for example tissue)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Internal Choice Rule:** Each Part B question offers **Choice A or Choice B**. Both are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Describe in detail the biochemical mechanism of enzymatic browning in fruits and vegetables during storage. Mention the role of PPO and explain any four control methods. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Enzymatic browning is a major cause of post-harvest quality loss in fresh-cut fruits and vegetables. It is catalyzed by the enzyme **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)**, also called tyrosinase, phenolase, or catechol oxidase.

**Mechanism (3 marks):**

The reaction proceeds in two main enzymatic steps:

$$
\text{Monophenol} \xrightarrow{\text{PPO (Hydroxylase)}} o\text{-Diphenol}
$$

$$
o\text{-Diphenol} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{PPO (Oxidase)}} o\text{-Quinone} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

The **o-quinones** are highly reactive and non-enzymatically polymerize with amino acids, proteins, and other quinones to form **melanins** — the brown-black pigments observed on cut apple surfaces.

**Role of PPO:**
- **Active site** contains a **binuclear copper center (Cu²⁺–Cu²⁺)**.
- Optimum **pH = 5.0 – 7.0**; denatured above **90 °C**.
- Subcellular location: **Plastids (chloroplasts)**; substrates in **vacuoles**. Cellular disruption (cutting, bruising) brings them together.

**Control Methods (3 marks):**

| Method | Mechanism | Example |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| **Heat Blanching** (90 °C, 2–5 min) | Denatures PPO irreversibly | Potatoes, mushrooms before freezing |
| **Sulfiting** (SO₂ 100–500 ppm) | Reduces quinones back to phenols | Dried fruits, apple slices |
| **Acid Dip** (1 % citric acid) | Lowers pH below PPO optimum | Fresh-cut fruits |
| **Modified Atmosphere Packaging** | Lowers O₂ to 2–5 % | Pre-cut salads |
| **Cold Storage** (4 °C) | Slows enzyme kinetics | Fresh produce |
| **Chelating Agents** (EDTA) | Removes Cu²⁺ from PPO active site | Mushroom preservation |

**Conclusion (0 mark, optional):** Enzymatic browning is preventable with multi-hurdle strategies combining physical and chemical barriers.

---

#### **(b) Explain the changes in flavour of stored food materials with special reference to lipid oxidation. Discuss its three stages and control measures. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Flavour is the combined perception of taste and aroma. During storage, **lipid oxidation** is the leading cause of off-flavour development in fat-containing foods (oils, nuts, fish, meat, dairy).

**Three Stages of Lipid Oxidation (4 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Initiation:**
A hydrogen atom is abstracted from an unsaturated fatty acid (RH) by initiators (heat, light, metal ions like Fe²⁺/Cu⁺, free radicals):

$$
\text{RH} \xrightarrow{h\nu, \Delta, \text{M}^{n+}} \text{R}^\bullet + \text{H}^\bullet
$$

**Stage 2 — Propagation:**
The alkyl radical reacts with molecular oxygen to form a peroxy radical, which abstracts H from another fatty acid:

$$
\text{R}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROO}^\bullet
$$

$$
\text{ROO}^\bullet + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{R}^\bullet
$$

**Hydroperoxides (ROOH)** are the primary products — odorless at first but unstable.

**Stage 3 — Termination:**
Radicals combine to form non-radical products:

$$
\text{R}^\bullet + \text{R}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{R-R}
$$

$$
\text{ROO}^\bullet + \text{ROO}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{ROOR} + \text{O}_2
$$

Hydroperoxides also **decompose** into **secondary products**: aldehydes (hexanal, malonaldehyde), ketones, alcohols — producing **rancid, painty, fishy off-flavours**.

**Control Measures (2 marks):**

1. **Addition of antioxidants** — BHA, BHT, TBHQ, tocopherols (Vitamin E), ascorbyl palmitate (chain-breaking antioxidants).
2. **Exclusion of O₂** — Vacuum packaging, N₂ flushing, MAP.
3. **Exclusion of light** — Opaque, dark-coloured packaging.
4. **Chelation of pro-oxidant metals** — EDTA, citric acid (synergistic antioxidants).
5. **Low temperature storage** — Reduces rate constant $k$ per Arrhenius/Q₁₀ rule.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) Discuss the various texture changes occurring in fruits and vegetables during storage. Highlight the role of cell wall degrading enzymes and methods to control softening. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Texture (firmness, crispness, juiciness) is a critical quality attribute that deteriorates rapidly after harvest due to cell wall degradation and moisture loss.

**Texture Changes (3 marks):**

1. **Softening/Melting** — Loss of firmness; peaches, mangoes, tomatoes become soft.
2. **Mealiness/Woolliness** — Dry, granular texture (chilling injury in peaches, apples).
3. **Shriveling** — Loss of turgor due to > 3–5 % moisture loss (apples, potatoes).
4. **Hardening** — Lignification in asparagus, sweet corn; retrograded starch in rice.
5. **Loss of Crispness** — Wilting in lettuce, celery.

**Role of Cell Wall Degrading Enzymes (2 marks):**

| Enzyme | Substrate | Action |
|--------|-----------|--------|
| **Polygalacturonase (PG)** | Polygalacturonic acid | Hydrolyzes α-1,4 bonds → middle lamella dissolution |
| **Pectin Methyl Esterase (PME)** | Methylated pectin | Demethylates → makes pectin PG-susceptible |
| **Cellulase** | Cellulose | Degrades microfibrils → weakened wall |
| **β-Galactosidase** | Pectin side chains | Removes galactose → pectin destabilization |
| **Pectate Lyase** | Pectic acid | β-elimination (soft fruits) |

**Control Methods (1 mark):**
- **Calcium chloride (CaCl₂) dip** (1–2 %) — Ca²⁺ crosslinks pectate → firmer texture.
- **Hot Water Treatment** (HWT) at 50–55 °C — Inactivates PG partially.
- **Edible coatings** (chitosan, alginate) — Reduce transpiration.
- **Controlled Atmosphere Storage** (low O₂, high CO₂).
- **Modified Humidity Packaging** (90–95 % RH).

---

#### **(b) Explain the degradation of chlorophyll and carotenoid pigments during storage. How do they affect the colour quality of fruits and vegetables? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Pigments are responsible for the natural colour of foods. During storage, **chlorophyll** (green), **carotenoids** (orange/yellow/red), and **anthocyanins** (red/purple) undergo chemical and enzymatic degradation, leading to **loss of visual appeal**.

**Chlorophyll Degradation (3 marks):**

Chlorophyll consists of a **porphyrin ring with central Mg²⁺** and a phytol tail. The green colour is due to the Mg²⁺-porphyrin complex.

**Mechanism:**

$$
\text{Chlorophyll} \xrightarrow{\text{Chlorophyllase}} \text{Chlorophyllide (still green)}
$$

$$
\text{Chlorophyllide} \xrightarrow{\text{Mg}^{2+} \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+} \text{Pheophytin (olive-brown)}
$$

In stored leafy vegetables (spinach, broccoli), the replacement of Mg²⁺ by H⁺ (low pH, heat) converts chlorophyll → **pheophytin** → **pheophorbide** (yellow-brown).

**Factors accelerating chlorophyll loss:** Low pH, high temperature, presence of acids, oxygen, light.

**Carotenoid Degradation (2 marks):**

- **Carotenoids** (β-carotene, lycopene, xanthophylls) are unsaturated isoprenoids; susceptible to **oxidative degradation**.
- **Lipoxygenase (LOX)** catalyzes co-oxidation of carotenoids with lipids.
- **Isomerization:** *trans*-carotenoids → *cis*-isomers (less colourful).
- Result: Faded orange/red colour in stored carrots, tomatoes, papaya.

**Effect on Colour Quality (1 mark):**

| Storage Change | Visual Impact |
|----------------|---------------|
| Chlorophyll → pheophytin | Green → Yellow/Brown (e.g., yellowing of broccoli) |
| β-carotene oxidation | Orange fades (carrots become pale) |
| Lycopene isomerization | Red dullness in tomato paste |
| Anthocyanin degradation | Loss of red/purple (strawberries, grapes) |

**Control (optional, brief):** Modified atmosphere, low temperature, opaque packaging, antioxidant treatment (BHT, ascorbic acid).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_6_START -->

    COLOUR CHANGE

    TEXTURE CHANGE

---

## 6.2 ⚡ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Must memorize for KTU exams:**

### 🔵 COLOUR CHANGE — Key Points
- **PPO** = enzyme responsible for enzymatic browning; needs **O₂, Cu²⁺, pH 5–7**.
- **Maillard** = non-enzymatic; requires **reducing sugar + amino acid + heat**.
- **Chlorophyll → Pheophytin** when Mg²⁺ is replaced by H⁺ (olive-brown).
- **Ascorbic acid oxidation** causes browning in stored citrus juices.
- **Anthocyanins** are sensitive to **pH**: red (acidic) → purple → blue (alkaline).

### 🟢 TEXTURE CHANGE — Key Points
- **PG (Polygalacturonase)** is the *primary softening enzyme*.
- **PME** acts *before* PG by demethylating pectin.
- **Shriveling threshold** = **3–5 % weight loss** in most fruits.
- **CaCl₂ (1–2 %)** crosslinks pectin → maintains firmness.
- **Starch retrogradation** causes **bread staling** and **rice hardening** in cold storage.

### 🟡 FLAVOUR CHANGE — Key Points
- **Lipid oxidation** = three stages: **Initiation → Propagation → Termination**.
- **Hydroperoxides** = primary oxidation products; **Aldehydes/Ketones** = secondary off-flavour compounds.
- **Hexanal** = marker of oxidation (beany flavor in soy).
- **Limonin** = delayed bitterness in stored citrus.
- **Antioxidants** (BHA, BHT, TBHQ, tocopherols) **donate H** to free radicals, breaking the chain.

### 🔴 FORMULAE TO MEMORIZE (Examiner's Favorite)
- **Q₁₀ relationship:** $S_2 = S_1 \times Q_{10}^{\Delta T/10}$
- **Arrhenius equation:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$
- **Water loss:** $W = A(P_s - P_a)/R_w$

### 🟣 CONTROL STRATEGY Mnemonic — **"BLAST MAP"**
- **B** – Blanching
- **L** – Low temperature
- **A** – Antioxidants
- **S** – Sulfites / Sanitizers
- **T** – Tight packaging (vacuum, N₂)
- **M** – Modified Atmosphere Packaging
- **A** – Acid dips (citric acid)
- **P** – Permeability-controlled edible coatings

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / COMMON PITFALLS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PPO and POD:** PPO is **Polyphenol Oxidase**, NOT Peroxidase. Writing "peroxidase" loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Reversing Q₁₀ formula:** Most students invert $S_1/S_2$. **Remember:** Lower T = Higher shelf-life = $S_2 > S_1$.
> 3. **Forgetting the 3 PPO requirements:** Always mention **O₂ + PPO + Phenol + pH 5–7**. Missing any one = incomplete answer.
> 4. **Maillard vs. Caramelization confusion:** Maillard needs **amino acid + reducing sugar**; caramelization needs **only sugar + high heat (≥160 °C)**.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention lipid oxidation's three stages by name** in 14-mark answers — examiners allocate 3–4 marks specifically for this.
> 6. **No diagrams = lost marks:** Always draw a **labeled flow diagram** for browning or lipid oxidation. A neat diagram fetches **2–3 extra marks** in 14-mark answers.
> 7. **Ignoring units:** Always mention **pH, °C, kJ/mol, a_w**, etc. Examiners reward unit-aware answers.

---

topics.title: Harvesting Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT614 - POST HARVEST HANDLING OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Harvesting Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Harvesting** is the deliberate, scientifically planned operation of separating the economically valuable edible portion (fruit, grain, seed, tuber, leaf, or root) from the parent plant or soil at the precise stage of maturity when its post-harvest quality attributes (firmness, sugar content, color, aroma, shelf-life potential) are at their optimum commercial peak.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology of Post-Harvest Handling of Food Materials, harvesting is treated as the **first critical unit operation** in the post-harvest value chain. It is a **physical separation process** governed by:

$$\text{Harvest Operation} = f(\text{Maturity Index}, \text{Crop Biology}, \text{Climatic Window}, \text{End Use})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (High-Yield):** Harvesting is not merely "picking the fruit." It is a quality-determining step. A wrong harvest stage can NEVER be corrected by downstream refrigeration, packaging, or transportation — making it the most irreversible step in post-harvest technology.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of harvesting like **cutting a diamond at the right facet angle**.

- A diamond cutter must strike at the precise grain direction and angle. If struck too early, the stone fractures. If struck too late, the value drops.
- Similarly, a mango harvested at **75% maturity** ripens into a sweet, aromatic fruit. The same mango harvested at **95% maturity** has poor shelf life, while one harvested at **50% maturity** never ripens properly and tastes acidic.
- Just like a diamond cutter uses a hand-loupe or laser-guided tool depending on the stone, a farmer uses a **sickle, secateur, or combine harvester** depending on the crop.

**In one line:** *Harvesting = "Cutting at the right time, in the right way, with the right tool."*

---

## 1.3 Broad Classification (KTU Syllabus Scope)

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies harvesting methods into three umbrella categories:

| # | Method | Energy Source | Typical Crops |
|---|--------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Manual Harvesting** | Human muscle power | Fruits, vegetables, spices, plantation crops |
| 2 | **Mechanical Harvesting** | Engine / electric motor / PTO | Cereals, oilseeds, pulses, root crops |
| 3 | **Semi-Mechanical / Assisted Harvesting** | Human + tool-assisted | Tea leaves, grapes, apples, mangoes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** Global food losses due to improper harvesting range from **5–25%** of total production (FAO estimate). Mechanical harvesting alone can reduce harvest losses to **2–8%** for cereals if calibrated correctly.

---

## 1.4 Maturity Indices — The Pre-Condition for Harvesting

Harvesting cannot be discussed without maturity indices, because **method selection is dictated by maturity stage**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Maturity is the *internal physiological readiness* of the produce to be harvested for its intended end-use. Maturity ≠ Ripeness. A banana is harvested **mature but unripe**, and ripens later.

### Common Maturity Indices Used in Post-Harvest Practice:

1. **Visual / External Indices** — Color change, size, shape, abscission layer formation
2. **Physical / Mechanical Indices** — Firmness, specific gravity, ease of separation
3. **Chemical / Biochemical Indices** — TSS (Total Soluble Solids), acidity, sugar-acid ratio, starch content
4. **Physiological Indices** — Respiration rate, ethylene evolution, days after flowering (DAF)
5. **Calculated / Derived Indices** — Heat units / Growing Degree Days (GDD)

$$\text{GDD} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{T_{\max,i} + T_{\min,i}}{2} - T_{\text{base}}$$

where $T_{\text{base}}$ is the crop-specific base temperature (typically **10 °C** for most fruits).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "WHY–HOW–WHAT" of Harvesting Method Selection

A KTU examiner expects students to justify *why* a particular method is chosen. The decision flows in three layers:

**Layer 1 — WHY is the crop ready? (Maturity confirmation)**
- Has it reached physiological maturity?
- Does the end-use require fresh consumption, processing, or storage?
- Is the weather window favorable (rain probability, temperature)?

**Layer 2 — HOW will it be detached? (Mechanism)**
- Pull / Twist (e.g., berries)
- Cut / Snip (e.g., mango pedicel)
- Shake (e.g., nuts, cherries)
- Cut + Lift (e.g., paddy)
- Dig / Lift (e.g., carrot, potato)

**Layer 3 — WHAT tool/machine? (Equipment)**
- Hand knife / sickle / secateur
- Platform / ladder
- Tractor-mounted shaker
- Combine harvester

---

## 2.2 Detailed Sub-Classification of Harvesting Methods

### 2.2.1 Manual Harvesting Methods

Manual harvesting remains dominant for **fruits, vegetables, flowers, and plantation crops** because mechanical damage tolerance is very low.

**Common Manual Tools:**

| Tool | Working Principle | Typical Application |
|------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Sickle** | Curved blade, pulling-cutting action | Paddy, wheat, fodder |
| **Secateur (Pruning Shear)** | Two-blade scissor action on stem | Grapes, mango, apple, citrus |
| **Harvesting Knife** | Straight blade, slicing | Cabbage, cauliflower, banana bunch |
| **Pickers / Poles with Bag** | Long pole with cutter + collection bag | Apple, sapota, guava |
| **Ladder + Hand Pick** | Reach high fruits manually | Coconut, arecanut, mango |
| **Climbing with Rope/Spikes** | Human climbs tree trunk | Coconut, toddy palm |

### 2.2.2 Mechanical Harvesting Methods

Mechanical harvesting dominates **field crops** where the entire plant or its seed head is harvested at once.

**Major Mechanical Harvesters:**

| Machine | Crops | Working Principle |
|---------|-------|-------------------|
| **Combine Harvester** | Wheat, paddy, maize, soybean | Reaping + Threshing + Winnowing in one pass |
| **Mower / Reaper** | Fodder, wheat | Cutting standing crop |
| **Thresher** | Paddy, wheat, sorghum | Separating grain from panicle |
| **Potato Digger** | Potato, groundnut | Lifts tubers from soil via share + conveyor |
| **Tree Shaker** | Olive, almond, cherry, nut crops | Inertial shaking detaches mature fruits |
| **Air Blast Sprayer-Shaker** | Citrus | Pulsed air dislodges fruit |
| **Sugar Cane Harvester** | Sugarcane | Cuts base + strips leaves + cuts tops |

### 2.2.3 Semi-Mechanical / Assisted Methods

A hybrid where human skill is augmented by a powered tool.

- **Motorized pruners** for tea, grape vines
- **Platform-mounted hydraulic lifts** for orchard fruit picking
- **Hand-held mechanical shakers** for small orchards
- **Vacuum harvesters** for blueberries (suction-based detachment)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA SET FOR MODULE 3 — MEMORIZE FOR ESE**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Growing Degree Days | $\text{GDD} = \sum \dfrac{T_{\max}+T_{\min}}{2} - T_{\text{base}}$ | °C-day | Predict harvest date |
| 2 | Sugar-Acid Ratio | $\text{SAR} = \dfrac{\text{TSS (%)}}{\text{Titratable Acidity (%)}}$ | dimensionless | Fruit maturity index |
| 3 | Harvesting Efficiency | $\eta_{H} = \dfrac{M_{H}}{M_{A}} \times 100$ | % | Compare manual vs mechanical |
| 4 | Mechanical Damage Index | $\text{MDI} = \dfrac{D}{T} \times 100$ | % | Quality loss assessment |
| 5 | Throughput of Harvester | $Q = v \cdot w \cdot Y \cdot \eta_{f}$ | kg/hr or t/ha | Field capacity |
| 6 | Force to Detach Fruit | $F_{d} = F_{p} - F_{a}$ | N (Newton) | Tree-shaker design |
| 7 | Respiration Rate Index | $R = \dfrac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{m \cdot t}$ | mL CO₂/kg·hr | Shelf-life estimation |

**Where:**

- $M_{H}$ = mass of harvested produce
- $M_{A}$ = mass of available mature produce
- $D$ = damaged units, $T$ = total units
- $v$ = forward speed of machine (m/s)
- $w$ = working width (m)
- $Y$ = crop yield (kg/m²)
- $\eta_{f}$ = field efficiency factor (**typically 0.7–0.85**)
- $F_{p}$ = pedicel retention force, $F_{a}$ = abscission layer resistance

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Designing tree shakers:** Engineers use $F_{d}$ values to size eccentric mass, frequency (typically **10–25 Hz**), and amplitude (**2–5 cm**).
- **Cold chain logistics:** Knowing harvest maturity allows warehouse managers to schedule pre-cooling capacity.
- **Precision agriculture:** GDD-based harvest forecasting integrates with IoT weather stations.
- **Food processing industries:** TSS and acidity at harvest determine whether fruits go to **juice, jam, fresh market, or drying**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Harvesting Efficiency Calculation

A mango orchard has **12,000 mature fruits** available for harvest. A team of pickers using manual ladders harvested **10,800 fruits** in a single working day. Determine the **harvesting efficiency** and identify the **gap due to losses / non-recovery**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the given data.**

$$M_{A} = 12{,}000 \text{ fruits (available)}$$

$$M_{H} = 10{,}800 \text{ fruits (harvested)}$$

**Step 2 — Recall the formula from the cheat sheet.**

$$\eta_{H} = \frac{M_{H}}{M_{A}} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values into the formula.**

$$\eta_{H} = \frac{10{,}800}{12{,}000} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Perform the division.**

$$\eta_{H} = 0.90 \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Final answer with units.**

$$\boxed{\eta_{H} = 90\%}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result (mandatory for full marks).**

The orchard had **10% loss/non-recovery** (1,200 fruits lost). This represents fallen fruits, fruits too high to reach, and bird-damaged fruits. To improve, the farmer should adopt **hydraulic platform lifts** to push efficiency above **95%**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Sugar-Acid Ratio (SAR) for Fruit Harvest Decision

A batch of guava fruits at harvest shows:
- Total Soluble Solids (TSS) = **12.5 °Brix**
- Titratable Acidity (TA) = **0.65% citric acid equivalent**

Determine the SAR and decide whether the fruits are suitable for **table consumption** (SAR ≥ 15) or **processing** (SAR 8–15).

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify data.**

$$\text{TSS} = 12.5, \quad \text{TA} = 0.65$$

**Step 2 — Apply SAR formula.**

$$\text{SAR} = \frac{\text{TSS (%)}}{\text{TA (\%)}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$\text{SAR} = \frac{12.5}{0.65}$$

**Step 4 — Perform the division.**

$$\text{SAR} = 19.23$$

**Step 5 — Final answer.**

$$\boxed{\text{SAR} = 19.23}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering decision.**

Since SAR > 15, the guavas are suitable for **fresh table consumption**. For processing into **guava paste or jelly**, fruits with SAR 8–15 would be preferred due to higher pectin content.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — Combine Harvester Throughput

A combine harvester operates in a wheat field with the following parameters:
- Forward speed $v = 1.5$ m/s
- Working width (cutter bar) $w = 4$ m
- Crop yield $Y = 0.8$ kg/m²
- Field efficiency $\eta_{f} = 0.80$

Calculate the **effective throughput** $Q$ in **kg/hour**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Recall the throughput formula.**

$$Q = v \cdot w \cdot Y \cdot \eta_{f}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values.**

$$Q = 1.5 \times 4 \times 0.8 \times 0.80$$

**Step 3 — Multiply step by step.**

$$Q = 1.5 \times 4 = 6$$

$$Q = 6 \times 0.8 = 4.8$$

$$Q = 4.8 \times 0.80 = 3.84 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert from kg/s to kg/hr.**

Since **1 hour = 3600 seconds**:

$$Q = 3.84 \times 3600$$

**Step 5 — Final numerical value.**

$$Q = 13{,}824 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 6 — Express in tonnes per hour.**

$$Q = 13.824 \text{ tonnes/hr} \approx 13.82 \text{ t/hr}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 13.82 \text{ tonnes/hr}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Manual vs Mechanical Harvesting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a frequently asked 7-mark question in KTU Module 3.**

| Parameter | Manual Harvesting | Mechanical Harvesting |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Capital Cost** | Very low | Very high (₹15–50 lakhs for combine) |
| **Operating Cost** | High (labor wages) | Low per unit mass |
| **Labor Requirement** | 20–50 persons/ha | 1–2 operators |
| **Speed of Operation** | 0.1–0.5 ha/day | 1–3 ha/hr |
| **Selectivity** | High (only mature fruits picked) | Low (whole crop harvested) |
| **Damage to Produce** | 1–5% (gentle handling) | 5–15% (impact bruises) |
| **Suitability for Fresh Market** | Excellent | Poor to moderate |
| **Suitability for Processing** | Good | Excellent |
| **Skill Required** | Trained pickers | Trained machine operators + mechanics |
| **Field Losses** | 5–10% | 2–8% if well-calibrated |
| **Crop Examples** | Mango, apple, banana, tomato, chilli | Wheat, paddy, soybean, maize |

---

## 3.5 Harvesting Sequence (KTU Board-Standard Steps)

For a **14-mark descriptive answer**, the student should write these 7 sequential steps:

1. **Pre-harvest assessment** — Maturity evaluation via TSS, color charts, firmness, DAF
2. **Decision on method** — Manual / mechanical / semi-mechanical
3. **Field preparation** — Ladders, crates, bins, clippers sanitized
4. **Harvesting operation** — Detachment at proper pedicel point
5. **Field collection** — Primary containers (crate/basket)
6. **Pre-sorting** — Remove damaged, diseased, over-ripe
7. **Transport to pack-house / cold storage** — Within **2–4 hours** for perishables

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — Decision Tree for Harvesting Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crop Ready for Harvest] --> B{Is Crop a Fruit or Vegetable?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Total Cultivated Area}
    B -- No --> D{Cereal / Oilseed / Pulse?}
    C -- Less than 2 ha --> E[Manual Harvesting]
    C -- More than 5 ha --> F{Is Produce for Fresh Market?}
    F -- Yes --> E
    F -- No --> G[Semi-Mechanical Assisted]
    D -- Yes --> H{Field Size and Topography}
    H -- Flat, More than 1 ha --> I[Combine Harvester]
    H -- Small or Uneven --> J[Reaper plus Thresher]
    E --> K[Use Secateurs, Poles, Ladders]
    G --> L[Use Hydraulic Platforms, Motorized Pruners]
    I --> M[Reap plus Thresh plus Clean in Single Pass]
    J --> N[Two Stage Operation]
    K --> O[Collect in Crates to Pack House]
    L --> O
    M --> P[Bulk Transport to Silo]
    N --> P
```

**Diagram Caption:** Decision tree showing how crop type, market destination, and field size dictate the choice of harvesting method — directly aligned with KTU Module 3 syllabus outcomes.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Combine Harvester

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Header and Reel] --> B[Cutter Bar]
    B --> C[Conveyor and Auger]
    C --> D[Threshing Cylinder]
    D --> E[Straw Walkers]
    D --> F[Cleaning Sieves]
    F --> G[Blower Fan]
    G --> H[Grain Elevator]
    H --> I[Grain Tank]
    E --> J[Straw Discharge]
    F --> K[Chaff Discharge]
    I --> L[Bagging or Bulk Unload]
```

**Diagram Caption:** Functional block diagram showing the seven-stage separation process inside a combine harvester — Reaping → Threshing → Separation → Cleaning → Collection. This is the **gold-standard answer illustration** for 14-mark questions on mechanical harvesting.

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Tree Shaker Operational Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tractor PTO] --> B[Rotary Eccentric Mass]
    B --> C[Vibration at 10 to 25 Hz]
    C --> D[Clamp Attached to Trunk or Limb]
    D --> E[Inertial Force on Fruit]
    E --> F[Abscission Layer Shears]
    F --> G[Fruit Falls onto Catch Frame]
    G --> H[Conveyor to Collection Bin]
    H --> I[Pre-cooling or Cold Storage]
```

**Diagram Caption:** Shows the engineered process of inertial detachment used for nuts, olives, cherries, and certain citrus — a high-value question for KTU 2024 ESE.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define harvesting. Why is it considered the most critical unit operation in post-harvest handling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Harvesting is the operation of separating the edible portion of a crop from its parent plant at the stage of maturity suitable for its intended end-use.

It is the most critical unit operation because **post-harvest quality cannot be improved after harvesting** — only preserved. Wrong maturity at harvest leads to irreversible losses in taste, texture, shelf life, and market value. Therefore, harvesting is the *point of no return* in the post-harvest chain.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for "irreversible" concept, 1 mark for example/elaboration.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`
**List any four maturity indices used to decide the harvesting time of fruits.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Visual** — Change in skin color (e.g., mango green → yellow)
2. **Physical** — Fruit firmness (e.g., apple penetrometer reading 60–70 N)
3. **Chemical** — Total Soluble Solids (TSS) ≥ 12 °Brix for ripe mango
4. **Physiological** — Days after flowering (DAF), e.g., 90–110 days for Alphonso mango

*(Award ¾ mark each for four valid indices.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B1 — OPTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]`

**(a)** Classify harvesting methods in detail. Discuss the working principle, suitability, and limitations of mechanical harvesting for cereals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram the functional components of a combine harvester. Discuss its field efficiency and the factors affecting throughput. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification of Harvesting Methods:**

| Class | Sub-types | Examples |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| Manual | Hand picking, cutting, pulling | Mango, apple, tomato |
| Mechanical | Combine, reaper, shaker | Wheat, paddy, almond |
| Semi-mechanical | Motorized pruners, hydraulic platforms | Tea, grape, citrus |

**Mechanical harvesting for cereals:**

- **Working principle:** A combine harvester performs **reaping + threshing + winnowing** in a single pass.
- **Suitability:** Best for large, flat fields of paddy, wheat, soybean, maize, and sunflower.
- **Limitations:**
  - High capital cost (**₹20–50 lakhs**)
  - Inefficient on small / irregular plots
  - Crop losses of 5–15% due to shattering and threshing damage
  - Not suitable for lodged (fallen) crops

**(b) Combine Harvester Block Diagram** *(Refer to Section 4.2 above)*:

The seven functional stages are:

1. **Header with reel** — gathers standing crop
2. **Cutter bar** — shears stalks at base
3. **Conveyor + auger** — feeds cut crop into threshing cylinder
4. **Threshing cylinder** — beats grain from panicle
5. **Straw walkers** — separate grain from straw by vibration
6. **Cleaning sieves + fan** — separate grain from chaff by air-stream
7. **Grain tank** — collects cleaned grain; discharged via auger

**Throughput formula:**

$$Q = v \cdot w \cdot Y \cdot \eta_{f}$$

**Field efficiency** typically ranges from **0.7 to 0.85**. Factors affecting throughput:
- Forward speed and cutter width
- Crop moisture content (ideally **14–18%** for threshing)
- Field topography and operator skill
- Grain-straw ratio

---

### **Question B1 — OPTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`

**(a)** Discuss the maturity indices used for harvesting fruits. How is the sugar-acid ratio used as a maturity indicator? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare manual and mechanical harvesting with respect to cost, quality, speed, and suitability. Mention suitable crops for each method. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Maturity Indices for Fruits:**

Maturity indices are physical, chemical, physiological, and visual markers that signal the right time to harvest.

**Major indices:**

1. **Days After Flowering (DAF)** — Each cultivar has a fixed DAF window
2. **Skin Color** — Chlorophyll breakdown reveals carotenoids / anthocyanins
3. **Firmness** — Penetrometer reading (apple: 60–70 N)
4. **Total Soluble Solids (TSS)** — Refractometer reading in °Brix
5. **Titratable Acidity (TA)** — Drops as fruit ripens
6. **Sugar-Acid Ratio (SAR)** — Combined maturity indicator

**Sugar-Acid Ratio (SAR) derivation:**

$$\text{SAR} = \frac{\text{TSS (\%)}}{\text{Titratable Acidity (\%)}}$$

For example, mango variety Alphonso is harvested when SAR = **20–25**. Citrus fruits for juice need SAR ≥ **7–8**. A high SAR indicates sweetness dominance, while a low SAR indicates sourness — making SAR the most reliable chemical index.

**(b) Comparison Table** *(Refer to Section 3.4 above for full matrix)*:

| Parameter | Manual | Mechanical |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Capital cost | Low | High |
| Selectivity | High | Low |
| Speed | Slow | Fast |
| Damage | Low (1–5%) | Moderate (5–15%) |
| Best crops | Fruits, vegetables | Cereals, oilseeds |

**Suitable crops:**
- **Manual:** Mango, banana, tomato, apple, chilli, flowers
- **Mechanical:** Paddy, wheat, maize, soybean, sunflower
- **Semi-mechanical:** Tea, coffee, grape, citrus

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing maturity with ripeness** — most common deduct **1–2 marks** mistake.
> 2. **Skipping units** in formula answers (always write °Brix, %, kg/hr).
> 3. **Writing block diagrams without arrows** — KTU examiners want functional flow arrows.
> 4. **Listing maturity indices without specifying which crop** — examiners expect "TSS ≥ 12 °Brix for **mango**" not just "TSS".
> 5. **Omitting harvesting efficiency numerical interpretation** — always state "10% loss observed" after a numerical answer.
> 6. **In 14-mark questions, forgetting the "limitations" sub-part** — most common 2-mark loss.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### ✅ Rapid Revision Checklist

- ☐ Harvesting = first + **irreversible** unit operation in post-harvest chain
- ☐ Three methods: **Manual / Mechanical / Semi-mechanical**
- ☐ Maturity ≠ Ripeness — maturity at harvest; ripeness after harvest
- ☐ Five categories of maturity indices: visual, physical, chemical, physiological, calculated
- ☐ GDD formula: $\sum \dfrac{T_{\max}+T_{\min}}{2} - T_{\text{base}}$, base temp typically **10 °C**
- ☐ SAR formula: $\dfrac{\text{TSS}}{\text{TA}}$, mango harvest SAR ≈ **20–25**
- ☐ Harvesting efficiency $\eta_H = \dfrac{M_H}{M_A} \times 100$, target ≥ **90%**
- ☐ Combine harvester = Reap + Thresh + Winnow in single pass
- ☐ Field efficiency $\eta_f =$ **0.7–0.85**
- ☐ Throughput $Q = v \cdot w \cdot Y \cdot \eta_f$ in kg/hr or t/hr
- ☐ Tree shaker detachment uses inertial force at **10–25 Hz**, amplitude **2–5 cm**
- ☐ Manual harvesting damage: **1–5%**; Mechanical damage: **5–15%**
- ☐ Combine cost: **₹20–50 lakhs**; Operates at **1–3 ha/hr**
- ☐ Standard harvesting sequence = **7 steps** (assessment → decision → field prep → harvest → collect → sort → transport)

### 🎯 KTU 2024 Module 3 Outcome Alignment

This topic directly satisfies:
- **CO3:** Apply knowledge of maturity indices and harvesting techniques to minimize post-harvest losses
- **CO4:** Evaluate the suitability of manual vs mechanical harvesting for different crops
- **Bloom's Levels Covered:** Remember (L1), Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyze (L4)

> **End of Module 3 — Harvesting Methods Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Conditions and Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT614 - POST HARVEST HANDLING OF FOOD MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Storage Conditions and Types # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Food Storage in Post-Harvest Engineering? In the context of post-harvest technology, **storage** is defined as the preservation and protection of food materials (cereals, pulses, oilseeds, fruits, vegetables, spices, and processed foods) between the time of harvest and the time of consumption. The principal objective is to **maintain quality, quantity, and nutritional value** while preventing quantitative losses caused by **biotic** (insects, moulds, rodents, microbes) and **abiotic** (temperature, humidity, moisture) factors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** > As per the OEFTT614 syllabus, the central theme of Module 3 is to enable the student to appreciate the link between **physical/biological activity of the food material** and the **storage microenvironment**. You will be expected to design (or justify) storage environments for specific commodities. The global post-harvest food loss is reported to be roughly **30–40% for perishables** and **5–15% for durable (non-perishable) grains** when storage practices are inadequate. This makes storage engineering a cornerstone of food security. --- ## 1.2 Storage Conditions – The Five Pillars Food deterioration during storage is governed by five inter-linked environmental parameters. Mastering these is equivalent to "tuning the knobs" of the storage environment. | # | Parameter | Symbol/Unit | Role in Storage | |---|-----------|-------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Temperature** | T (°C / K) | Controls respiration, microbial & enzymatic kinetics | | 2 | **Relative Humidity** | RH (%) | Governs moisture migration & mould growth | | 3 | **Atmospheric Composition** | O₂, CO₂, N₂ (%) | Determines respiration & oxidative spoilage | | 4 | **Moisture Content of the food** | M (%, w.b.) | Decides whether the food is "safe" microbiologically | | 5 | **Time** | t (days / months) | Cumulative effect of above parameters | > [!NOTE] > **Key Biochemical Insight:** > The **Q₁₀ rule** states that for every **10 °C rise** in temperature, the rate of biological/chemical reactions (respiration, lipid oxidation, microbial growth) **roughly doubles (Q₁₀ ≈ 2)**. This is why low-temperature storage dramatically extends shelf life. $$ k_2 \approx k_1 \cdot Q_{10}^{\left(\frac{T_2 - T_1}{10}\right)} $$ --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > **Analogy – "The House for the Food"** > Think of a storage structure as a **house** and the food material as a **resident**. > - **Temperature** is like the **air-conditioning setting** (too hot → resident gets sick). > - **Relative Humidity** is like **indoor moisture** (too damp → moulds grow on the walls). > - **Gas composition** is like the **ventilation** (closed room = suffocation, polluted air = spoilage). > - **Moisture content** is the **resident's body water** (more water = more life for microbes). > - **Time** is simply **how long the resident stays** in the house. > > A good storage engineer builds a house that keeps the resident **cool, dry, breathable, and safe** for the longest possible time. --- ## 1.4 Classification of Storage – The Big Picture The KTU syllabus broadly classifies storage systems into the following families: 1. **Traditional / Indigenous Storage Structures** — mud bins, bamboo baskets, underground pits, **khallu**, **kutcha houses**. 2. **Improved / Scientific Storage Structures** — **Pusa bin**, **metal silos**, **gobars gas-based storage**, **Hapur Kothi**. 3. **Modern / Engineered Storage** — **Cold storage (refrigerated)**, **Controlled Atmosphere (CA) storage**, **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)**, **Hermetic storage**, **warehouses with aeration**. Each of these will be elaborated in Section 2 and Section 3. --- ## 1.5 Why Study "Conditions and Types" Together? Because they are **co-dependent**: - A **type** (e.g., cold storage) is chosen only because the **condition** (e.g., low temperature) is required. - The condition is enforced *by* the structure/type. - In exam questions, KTU frequently pairs them — "Discuss the storage conditions for X" AND "Suggest a suitable storage type." # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Storage Conditions ### 2.1.1 Temperature (T) - **Respiration rate** of fresh produce follows **Arrhenius-type kinetics**. - Most fruits & vegetables are best stored near **0–4 °C** (just above freezing). - Tropical produce (banana, mango, papaya) suffers **chilling injury** below ~13 °C. - Grains are best stored below **15 °C** to suppress insect activity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite Concept:** > "**Chilling Injury vs Freezing Injury**" – Students often confuse the two. > - Chilling injury = metabolic disorder due to exposure to 0–13 °C in chilling-sensitive species. > - Freezing injury = physical damage from ice nucleation below 0 °C. ### 2.1.2 Relative Humidity (RH) - Defined as the ratio of actual water vapour pressure to saturation vapour pressure at the same T. - **High RH (85–95 %)** → desirable for most fresh fruits/vegetables (prevents shrivelling). - **Low RH (< 70 %)** → essential for dry grains (paddy, wheat) to prevent mould. $$ RH (\%) = \frac{p_{H_2O}}{p_{H_2O}^{sat}(T)} \times 100 $$ ### 2.1.3 Atmospheric Composition - **Normal air**: O₂ ≈ 21 %, CO₂ ≈ 0.04 %, N₂ ≈ 78 %. - **Controlled Atmosphere (CA)**: O₂ lowered to 2–5 %; CO₂ raised to 2–5 %. - **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)**: gases are *initially* adjusted inside sealed packs; further active control is limited. ### 2.1.4 Moisture Content (M) - Expressed as **% wet basis (w.b.)** or **% dry basis (d.b.**). - **Safe storage moisture** for cereals is generally **≤ 12–14 % w.b.** (depending on grain). - The relationship is given by the **sorption isotherm** of the food. $$ M_{w.b.}(\%) = \frac{m_{water}}{m_{water} + m_{dry\,matter}} \times 100 $$ $$ M_{d.b.}(\%) = \frac{m_{water}}{m_{dry\,matter}} \times 100 $$ ### 2.1.5 Time & Respiration Quotient (RQ) The **respiration quotient** is a diagnostic indicator: $$ RQ = \frac{CO_2\,\text{produced (mol)}}{O_2\,\text{consumed (mol)}} $$ - RQ = 1 → carbohydrate substrate (glucose/fructose). - RQ > 1 → organic acid substrate (e.g., malic acid in apple). - RQ < 1 → lipid/fat substrate (e.g., in oilseeds). --- ## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Storage Types ### 2.2.1 Traditional Storage Structures | Structure | Region / Use | Capacity | Material | |-----------|--------------|----------|----------| | Mud bin / Kuthla | Rural India | 1–5 t | Clay + straw | | Bamboo basket (Pala / Doko) | Hills | 50–100 kg | Bamboo | | Underground pit (Khatti) | Dry regions | 1–10 t | Soil | | Kachha house (Kutcha godown) | All India | 5–50 t | Wood/thatch | | Gummi / earthen pot | South India | 50–500 kg | Clay | **Limitations:** High losses (10–30 %), susceptible to rodents, insects, and moisture ingress. ### 2.2.2 Improved Storage Structures - **Pusa Bin (IARI design)** – galvanised iron cylindrical bin, capacity 1.5–15 t, fitted with aeration duct and lid. **Loss < 1 %**. - **Hapur Kothi / Nanda bin** – large capacity metal silos for cooperative godowns. - **Gobar Gas Linked Storage** – airtight grain silo connected to a biogas plant; CO₂ from biogas displaces O₂, suffocating insects. ### 2.2.3 Modern / Engineered Storage 1. **Cold Storage (Refrigerated)** - Uses mechanical vapour-compression refrigeration. - Maintains 0–4 °C and 85–95 % RH. - Capacity ranges from 5 t to > 50 000 t. 2. **Controlled Atmosphere (CA) Storage** - Combines low T + low O₂ + high CO₂. - **Used for: apple, pear, kiwi, cabbage.** - Oxygen maintained at **1–3 %**, CO₂ at **2–5 %**. 3. **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)** - **Active MAP**: gases flushed in during packing. - **Passive MAP**: relies on product respiration to modify the headspace. 4. **Hermetic Storage** - Airtight sealing → insects and grain respire → O₂ falls below 2 % → insects die. - **Triple bagging (PICS bags)** is a classic example developed by Purdue University. 5. **Warehouse / Silos with Aeration** - Uses forced ventilation to maintain uniform T and moisture. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Print this section before exams.** It contains every equation that has appeared (or is highly likely to appear) in OEFTT614 question papers. | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Application | |---|---------|---------|-------|-------------| | 1 | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{10/(T_2-T_1)}$ | Temperature acceleration factor | dimensionless | Shelf-life calculation | | 2 | $k = A\,e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius equation for spoilage | s⁻¹ | Respiration / oxidation | | 3 | $RH = \dfrac{p_v}{p_{vs}(T)} \times 100$ | Relative humidity | % | Storage microenvironment | | 4 | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w+m_d}\times 100$ | Moisture (wet basis) | % | Grain safety | | 5 | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d}\times 100$ | Moisture (dry basis) | % | Isotherm plotting | | 6 | $RQ = \dfrac{n_{CO_2}}{n_{O_2}}$ | Respiration quotient | dimensionless | Commodity diagnostics | | 7 | $EMC = f(RH,T)$ | Equilibrium moisture content | % w.b. | Predicting moisture change | | 8 | $S_{min} = T_{dewp} + 1\,°C$ | Safe cold storage setting | °C | Cold room design | | 9 | $\dot{Q} = UA\,\Delta T_{LM}$ | Refrigeration load | W (J/s) | Cold storage sizing | | 10 | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | K | Heat load calculation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Rule of Thumb:** > For every **1 % rise in moisture content** of wheat above 14 % w.b., the **safe storage life reduces by ~50 %** under tropical conditions. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Concepts - **Export Industry:** Indian mango exporters use **CA + MAP** to ship Alphonso to the EU and USA. - **Public Distribution System (PDS):** India's Food Corporation of India (FCI) uses **metal silos and CAP (Cover-and-Plinth) storage** for 60+ million tonnes of grain. - **FSSAI Standards:** Mandate RH ≤ 70 % in dry-goods warehouses and 85–95 % in cold stores. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Q₁₀ Temperature Coefficient & Shelf-Life Estimation **Problem Statement:** At 25 °C, the respiration rate of a fruit is measured to be $k_1 = 20$ mg CO₂ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹. Assuming $Q_{10} = 2$, what is the respiration rate at 5 °C? How much does the shelf life increase? ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Identify the parameters.** $$ T_1 = 25\,°C,\quad k_1 = 20\;\frac{mg\,CO_2}{kg\cdot h},\quad T_2 = 5\,°C,\quad Q_{10} = 2 $$ **Step 2 — Write the Q₁₀ relationship.** $$ \frac{k_2}{k_1} = Q_{10}^{\;\Delta T/10},\quad \text{where } \Delta T = T_1 - T_2 $$ **Step 3 — Compute ΔT.** $$ \Delta T = 25 - 5 = 20\,°C $$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the equation.** $$ \frac{k_2}{20} = 2^{\,20/10} = 2^{2} = 4 $$ **Step 5 — Solve for k₂.** $$ k_2 = 20 \times 4 = 80\;\frac{mg\,CO_2}{kg\cdot h} $$ **Step 6 — Interpret the result and the shelf life.** Since respiration rate at 5 °C is *lower*, the spoilage rate is *slower*. The ratio of shelf lives is the inverse ratio of rates: $$ \frac{SL_2}{SL_1} = \frac{k_1}{k_2} = \frac{20}{80} = \frac{1}{4} $$ That is, **respiration is 4× faster at 25 °C than at 5 °C**, so the fruit's storage life at 5 °C is **4× longer** than at 25 °C. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is exactly why **cold chain logistics** is the single most powerful post-harvest tool for perishables. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH) & Safe Moisture **Problem Statement:** Wheat at moisture 13 % w.b. has an EMC/ERH relationship such that ERH = 65 % at 25 °C. The critical relative humidity for *Aspergillus* mould is ~70 %. Will the grain be safe? ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Compare ERH to the critical mould RH.** $$ ERH_{grain} = 65\,\% < 70\,\% = ERH_{Aspergillus} $$ **Step 2 — Conclusion.** $$ \text{ERH}_{grain} < \text{ERH}_{crit} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{No mould growth.} $$ **Step 3 — Re-evaluate at 16 % w.b. moisture.** If the sorption isotherm gives ERH = 78 % at 16 % w.b., then: $$ 78\,\% > 70\,\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Mould will grow; grain is unsafe.} $$ **Step 4 — General relationship.** For safe storage, the following inequality must hold: $$ ERH_{food}(M,T) \;\;<\;\; ERH_{microbe\,critical} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite:** > This is the **"water activity principle"**. The critical aw for most spoilage moulds is **0.70**, for yeasts **0.60**, and for bacteria **0.86**. Foods with **aw < 0.60** are microbiologically shelf-stable. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Refrigeration Load for a Cold Store **Problem Statement:** A cold store contains 10 tonnes of apples at 1 °C. Apples are loaded at 25 °C once per day. The specific heat of apples is $c_p = 3.6$ kJ kg⁻¹ K⁻¹. Respiration heat is ~1.5 kJ kg⁻¹ day⁻¹. Ambient = 35 °C. Wall heat gain $\dot{Q}_{wall} = 4$ kW. Compute the **peak refrigeration load**. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Sensible heat of produce cooling (once per day).** $$ Q_1 = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 10000 \times 3.6 \times (25-1) $$ $$ Q_1 = 10000 \times 3.6 \times 24 = 864{,}000\;\text{kJ/day} $$ **Step 2 — Respiration heat.** $$ Q_2 = 10000 \times 1.5 = 15{,}000\;\text{kJ/day} $$ **Step 3 — Wall heat gain.** $$ Q_3 = \dot{Q}_{wall} \times 24 \times 3600 = 4 \times 86400 = 345{,}600\;\text{kJ/day} $$ **Step 4 — Total daily load.** $$ Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 = 864{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 345{,}600 = 1{,}224{,}600\;\text{kJ/day} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to kW (peak load).** $$ \dot{Q}_{peak} = \frac{1{,}224{,}600}{24 \times 3600} = \frac{1{,}224{,}600}{86{,}400} \approx 14.17\;\text{kW} $$ **Step 6 — Add a 20 % safety margin (KTU norm).** $$ \dot{Q}_{design} = 14.17 \times 1.20 \approx 17.0\;\text{kW} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Condition vs. Commodity | Commodity | Recommended T (°C) | Recommended RH (%) | Max Safe Moisture (%, w.b.) | Preferred Storage Type | Approx. Shelf Life | |-----------|---------------------|--------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------|---------------------| | Paddy / Rice | 10–15 | 60–70 | 13–14 | Metal silo, Pusa bin | 6–12 months | | Wheat | 10–15 | 60–70 | 12–13 | Metal silo, FCI godown | 6–18 months | | Maize (shelled) | 5–10 | 65–70 | 13–14 | Hermetic PICS bag | 6–12 months | | Apple | 0–4 | 90–95 | — | CA store | 4–8 months | | Banana | 13–15 | 85–90 | — | Ripening room (ethylene) | 1–2 weeks | | Potato | 3–5 | 90–95 | — | Cold store (dark) | 4–6 months | | Tomato (ripe) | 8–10 | 85–90 | — | MAP | 1–2 weeks | | Onion | 0–2 | 65–70 | — | Cold store (dry) | 4–6 months | | Mango | 12–14 | 85–90 | — | CA + MAP | 2–4 weeks | | Pulses (dal) | 5–10 | < 60 | 9–10 | Metal silo | 9–12 months | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Concept Map – Storage Engineering Framework ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Harvested Food Material] --> B[Identify Commodity] B --> C{Perishable?} C -- Yes --> D[Fresh Fruits / Vegetables / Dairy / Meat] C -- No --> E[Cereals / Pulses / Oilseeds / Spices] D --> F[Respiration & Moisture Loss] E --> G[Insect & Mould Damage] F --> H[Apply Cold Storage 0-4C] F --> I[Apply CA or MAP] F --> J[Apply Ethylene Control] G --> K[Apply Hermetic PICS] G --> L[Apply Metal Silo or Pusa Bin] G --> M[Aeration & Drying] H --> N[Quality Maintained at Consumer End] I --> N J --> N K --> N L --> N M --> N ``` --- ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram – Modern Cold Storage Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Produce Inlet
with Pre-cooling] --> B1[Insulated Cold Room
T 0-4C, RH 90-95%] B1 --> C1[Refrigeration Unit
Vapour Compression] C1 --> D1[Condenser
Heat Rejection to Ambient] C1 --> E1[Evaporator Coils
Inside Cold Room] E1 --> B1 B1 --> F1[Humidifier / Dehumidifier] F1 --> B1 B1 --> G1[Ethylene Scrubber
KMnO4 or Ozone] G1 --> B1 B1 --> H1[Atmosphere Control
O2 2-3 percent, CO2 3-5 percent] H1 --> B1 B1 --> I1[Storage Outlets
Dispatch to Market] I1 --> J1[Quality Checked
Consignment] ``` --- ## 4.3 Decision Tree – Choosing the Right Storage Type ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Start: Choose Storage Type] --> Q1{Is the commodity
perishable?} Q1 -- No --> Q2{Storage duration?} Q2 -- Less than 3 months --> R1[Improved Pusa Bin
or CAP Warehouse] Q2 -- More than 3 months --> R2[Metal Silo
with Aeration] Q1 -- Yes --> Q3{Is chilling
injury risk?} Q3 -- Yes --> R3[CA Store at 13-15C
e.g., Banana, Mango] Q3 -- No --> R4{RH requirement
high?} R4 -- Yes --> R5[Cold Store 0-4C
90-95 percent RH] R4 -- No --> R6[MAP Packaging
plus Cool Storage] R5 --> END[Quality Retention Achieved] R6 --> END R3 --> END R1 --> END R2 --> END ``` --- ## 4.4 Hermetic Storage Mechanism – Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Airtight PICS Bag or Cocoon] --> P2[Inside: Grain
plus Insects and Mould] P2 --> P3[Grain and Pests
Respire and Consume O2] P3 --> P4[O2 Level Drops
from 21 percent to below 2 percent] P4 --> P5[CO2 Rises
to above 15 percent] P5 --> P6[Insects and Mould
Asphyxiate and Die] P6 --> P7[Moisture Retained
No Aeration Loss] P7 --> P8[Grain Safe
for 6-12 Months] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define the term "safe storage moisture content" of a food grain. Mention the safe moisture level for wheat and paddy.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Safe storage moisture content is the maximum moisture content (% w.b.) at which a grain can be stored for **6–12 months** without significant mould growth, insect activity, or quality deterioration. It is governed by the **equilibrium relative humidity (ERH) of the grain** being below the critical ERH (~70 %) for common storage moulds. > > - **Wheat:** ~12–13 % w.b. > - **Paddy (rough rice):** ~13–14 % w.b. > > *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for principle, 1 mark for the two values.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **What is Controlled Atmosphere (CA) storage? How does it differ from Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | CA Storage | MAP | |---------|------------|-----| | Atmosphere regulation | Continuous active control | Initial flush only | | Gas tightness | Highly engineered room | Sealed pack | | Composition change | Monitored & corrected dynamically | Drifts with respiration | | Commodities | Apple, pear, kiwi (long-term) | Tomato, salad, fresh-cut (short-term) | | Cost | High capital | Low capital | *(1 mark each for definition + 2-mark comparative table.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > In Part-B questions, KTU examiners *strictly* allocate 7 marks for each sub-part. **Common pitfalls where students lose marks:** > 1. Writing only definitions and skipping the **engineering/quantitative analysis** (loses 2–3 marks per sub-part). > 2. Missing **units** in numerical answers (loses 1 mark per sub-part). > 3. Failing to draw a **labelled diagram** of the storage structure (loses 2 marks in sub-parts that ask for "describe with diagram"). > 4. Confusing **CA** with **MA** and **Hermetic** storage. --- ### PART B — Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper, Modified]** **Q. (a)** Describe the various **storage conditions** required for food grains with reference to temperature, relative humidity, moisture content, and gas composition. (7 marks) **Model Solution — Part (a):** 1. **Temperature (2 marks):** - Optimum: 10–15 °C for cereals, pulses, oilseeds. - Higher T accelerates **respiration of insects and moulds** (Q₁₀ effect). - High T also increases **lipid oxidation** in oilseeds. - **Insect activity ceases** below 13 °C; **mould growth stops** below 5 °C. 2. **Relative Humidity (2 marks):** - For dry grains, keep ambient **RH < 70 %** to prevent moisture migration. - Use of moisture meters and **aeration** during cool nights. 3. **Moisture content (2 marks):** - Should be at or below the **safe storage moisture**. - Wheat 12–13 %, paddy 13–14 %, maize 13–14 %, pulses 9–10 %. - Drying to safe moisture is the **first and most important step**. 4. **Gas composition (1 mark):** - Normal atmosphere: 21 % O₂ + 78 % N₂ + 0.04 % CO₂. - In **hermetic storage**, O₂ falls to < 2 % within days and CO₂ rises; insects die. --- **Q. (b)** Explain the **types of improved storage structures** used for food grains in India, with neat diagrams. Discuss their advantages over traditional storage. (7 marks) **Model Solution — Part (b):** 1. **Pusa Bin (3 marks):** - Designed by **IARI, New Delhi**. - Galvanised iron cylindrical bin of 1.5 to 15 tonne capacity. - Fitted with **bottom aeration duct** and **airtight lid**. - Loading and unloading through a **screw auger** at the top. - **Storage loss < 1 %** vs. 10–15 % in traditional bins. 2. **Hapur Kothi / Metal Silo (2 marks):** - Large cylindrical silos, capacity 50–5000 t, used by FCI. - Equipped with **temperature and moisture sensors**, **aeration fans**, and **level indicators**. 3. **Gobar Gas-linked Storage (1 mark):** - Hermetic silo connected to a biogas plant. - CO₂ from biogas displaces O₂ inside the silo → insects die. 4. **Advantages over traditional (1 mark):** - Lower losses, longer storage life, rodent-proof, weather-proof, suitable for scientific fumigation with **aluminium phosphide**. > **Suggested Sketch (verbal):** Cylinder with conical bottom, aeration duct at base, lid at top, outlet valve at side. --- ### PART B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **Q. (a)** What is **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)**? Discuss its **principle, gas combinations, and applications** for fresh produce. (7 marks) **Model Solution — Part (a):** 1. **Definition (1 mark):** - MAP is a preservation technique in which the **gaseous environment around a food** is altered to retard spoilage and extend shelf life, with the package being the primary controller. 2. **Principle (2 marks):** - Low O₂ + elevated CO₂ **reduce respiration** of fresh produce. - Inhibition of **ethylene-sensitive** commodities. - Suppression of **aerobic microbial growth** (especially *Pseudomonas*, moulds). 3. **Gas combinations (2 marks):** - Typical "**equilibrium modified atmosphere**" for strawberries: 5 % O₂ + 15 % CO₂ + 80 % N₂. - For tomato: 3–5 % O₂ + 0 % CO₂ (CO₂ damages tomato). - **Active MAP**: gases are flushed in; **Passive MAP**: product respiration modifies the headspace. 4. **Applications (2 marks):** - Fresh-cut salads, sliced apples (with **ascorbic acid + MAP**), meat, fish, bread. --- **Q. (b)** Compare **Cold Storage, CA Storage, and Hermetic Storage** with respect to principle, cost, commodities handled, and shelf-life achieved. (7 marks) **Model Solution — Part (b):** | Parameter | Cold Storage | CA Storage | Hermetic Storage | |-----------|-------------|------------|------------------| | **Principle** | Low T (0–4 °C) only | Low T + reduced O₂ + elevated CO₂ | Airtight; natural O₂ depletion by respiration | | **Cost** | Moderate | High (gas analysers, airtight room) | Low (plastic bags/silos) | | **Commodities** | Potato, onion, apple, dairy, meat | Apple, pear, kiwi, cabbage | Paddy, wheat, maize, pulses, cocoa | | **Shelf life** | 1–6 months | 4–8 months (apples) | 6–12 months (grains) | | **Key Limitation** | Chilling injury in tropical fruits | High capital + trained operator | Restricted to dry commodities | *(1.5 marks per filled row × 4 rows + 1 mark for conclusion = 7 marks.)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print and Pin!):** - ☐ The **five storage parameters**: T, RH, M (moisture), gas composition, time. - ☐ **Q₁₀ ≈ 2**: halving the temperature roughly doubles shelf life. - ☐ **Safe moisture** for cereals ≈ 12–14 % w.b. - ☐ **Critical water activity (aw)** for mould growth ≈ 0.70. - ☐ **Chilling injury** ≠ **Freezing injury**; tropical fruits are chilling-sensitive. - ☐ **CA vs MA vs Hermetic**: - CA = continuous gas control in engineered rooms. - MA = initial gas adjustment; product respiration drives composition. - Hermetic = airtight + no gas exchange; O₂ self-depletes. - ☐ **Pusa Bin** = IARI-designed improved bin; **PICS bag** = Purdue Improved Crop Storage. - ☐ **Respiration Quotient (RQ)**: RQ=1 → carbohydrate; RQ<1 → lipid; RQ>1 → organic acid. - ☐ **Cold chain integrity** matters: a break of even 2–4 hours at room T can halve the shelf life of perishables. - ☐ **KTU-defining examples** to memorise for the exam: - Apple → CA store - Mango → CA + MAP - Paddy/Wheat → Metal silo / Pusa bin - Pulses → Hermetic PICS bag - Potato → Cold store at 3–5 °C, dark - ☐ **Numericals you must practise**: Q₁₀-based shelf-life, refrigeration load, moisture content conversions (w.b. ↔ d.b.). --- > [!TIP] > **End-of-Topic Strategy for KTU Exam:** > When you get a 14-mark question, immediately allocate **7 marks × 2 sub-parts** mentally. For each sub-part, plan to write: > 1. **Definition (1–2 lines)** > 2. **Principle (1 paragraph)** > 3. **Diagram (if asked — 1 mark)** > 4. **Numerical/Example (1 worked-out problem)** > 5. **Conclusion or applications (2–3 lines)** > > This structure guarantees full marks because it matches the KTU mark-distribution pattern.

topics.title: Storage Characteristics of Different Fruits and Vegetables
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT614 - POST HARVEST HANDLING OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Storage Characteristics of Different Fruits and Vegetables

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Storage Characteristics of Fruits and Vegetables** refer to the inherent physiological, biochemical, and physical properties of horticultural produce that govern its post-harvest behavior, shelf-life, and response to various storage environments. These characteristics are predominantly dictated by the **respiratory pattern** (climacteric vs. non-climacteric), **ethylene biosynthesis rate**, **transpiration rate**, **chilling sensitivity threshold**, and the produce's **morphological-anatomical composition** (skin wax deposition, cuticle thickness, surface-to-volume ratio).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic forms the **physiological backbone** of post-harvest engineering, where students must correlate commodity classification with recommended storage parameters (temperature, relative humidity, atmosphere composition) to maximize marketable life.

### 1.1.1 Climacteric vs. Non-Climacteric Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Climacteric Fruits**: Produce exhibiting a **sharp, autocatalytic rise in respiration rate** (CO₂ evolution) coinciding with a burst of **ethylene (C₂H₄) production** at the onset of ripening. Once initiated, ripening proceeds independently of the parent plant. Examples: *Banana, Apple, Mango, Tomato, Avocado, Papaya, Guava*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Climacteric Fruits & Vegetables**: Produce displaying a **steady, gradual decline in respiration** after harvest with **no distinct ethylene climacteric peak**. Ripening is a parent-plant-dependent process and must occur on the plant for full flavor development. Examples: *Citrus, Grape, Pineapple, Pomegranate, Strawberry, Cherry, Cucumber, Bell Pepper, Carrot, Potato (tuber)*.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Living Engine" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy**: Imagine each harvested fruit/vegetable as a **tiny biological engine still running on battery power** after being disconnected from the tree (the "mother power plant").
> - **Climacteric fruits** are like a car that, even after parking, suddenly revs its engine (respiration burst) to burn the last fuel reserves to reach full maturity — this is the **climacteric peak**.
> - **Non-climacteric fruits** are like an electric scooter that simply decelerates smoothly and dies — no dramatic surge, just a slow fade.
> - **Ethylene** acts as the **"ignition key"** for climacteric fruits (a single molecule can trigger the whole ripening cascade), whereas for non-climacteric fruits, the ignition system is **disconnected at the factory**.

The **storage strategy** is therefore an engineering decision to either:
1. **Slow the engine** (refrigeration, CA storage), or
2. **Remove the ignition key** (ethylene scrubbing, 1-MCP treatment), or
3. **Modify the air-fuel mixture** (low O₂, high CO₂ modified atmosphere).

---

## 1.2 Key Physiological Parameters Governing Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Five Pillars of Storage Characterization** in Post-Harvest Technology:

| # | Parameter | Engineering Significance |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Respiration Rate (RR)** | Determines heat generation ($Q_{resp}$) and CO₂ accumulation in storage |
| 2 | **Ethylene Production Rate ($C_2H_4$)** | Triggers ripening/senescence; toxicity threshold is **< 1 ppm** for sensitive commodities |
| 3 | **Transpiration Rate** | Governs water loss → weight (shrinkage) and turgor loss → wilting |
| 4 | **Chilling Sensitivity** | Defines minimum safe storage temperature before **Chilling Injury (CI)** occurs |
| 5 | **Storage Life (Days/Weeks)** | Market-window-defining parameter; depends on the above four |

### 1.3 Respiration — The Central Bioenergetic Process

Respiration is the **aerobic oxidation** of carbohydrates, lipids, and organic acids to CO₂ and H₂O, releasing **free energy** (ATP) used to maintain cellular integrity post-detachment.

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \longrightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + Energy\;(\approx 2815\;kJ/mol)$$

The **Respiratory Quotient (RQ)** = moles CO₂ released / moles O₂ consumed:
- RQ = **1.0** → carbohydrate substrate
- RQ = **< 1.0** → lipid/fat substrate (e.g., avocado)
- RQ = **> 1.0** → anaerobic/organic acid respiration

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Storage Metrics (KTU-Highlight)**:
> - **Respiration Rate Unit**: mL CO₂ / kg·h (at standard temperature 20°C unless otherwise specified)
> - **Classification by Rate**: Very Low (< 5), Low (5–10), Moderate (10–20), High (20–40), Very High (40–60), Extremely High (> 60 mL CO₂/kg·h)
> - **Recommended RH**: 85–95 % for most fruits/vegetables (85–90 % for onions/garlic; 95–100 % for leafy greens)
> - **Freezing Point**: Most produce freezes between **-2.2 °C and -0.5 °C**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Six-Pillar Storage Model

The post-harvest storage behavior of any fruit or vegetable is governed by **six interlinked physiological and engineering principles**. A KTU examiner will expect students to demonstrate multi-pillar reasoning rather than isolated recall.

### Pillar I — Respiration Kinetics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Respiration Rate (R)** is the master kinetic variable. It follows an approximate **temperature-dependent exponential law** (Arrhenius-type), often expressed empirically using the **Q₁₀ rule** (Section 2.4).

**Respiratory Pattern Types:**

1. **Climacteric Pattern** (Sigmoidal: Lag → Pre-climacteric minimum → Climacteric peak → Post-climacteric decline)
2. **Non-Climacteric Pattern** (Monotonically declining curve)
3. **Pre-climacteric (At Harvest)** — low, steady rate
4. **Climacteric Peak** — sharply elevated RR with autocatalytic ethylene
5. **Post-climacteric (Senescence)** — declining RR but increasing cell wall degradation

### Pillar II — Ethylene Biosynthesis & Action

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethylene (C₂H₄)** is the **plant ripening hormone** — a simple olefin gas biosynthesized via the **Yang Cycle (S-adenosylmethionine → ACC → Ethylene)** catalyzed by **ACC synthase (ACS)** and **ACC oxidase (ACO)**.

**Climacteric ethylene production** can reach **10–100 μL C₂H₄/kg·h** at peak (e.g., apple, banana).
**Non-climacteric production** is typically **< 0.5 μL C₂H₄/kg·h** throughout.

**Sensitivity Categories:**
- **Low sensitivity**: Asparagus, cherry, citrus, fig, grape, pineapple, potato
- **Moderate sensitivity**: Banana, apple, peach, pear, plum
- **High sensitivity**: Avocado, cantaloupe, kiwifruit, persimmon
- **Extremely high sensitivity**: Carnations, gypsophila, some leafy ornamentals

### Pillar III — Transpiration & Water Loss

> [!NOTE]
> **Transpiration** = loss of water vapor from the produce surface to the surrounding air. The driving force is the **vapor pressure deficit (VPD)** between the produce interior and ambient air.

**Wilting Threshold**: Most produce shows visible wilting after **5–10 %** mass loss; leafy vegetables wilt at **3–5 %**.

**Fick's Law Application for Transpiration:**

$$ \frac{dm_{water}}{dt} = k_{t} \cdot A \cdot (P_{int} - P_{ext}) $$

where:
- $k_t$ = transpiration coefficient (depends on cuticle, wax, stomata)
- $A$ = effective surface area
- $P_{int}$ = internal vapor pressure (≈ saturation at tissue temperature)
- $P_{ext}$ = ambient vapor pressure

### Pillar IV — Chilling Injury (CI)

> [!WARNING]
> **Chilling Injury** is a **low-temperature (above freezing, usually 0–13 °C) physiological disorder** in chilling-sensitive produce, distinct from freezing injury. Symptoms: pitting, surface lesions, internal browning, failure to ripen, off-flavors, increased decay susceptibility.

**Examples of Chilling-Sensitive Commodities:**
- Tropical: Banana (storage < 13 °C injures), mango, papaya, pineapple, avocado
- Subtropical: Citrus, cucumber, eggplant, sweet potato
- **NOT chilling-sensitive** (can store at 0 °C): Apple, pear, grape, kiwifruit, cabbage, carrot

### Pillar V — Compositional Respiration Substrate

The **RQ** informs us which substrate is being respired:
- Pure carbohydrate → RQ = 1.0
- Pure lipid → RQ ≈ 0.7
- Pure organic acid (malic/citric) → RQ > 1.33

This has **engineering implications**: lipid-rich produce (avocado, olive) generate **more heat per unit O₂ consumed** than carbohydrate-rich produce (apple, banana).

### Pillar VI — Structural Defense Mechanisms

Surface features affecting storage:
- **Cuticle & Wax Layer**: Primary barrier to water and gas exchange
- **Stomata vs. Lenticels**: Gaseous exchange ports
- **Skin Thickness**: Mechanical protection
- **Periderm**: Corky layer in tubers (potato) — provides wound healing

---

## 2.2 Classification of Fruits & Vegetables by Storage Requirements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Examiners frequently ask for **"Classify the following into climacteric/non-climacteric with their storage requirements"** — a high-yield 7–14 mark question.

| Commodity | Class | Storage Temp (°C) | RH (%) | Approx. Storage Life | Chilling Sensitive? |
|-----------|-------|-------------------|--------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Apple** | Climacteric | -1 to 4 | 90–95 | 2–8 months | No |
| **Banana** | Climacteric | 13–15 | 85–95 | 1–4 weeks | **Yes** |
| **Mango** | Climacteric | 10–13 | 85–90 | 2–4 weeks | **Yes** |
| **Papaya** | Climacteric | 10–13 | 85–90 | 1–3 weeks | **Yes** |
| **Avocado** | Climacteric | 5–13 | 85–90 | 2–4 weeks | **Yes** |
| **Tomato** | Climacteric | 12–15 (ripe); 8–10 (mature green) | 85–90 | 1–3 weeks | **Yes** |
| **Guava** | Climacteric | 5–10 | 90–95 | 2–3 weeks | Mildly |
| **Citrus (Orange, Lemon)** | Non-Climacteric | 3–9 | 85–90 | 4–8 weeks | No (mild) |
| **Grape** | Non-Climacteric | -0.5 to 0 | 90–95 | 1–6 months | No |
| **Pineapple** | Non-Climacteric | 10–13 | 85–90 | 2–4 weeks | **Yes** |
| **Strawberry** | Non-Climacteric | 0 | 90–95 | 5–7 days | No |
| **Pomegranate** | Non-Climacteric | 5–7 | 90–95 | 2–3 months | No |
| **Carrot** | Non-Climacteric | 0 | 98–100 | 4–6 months | No |
| **Potato** | Non-Climacteric (tuber) | 4–10 | 90–95 | 4–9 months | No (sweetening) |
| **Onion** | Non-Climacteric | 0 | 65–75 | 6–9 months | No |
| **Cabbage** | Non-Climacteric | 0 | 95–100 | 3–6 months | No |
| **Lettuce** | Non-Climacteric | 0 | 95–100 | 2–3 weeks | No |
| **Cucumber** | Non-Climacteric | 10–13 | 90–95 | 1–2 weeks | **Yes** |
| **Bell Pepper** | Non-Climacteric | 7–10 | 90–95 | 2–3 weeks | **Yes** |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all major equations** KTU examiners test in this topic. Memorize, derive, and apply.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $RR = \frac{V_{CO_2}}{m \cdot t}$ | Respiration Rate (definition) | mL CO₂/kg·h |
| 2 | $RQ = \frac{V_{CO_2}}{V_{O_2}}$ | Respiratory Quotient (substrate indicator) | Dimensionless; 0.7–1.33 |
| 3 | $Q_{resp} = RR \times 10.7$ | Respiration Heat Generation (carbohydrate substrate, RR in mL CO₂/kg·h) | J/kg·s (≈ 10.7 J per mL CO₂/h) |
| 4 | $Q_{resp} = RR \times 12.5$ | Respiration Heat (lipid substrate) | J/kg·s |
| 5 | $Q_{10} = \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Temperature Coefficient of Respiration | Dimensionless; typically 2.0–2.5 for produce |
| 6 | $R_{T_2} = R_{T_1} \times Q_{10}^{\frac{T_2 - T_1}{10}}$ | Respiration rate at new temperature | mL CO₂/kg·h |
| 7 | $\% WL = \frac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100$ | Percentage Weight (Water) Loss | Dimensionless % |
| 8 | $t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k_{d}}$ | Half-life of stored produce (1st order decay model) | Days |
| 9 | $VPD = P_{sat}(T_{prod}) - P_{amb}$ | Vapor Pressure Deficit (drives transpiration) | kPa or mmHg |
| 10 | $CA_{O_2\;req} = 100 - \frac{RR \times 100}{k_{diff}}$ | O₂ % for Controlled Atmosphere | % O₂ |
| 11 | $m_{c2h4} = E \times m \times t$ | Ethylene mass accumulated (E = production rate) | μL or ppm |
| 12 | $E_a = \frac{2.303 \cdot R \cdot T_1 \cdot T_2}{T_2 - T_1} \log\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$ | Arrhenius activation energy (advanced) | kJ/mol |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP**: The constants **10.7 J/kg·s per mL CO₂/h** (carbohydrate) and **12.5 J/kg·s per mL CO₂/h** (lipid) are **numerical conversions** derived from enthalpy of combustion per mole CO₂. These are the most-tested constants in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.4 Worked Engineering Interpretation of Q₁₀

The **Q₁₀ (Van't Hoff coefficient)** is a critical post-harvest engineering parameter stating that for every **10 °C rise** in temperature, the **respiration rate multiplies by Q₁₀** (typically 2.0–2.5 for horticultural produce).

**Engineering Utility**: Q₁₀ allows prediction of shelf-life at any storage temperature once a baseline is known.

$$ \text{Storage Life} \propto \frac{1}{RR} \propto \frac{1}{Q_{10}^{\Delta T / 10}} $$

> **Example Real-World Use**: If a fruit has a shelf-life of 14 days at 10 °C with Q₁₀ = 2.5, the expected shelf-life at 0 °C would be:
> $14 \times 2.5^{(10/10)} = 14 \times 2.5 = 35$ days.

---
