<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. Define Q₁₀ value. State its significance in post-harvest technology.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Q₁₀ is the temperature coefficient that expresses the factor by which the rate of a biochemical reaction changes for every 10 °C rise in temperature. **Significance:** In post-harvest technology, Q₁₀ is used to predict shelf-life of perishable commodities under varying storage temperatures. A high Q₁₀ (2–3 for most fresh produce) means the commodity is highly sensitive to temperature abuse. By lowering the storage temperature by 10 °C, the rate of respiration and senescence can be **reduced by 2 to 3 times**, which is the scientific basis of **cold chain logistics**. It is used for designing refrigerated transport, cold storage, and modified atmosphere packaging systems.

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between psychrophiles, mesophiles, and thermophiles with examples.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Optimum Growth Temp. | Example | Relevance to Post-Harvest |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Psychrophiles** | −5 to 20 °C | *Pseudomonas* spp. | Spoilage of chilled fish and meat |
| **Mesophiles** | 20 to 45 °C | *Salmonella*, *E. coli*, *Lactobacillus* | Most pathogens and common spoilage organisms |
| **Thermophiles** | 45 to 80 °C | *Bacillus stearothermophilus* | Survive pasteurization; relevant in canned foods |

Most post-harvest spoilage organisms of fruits and vegetables stored at ambient temperatures in Kerala are **mesophiles**, while chilled-storage spoilage is dominated by **psychrotrophs**.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Temperature Effects in Post-Harvest Systems**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Q₁₀ concept with its mathematical derivation. How is it used to estimate the shelf-life of perishables? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the effect of temperature on (i) respiration rate, (ii) transpiration, and (iii) microbial growth in harvested food materials. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Q₁₀ Concept and Derivation — 7 marks:**

The Q₁₀ value is defined as the ratio of reaction rates at two temperatures separated by 10 °C:

$$Q_{10} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}$$

For temperature differences other than 10 °C, the generalized form is:

$$Q_{10} = \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

Using the Arrhenius equation $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$:

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT_2}}{A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT_1}} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Shelf-life estimation (3 marks within this section):**
Since shelf-life is inversely proportional to the rate of deterioration:

$$SL_2 = \frac{SL_1}{Q_{10}^{\Delta T/10}}$$

**Example:** Tomato shelf-life at 25 °C = 7 days; $Q_{10}$ = 2. What is the shelf-life at 5 °C?

$$SL_2 = \frac{7}{2^{(25-5)/10}} = \frac{7}{2^2} = \frac{7}{4} = 1.75 \text{ days at 25 °C; hence 7 days at 5 °C}$$

This 4× extension is the **mathematical justification for the cold chain**.

---

**(b) Temperature Effects on Respiration, Transpiration, and Microbes — 7 marks:**

**(i) Respiration Rate (2 marks):**
Respiration follows the Arrhenius kinetics. As temperature rises, the rate doubles or triples per 10 °C increase. Heat of respiration ($q_r$) increases proportionally. For example, strawberry has $q_r ≈ 10$ mW/kg at 0 °C but ≈ 80 mW/kg at 25 °C. This heat must be removed by refrigeration to prevent accelerated senescence.

**(ii) Transpiration (2.5 marks):**
Transpiration is the loss of water vapor from fresh produce. It increases with temperature due to:
- Higher vapor pressure deficit (VPD) between produce and ambient air
- Increased permeability of cell membranes
- Higher metabolic activity

Consequences: shrivelling, weight loss (1% loss = visibly poor quality), texture loss. Controlled by maintaining **85–95% RH** and **low temperature**.

**(iii) Microbial Growth (2.5 marks):**
Microbial growth rate follows the typical bacterial growth curve (lag → log → stationary → death). Each microbial group has a characteristic optimum:
- Psychrophiles: −5 to 20 °C
- Mesophiles: 20 to 45 °C
- Thermophiles: 45 to 80 °C

Higher temperatures shorten the lag phase, accelerate the log phase, and increase spoilage rate. Low temperature storage at 0–4 °C keeps mesophilic pathogens dormant.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Insect Infestation and Microbial Spoilage**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Classify storage insects with suitable examples. Explain the life cycle of *Sitophilus oryzae* (rice weevil) and its role in post-harvest losses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is water activity (a_w)? Explain its role in microbial spoilage. Discuss the threshold a_w values for bacteria, yeasts, and moulds with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification and Life Cycle of *Sitophilus oryzae* — 7 marks:**

**Classification of Storage Insects (3 marks):**

| Type | Feeding Behavior | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Primary (Internal) | Bore into intact grains | *Sitophilus oryzae* (rice weevil), *Sitotroga cerealella* |
| Secondary (External) | Feed on broken grains/flour | *Tribolium castaneum* (red flour beetle) |
| Tertiary | Predators of other insects | *Xylocoris flavipes* |

**Life Cycle of *Sitophilus oryzae* (3 marks):**
1. **Egg stage (3–4 days):** Female lays 300–500 eggs, one per grain, sealing the hole with gelatinous secretion.
2. **Larval stage (3–4 weeks):** Grub feeds inside the kernel, hollowing it from within.
3. **Pupal stage (5–7 days):** Pupa forms inside the grain.
4. **Adult stage (2–3 months lifespan):** Bores out of the grain, mates, and the cycle repeats.

**Role in Post-Harvest Losses (1 mark):**
- Quantitative: 10–30% weight loss in untreated rice.
- Qualitative: Contamination with insect fragments, faeces, and uric acid.
- Secondary effects: Insect respiration raises grain temperature and moisture, creating **hot spots** that encourage mould growth and mycotoxin production.

---

**(b) Water Activity and Microbial Spoilage — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Water activity ($a_w$) is the ratio of the partial vapor pressure of water in a food ($p$) to the vapor pressure of pure water ($p_0$) at the same temperature:

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$

It represents the *available* water for microbial and enzymatic activity, not the total water content.

**Threshold Values (3 marks):**

| Microorganism | Minimum a_w | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Most spoilage bacteria | 0.86 | *Pseudomonas*, *Clostridium* |
| Most yeasts | 0.70 | *Saccharomyces*, *Candida* |
| Most moulds | 0.70 | *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium* |
| Xerophilic moulds | 0.60–0.70 | *Eurotium* (sugar-tolerant) |
| Halophilic bacteria | 0.75 | *Halobacterium* (salt-loving) |

**Role in Microbial Spoilage (2 marks):**
- Food with $a_w \geq 0.86$: supports bacterial spoilage (e.g., fresh meat, milk).
- Food with $a_w$ 0.70–0.86: supports only yeasts and moulds (e.g., jam, dried fruits).
- Food with $a_w < 0.60$: **microbiologically stable** at room temperature (e.g., biscuits, milk powder).

**Control Strategy:**
Reducing $a_w$ by drying, adding salt/sugar, or using humectants is the **most effective method of food preservation** — the basis of technologies like **dried fruits, jerky, and intermediate moisture foods (IMF)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Q₁₀ with Q₂₀:** Q₂₀ = $(Q_{10})^2$. Students often mix these up.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in the Arrhenius equation. T must always be in Kelvin.
> 3. **Confusing water content (%) with water activity (a_w):** These are entirely different concepts. A dry food (low % water) can still have high $a_w$ if the water is loosely bound.
> 4. **Mixing up internal and external feeders** in the insect classification — a very common KTU deduction.
> 5. **Writing Arrhenius without R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** — losing the unit mark.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- ✅ **Q₁₀ is dimensionless**, typically **2–3** for fresh produce; it is the **single most important KTU concept** in temperature effects.
- ✅ **Arrhenius equation** is the foundation of all temperature-dependent spoilage kinetics; remember $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ and $T$ in **Kelvin**.
- ✅ **Shelf-life is inversely proportional to reaction rate**: $SL_2 = SL_1 / Q_{10}^{\Delta T/10}$.
- ✅ **Insects are poikilothermic** — their activity is directly temperature-dependent; optimum reproduction at **25–32 °C**.
- ✅ **Storage insects** are classified as **primary (internal), secondary (external), and tertiary (predators)** — a high-yield KTU question.
- ✅ **Heat of respiration** of fresh produce must be removed by refrigeration; it is **non-trivial** for design of cold rooms.
- ✅ **Water activity (a_w)** is more critical than water content for microbial safety; thresholds: **bacteria ≥ 0.86**, **yeasts/moulds ≥ 0.70**.
- ✅ **Microbial groups by temperature**: **Psychrophiles (−5 to 20 °C), Mesophiles (20–45 °C), Thermophiles (45–80 °C)**.
- ✅ **Chilling injury** is a critical concept for tropical fruits (banana, mango) — storing them below 13 °C causes physiological damage.
- ✅ **Hot spots** in grain bulk storage are caused by insect + microbial respiration and lead to spontaneous heating — a major cause of warehouse fires in stored grains.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fungi, Virus, Bacteria etc.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT614 - POST HARVEST HANDLING OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fungi, Virus, Bacteria etc.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Post-Harvest Biological Threat Landscape

Post-harvest deterioration of food materials is caused by a triad of biological agents acting individually or synergistically. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, these agents are classified into:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** *Post-harvest biological spoilage agents* are living organisms—principally **fungi (molds and yeasts)**, **bacteria**, and **viruses**—that invade, colonize, and metabolize harvested food commodities, leading to qualitative and quantitative losses in nutritional value, marketability, and safety.

### 1.2 Microbial Spoilage Organisms — Formal Definitions

**Fungi (Molds & Yeasts):**
Fungi are eukaryotic, heterotrophic microorganisms that absorb nutrients through their cell wall. In post-harvest contexts, they appear as two morphological forms:
- **Molds:** Multicellular, filamentous (hyphal) growth, visible as fuzzy colonies.
- **Yeasts:** Unicellular, spherical/oval, reproduce mainly by budding.

**Bacteria:**
Prokaryotic, unicellular microorganisms (typical size 0.5–5 µm) that lack a true nucleus and reproduce by binary fission. They are the most aggressive spoilage agents in high-moisture foods.

**Viruses:**
Acellular, obligate intracellular parasites consisting of nucleic acid (DNA or RNA) enclosed in a protein coat (capsid). They cannot reproduce outside a living host cell.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Invisible Invaders"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine your harvested grain/fruit as a **fortified city** full of nutrients. The moment harvest breaks the natural protective skin (epidermis), three types of invaders attack:
> 1. **Bacteria = SWAT teams** (fast, microscopic, multiply every 20 minutes in warm/moist conditions).
> 2. **Fungi = Slow siege armies** (molds creep in and colonize via hyphae; yeasts ferment sugars quietly).
> 3. **Viruses = Spies** (they hijack the food's own cells/enzymes only when a host is present, mainly relevant in animal foods and fresh produce).
> 
> Storage conditions—**temperature, humidity, water activity, and pH**—decide which invader wins.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics for the KTU Exam

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Bacterial size | **0.5 – 5 µm** |
| Fungal hyphae diameter | **2 – 10 µm** |
| Yeast cell size | **3 – 8 µm** |
| Virus size | **20 – 300 nm** |
| Optimal water activity (aw) for bacteria | **≥ 0.91** |
| Optimal aw for yeasts | **≥ 0.88** |
| Optimal aw for molds | **≥ 0.70** (xerotolerant down to 0.61) |
| Generation time of bacteria | **20 min – 24 hr** (species-dependent) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Hierarchy of Spoilage Agents

### A. Fungi (Kingdom Fungi)

1. **Molds (Filamentous Fungi)**
   - *Aspergillus* spp. (e.g., *A. flavus* → aflatoxin in groundnut, maize)
   - *Penicillium* spp. (e.g., *P. expansum* → blue mold of apple, patulin toxin)
   - *Fusarium* spp. (Fusarium rot of cereals; fumonisins)
   - *Rhizopus*, *Mucor*, *Botrytis* (soft fruits, vegetables)
   - *Alternaria* (leafy vegetables, tomatoes)

2. **Yeasts**
   - *Saccharomyces* spp. (fermentation, fruit spoilage)
   - *Candida*, *Pichia*, *Zygosaccharomyces* (osmotolerant, spoil honey, jams, juices)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** *Aspergillus niger* and *Penicillium notatum* are the **most cited** spoilage molds in KTU exam questions. *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* is the standard yeast example.

### B. Bacteria

| Group | Gram | Oxygen Need | Typical Food Spoilage Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| *Pseudomonas* spp. | – | Aerobic | Psychrotrophic; spoil meat, fish, milk at chill temp |
| *Erwinia* spp. | – | Facultative anaerobe | Soft rot of fruits/vegetables |
| *Lactic Acid Bacteria* (LAB) | + | Facultative anaerobe | Souring of milk, meat, vegetables |
| *Clostridium* spp. | + | Obligate anaerobe | Butyric spoilage, canned food botulism risk |
| *Bacillus* spp. | + | Aerobic/Facultative | Flat sour spoilage in canned foods |
| *Enterobacteriaceae* | – | Facultative anaerobe | General spoilage, gas formation |

### C. Viruses

- **Foodborne viruses are NOT typical spoilage organisms** (since they don't multiply in food).
- They are important in **food safety** because they persist in foods and infect consumers.
- Examples: **Norovirus** (shellfish, salads, berries), **Hepatitis A virus** (shellfish, fresh produce), **Rotavirus** (water/vegetables).
- They are obligate parasites → **cannot replicate in the food itself**, only in the human host.

## 2.2 Factors Governing Microbial Spoilage (Intrinsic & Extrinsic)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept:** Spoilage = f(intrinsic × extrinsic × processing × implicit factors)

- **Intrinsic:** pH, water activity (aw), redox potential, nutrients, antimicrobial constituents.
- **Extrinsic:** Storage temperature, relative humidity, atmosphere composition.
- **Implicit:** Synergistic microbial interactions (succession).
- **Processing:** Blanching, salting, irradiation effects.

### 2.3 Water Activity (aw) — The Master Spoilage Variable

$$a_w = \frac{P}{P_0} = \frac{ERH \, (\%)}{100}$$

Where:
- $P$ = partial vapor pressure of water in food
- $P_0$ = vapor pressure of pure water
- $ERH$ = Equilibrium Relative Humidity (%) of storage atmosphere

### 2.4 Microbial Growth Rate (Logistic Model)

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = \mu N \left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right)$$

Where:
- $N$ = microbial population (CFU/g)
- $\mu$ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹)
- $K$ = carrying capacity
- $t$ = time (h)

### 2.5 Thermal Death Kinetics (D & Z value)

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{D_T}$$

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{Z}$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Key Boundary / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Water activity | $a_w = P/P_0 = ERH/100$ | Dimensionless, range **0 to 1** |
| Bacterial growth | $N = N_0 \cdot 2^{n}$ | $n$ = number of generations |
| Generation time | $g = t/n$ | Hours or minutes |
| D-value (decimal reduction time) | $D_T$ | Minutes at temperature $T$ |
| Z-value (thermal resistance) | $Z$ | °C (typical for bacteria: **5–10 °C**) |
| Log reduction | $\log(N_0/N)$ | Sterility: 12-log for canned food |
| F-value (sterilization value) | $F = t \cdot 10^{(T_{ref}-T)/Z}$ | Minutes (reference 121.1 °C, Z=10) |
| Specific growth rate | $\mu = \ln(N/N_0)/t$ | h⁻¹ |
| Aerobic Plate Count (APC) | Reported as $\log_{10}$ CFU/g | $N$ = colonies counted × dilution factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid Mark Loss:** Never confuse **aw (water activity)** with **moisture content (m.c.)**. They are related but NOT equal. aw is the thermodynamic property; m.c. is the mass of water per mass of food.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bacterial Population After n Generations

**Starting point:** One bacterial cell divides into 2, then 4, then 8... This is geometric progression.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{After 1st division: } & N_1 = N_0 \times 2 \\
\text{After 2nd division: } & N_2 = N_0 \times 2^2 \\
\text{After 3rd division: } & N_3 = N_0 \times 2^3 \\
\vdots \\
\text{After n generations: } & N_n = N_0 \times 2^n
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $N_0$ = initial cell count, $N_n$ = final count, $n$ = number of generations.

**Taking log base 2:**

$$
n = \log_2 \left( \frac{N_n}{N_0} \right) = \frac{\log_{10}(N_n/N_0)}{\log_{10} 2}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A food sample initially has $N_0 = 10^2$ CFU/g bacteria. After 6 hours at optimum temperature, count rises to $N = 10^8$ CFU/g. Find number of generations and generation time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{\log_{10}(10^8 / 10^2)}{\log_{10} 2} \\
  &= \frac{8 - 2}{0.3010} \\
  &= \frac{6}{0.3010} \\
  &\approx 19.93 \text{ generations}
\end{aligned}
$$

Generation time:

$$
g = \frac{t}{n} = \frac{6 \text{ h}}{19.93} \approx 0.301 \text{ h} \approx 18.1 \text{ min}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Water Activity from Raoult's Law (Ideal Solutions)

For an ideal solution, the partial vapor pressure of water is proportional to its mole fraction:

$$
P = x_w \cdot P_0
$$

Dividing by $P_0$:

$$
\frac{P}{P_0} = x_w \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_w = x_w
$$

For a real (non-ideal) food system, we introduce an activity coefficient $\gamma$:

$$
a_w = \gamma \cdot x_w
$$

Where $x_w$ = mole fraction of water in the food, $\gamma$ = activity coefficient.

## 3.3 Derivation: D-value and Z-value Relationship

The D-value at a reference temperature $T_{ref}$ is related to D-value at any other temperature $T$ by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(D_T) &= \log_{10}(D_{ref}) - \frac{T - T_{ref}}{Z} \\
\Rightarrow \log_{10}\left(\frac{D_T}{D_{ref}}\right) &= -\frac{T - T_{ref}}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows the D-value decreases (heat killing becomes faster) as $T$ increases above $T_{ref}$.

**Worked Example:**
For *Clostridium botulinum*, $D_{121.1°C} = 0.21$ min, $Z = 10$ °C. Find D at 111.1 °C.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}\left(\frac{D_{111.1}}{0.21}\right) &= -\frac{111.1 - 121.1}{10} = -\frac{-10}{10} = 1 \\
\Rightarrow \frac{D_{111.1}}{0.21} &= 10^1 = 10 \\
\Rightarrow D_{111.1} &= 2.1 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: At a 10 °C lower temperature, it takes **10 times longer** to achieve the same log reduction.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Microbial Susceptibility vs. Food Type

| Food Category | pH Range | aw Range | Dominant Spoilage Group | Why |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fresh meat, fish | 5.5 – 6.8 | 0.98 – 0.99 | Bacteria (*Pseudomonas*) | High pH, high aw |
| Fresh fruits | 3.0 – 5.0 | 0.97 – 0.99 | Molds + Yeasts | Low pH inhibits bacteria |
| Vegetables | 5.0 – 6.5 | 0.97 – 0.99 | Bacteria (*Erwinia*) + Molds | Moderate pH |
| Cereals/grains (dried) | 5.5 – 6.5 | 0.60 – 0.70 | Xerophilic molds (*Aspergillus*) | Low aw selects fungi |
| Honey, jams | 3.5 – 5.0 | 0.55 – 0.75 | Osmophilic yeasts | Sugar binds water → low aw |
| Milk | 6.5 – 6.8 | 0.99 | Bacteria (LAB, *Pseudomonas*) | High aw, near neutral pH |
| Canned food (low acid) | 5.0 – 7.0 | 0.95+ | *Clostridium* (anaerobic) | Anaerobic environment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic (KTU):** *"Low pH favors Fungi, High pH favors Bacteria."* The mold *Aspergillus* can tolerate aw as low as **0.61**—the lowest of common food spoilage organisms.

## 3.5 Predictive Microbiology — Growth Rate Calculation

For many spoilage bacteria, the modified Ratkowsky (square root) model describes growth rate vs. temperature:

$$
\sqrt{\mu} = b \cdot (T - T_{min})
$$

Where:
- $b$ = regression coefficient (°C⁻¹·h⁻½)
- $T_{min}$ = theoretical minimum growth temperature (°C)
- $T$ = storage temperature (°C)

**Rearranging for $\mu$:**

$$
\mu = b^2 \cdot (T - T_{min})^2
$$

This equation explains why even a 1–2 °C drop in refrigeration temperature drastically slows spoilage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Block Diagram of Post-Harvest Spoilage Agents

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Spoilage Agents of Food] --> A[Fungi]
    ROOT --> B[Bacteria]
    ROOT --> C[Viruses]
    ROOT --> D[Other Agents]
    D --> D1[Insects]
    D --> D2[Rodents]
    D --> D3[Mites]
    A --> A1[Molds Filamentous]
    A --> A2[Yeasts Unicellular]
    A1 --> A1A[Aspergillus]
    A1 --> A1B[Penicillium]
    A1 --> A1C[Fusarium]
    A1 --> A1D[Rhizopus Mucor]
    A2 --> A2A[Saccharomyces]
    A2 --> A2B[Candida]
    A2 --> A2C[Zygosaccharomyces]
    B --> B1[Gram Positive]
    B --> B2[Gram Negative]
    B1 --> B1A[Bacillus]
    B1 --> B1B[Clostridium]
    B1 --> B1C[Lactic Acid Bacteria]
    B2 --> B2A[Pseudomonas]
    B2 --> B2B[Erwinia]
    B2 --> B2C[Enterobacteriaceae]
    C --> C1[Norovirus]
    C --> C2[Hepatitis A]
    C --> C3[Rotavirus]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Mechanism of Microbial Spoilage

```mermaid
graph LR
    FOOD[Harvested Food] --> INJ[Injury to Protective Skin Peel Cuticle]
    INJ --> EXP[Exposure to Internal Nutrients]
    EXP --> CON[Contact with Air Water Microbes]
    CON --> ATT[Microbial Attachment Colonization]
    ATT --> ENZ[Enzyme Secretion]
    ENZ --> HYD[Hydrolysis of Proteins Lipids Carbohydrates]
    HYD --> OFF[Off Odour Off Flavour]
    HYD --> TEX[Texture Softening Discoloration]
    HYD --> TOX[Mycotoxin Production in Some Cases]
    OFF --> SPOIL[Final Spoilage Rejection]
    TEX --> SPOIL
    TOX --> SPOIL
```

## 4.3 Decision Block: Selecting Dominant Spoilage Group

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q{Food pH below 4.5?} -->|Yes| Y[Fungi dominate - Molds and Yeasts]
    Q -->|No| R
    R{Food aw below 0.86?} -->|Yes| M[Xerotolerant Molds Only]
    R -->|No| S
    S{Refrigerated Storage?} -->|Yes| P[Psychrotrophic Bacteria Pseudomonas]
    S -->|No| T
    T{Canned Sealed Anaerobic?} -->|Yes| C[Clostridium Anaerobic Sporeformers]
    T -->|No| G[Bacteria General Gram negative and LAB]
```

## 4.4 Lifecycle Block: Fungal Spoilage in Stored Grain

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Spore in Air or Soil] --> S2[Lands on Grain Surface]
    S2 --> S3[Germination Requires aw above 0.70]
    S3 --> S4[Hyphal Growth Penetration of Seed Coat]
    S4 --> S5[Secretion of Cellulase Pectinase Protease]
    S5 --> S6[Visible Mold Colony on Grain]
    S6 --> S7[Mycotoxin Production e.g. Aflatoxin]
    S7 --> S8[New Sporulation and Spread]
    S8 --> S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** These block diagrams are designed for quick recall in descriptive answers. In your answer sheet, redraw a simplified version of these flows to score full marks on architecture-based questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between molds and yeasts. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Molds | Yeasts |
|---|---|---|
| Cellularity | Multicellular (filamentous) | Unicellular |
| Morphology | Hyphae + mycelium + spores | Oval / spherical cells |
| Reproduction | Sexual and asexual spores | Budding (some fission) |
| Visibility | Visible to naked eye (fuzzy colonies) | Colonies are pasty, smaller |
| Examples | *Aspergillus niger*, *Penicillium roqueforti* | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, *Candida utilis* |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Define water activity (aw). Why is it critical in controlling microbial spoilage?**

**Model Answer:**

Water activity (aw) is defined as the ratio of the partial vapor pressure of water in a food ($P$) to the vapor pressure of pure water ($P_0$) at the same temperature:

$$a_w = \frac{P}{P_0} = \frac{ERH \, (\%)}{100}$$

It is a **dimensionless** quantity ranging from 0 (no free water) to 1 (pure water).

**Criticality:** Different microorganisms have minimum aw thresholds for growth. Bacteria typically need aw ≥ 0.91, yeasts ≥ 0.88, and most molds ≥ 0.70. By lowering aw (through drying, salting, sugaring), we selectively inhibit groups of spoilage organisms. This is the *principle behind preservation by drying, salting, and osmotic concentration.*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
**(a)** Classify the major spoilage bacteria of post-harvest foods. Discuss their Gram reaction, oxygen requirement, and the type of food they spoil. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the factors affecting microbial growth in stored foods. Discuss the role of water activity and pH. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification of Major Spoilage Bacteria:**

| Bacterium | Gram | Oxygen | Food Spoiled | Defect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| *Pseudomonas fluorescens* | – | Aerobic | Meat, fish, milk at 4 °C | Sliminess, off-odor |
| *Erwinia carotovora* | – | Facultative anaerobe | Potato, carrot, tomato | Soft rot |
| *Lactobacillus* spp. | + | Facultative anaerobe | Milk, meat, fermented foods | Souring, gas |
| *Clostridium botulinum* | + | Obligate anaerobe | Canned low-acid food | Botulism toxin |
| *Clostridium perfringens* | + | Obligate anaerobe | Cooked meat, vacuum-packed | Gas, putrid smell |
| *Bacillus subtilis* | + | Aerobic | Bread, canned food | Flat sour spoilage |
| *Enterobacter* spp. | – | Facultative anaerobe | Fresh produce, dairy | Gas, off-flavor |

**Conclusion:** Gram-negative aerobic rods (*Pseudomonas*) dominate spoilage of fresh, high-pH foods at refrigeration temperatures, while Gram-positive sporeformers (*Bacillus*, *Clostridium*) dominate in heat-processed and canned foods.

**(b) Factors Affecting Microbial Growth — Water Activity and pH:**

The major intrinsic factors are:

1. **Water Activity (aw):** 
   - Bacteria need aw ≥ 0.91 (except *Staphylococcus aureus* at 0.86).
   - Yeasts: aw ≥ 0.88.
   - Molds: aw ≥ 0.70 (xerophilic down to 0.61).
   - **Strategy:** Reducing aw by drying, salting, sugar addition (jams, honey) selectively inhibits bacterial spoilage.

2. **pH:**
   - **Low pH (< 4.5) foods** (fruits, pickles) — fungi dominate; bacteria inhibited.
   - **High pH (> 5.5) foods** (meat, fish, milk) — bacteria dominate.
   - pH adjustment (acidification) is a preservation method.

3. **Other Factors:** Temperature, redox potential (Eh), nutrient availability, presence of natural antimicrobials, microbial competition.

**Conclusion:** By manipulating aw and pH, the food industry controls which group of organisms will dominate and applies appropriate preservation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Describe the morphological and physiological characteristics of spoilage fungi. List the major spoilage fungi of stored grains and fresh fruits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are viruses? Discuss the role of viruses in food safety with examples of common foodborne viral pathogens. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Spoilage Fungi — Characteristics and Examples:**

**Morphology:**
- Fungi are eukaryotic, spore-bearing, non-photosynthetic organisms.
- **Molds** consist of thread-like hyphae forming a mycelium; reproduce by spores (conidia, sporangiospores, ascospores).
- **Yeasts** are unicellular, oval cells reproducing by budding or fission.

**Physiology:**
- Heterotrophic — secrete extracellular enzymes (cellulase, pectinase, amylase, protease, lipase) to hydrolyze complex food polymers.
- Grow over wide pH range (1.5 – 11), most grow between pH 4.5 – 6.5.
- Tolerate low aw (down to 0.61 for xerophiles).
- Aerobic to facultatively anaerobic; optimal growth 20–30 °C, but some psychrotrophs grow at refrigeration temperatures.

**Major Spoilage Fungi:**

| Fungus | Food Substrate | Defect / Toxin |
|---|---|---|
| *Aspergillus flavus* | Groundnut, maize | Aflatoxin B1 (carcinogenic) |
| *Aspergillus niger* | Onions, grapes, mango | Black mold, oxalic acid |
| *Penicillium expansum* | Apple, pear | Blue mold, patulin toxin |
| *Fusarium* spp. | Wheat, maize | Fusarium rot, fumonisins |
| *Rhizopus stolonifer* | Stone fruits, bread | Black bread mold, soft rot |
| *Botrytis cinerea* | Grape, strawberry | Gray mold |
| *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* | Juices, syrups | Fermentation, gas |

**(b) Viruses in Food Safety:**

**Definition:** Viruses are acellular, obligate intracellular parasites consisting of a nucleic acid core (DNA or RNA) enclosed in a protein capsid. They lack metabolism and cannot reproduce outside a host cell.

**Important Point:** Viruses do NOT multiply in food. They contaminate food from infected handlers, water, or sewage and then infect the consumer.

**Common Foodborne Viruses:**

| Virus | Genome | Source / Vehicle | Disease |
|---|---|---|---|
| Norovirus (Norwalk) | ssRNA | Shellfish, salads, water, berries | Acute gastroenteritis |
| Hepatitis A | ssRNA | Shellfish, fresh produce, water | Infectious hepatitis |
| Rotavirus | dsRNA | Water, raw vegetables | Childhood diarrhea |
| Adenovirus | dsDNA | Water, shellfish | Respiratory + GI illness |

**Control:** Personal hygiene of food handlers, sewage control, washing of fresh produce, depuration of shellfish, and adequate cooking (viruses are heat-sensitive above 70 °C for 2 min).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (Mark Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Confusing aw with moisture content (m.c.).** aw is a thermodynamic property of bound/unbound water; m.c. is a mass ratio. They are not the same.
> 2. **Stating "viruses spoil food."** This is **incorrect**. Viruses do NOT multiply in food; they cause disease in consumers. This is a frequent KTU trap question.
> 3. **Forgetting to specify Gram reaction** for bacteria in classification questions — costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up the toxin-producer:** Aflatoxin → *Aspergillus*; Patulin → *Penicillium*; Fumonisin → *Fusarium*.
> 5. **Writing only fungal names without grouping** as molds vs. yeasts — examiners deduct marks.
> 6. **Forgetting obligate anaerobe nature** of *Clostridium* — costs 1 mark in Part B.
> 7. **In water activity problems,** students often write aw = ERH instead of aw = ERH/100. This 100 divisor error loses a full mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Fungi** = eukaryotic, hyphal/yeast forms, aw ≥ 0.70 tolerance, important in low-pH and dried foods.
- **Bacteria** = prokaryotic, aw ≥ 0.91, dominate high-pH fresh foods (meat, fish, milk).
- **Viruses** = acellular, do NOT spoil food, cause disease in consumers (Norovirus, HAV).
- **Water activity thresholds (memorize):** Bacteria 0.91, Yeasts 0.88, Molds 0.70, Xerophilic molds 0.61.
- **Aflatoxin B1** = *Aspergillus flavus* on groundnut/maize — most carcinogenic natural toxin.
- **Patulin** = *Penicillium expansum* on apple — major juice contamination concern.
- **Fumonisin** = *Fusarium moniliforme* on maize — causes esophageal cancer.
- **Soft rot of vegetables** = *Erwinia carotovora* (Gram-negative, facultative anaerobe).
- **Botulism** = *Clostridium botulinum* (Gram-positive, obligate anaerobe, heat-resistant spores).
- **Psychrotrophic spoilage** at 4 °C = *Pseudomonas fluorescens* (Gram-negative aerobe).
- **Lactic acid bacteria (LAB)** = Gram-positive, fermentative, sour milk, pickles, sausages.
- **Generation time formula:** $N = N_0 \times 2^n$, $g = t/n$.
- **D-value** = time for 1-log reduction; **Z-value** = temperature change for 10-fold change in D.
- **aw equation:** $a_w = P/P_0 = ERH/100$.
- **Mnemonic:** *"Low pH → Fungi; Low aw → Molds; High pH + High aw → Bacteria."*
- **Viruses in food = safety issue, not spoilage issue.**
- **Most resistant food pathogen to heat** (in low-acid canned food) = *Clostridium botulinum* (D121.1°C = 0.21 min).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harvesting and Handling Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT614 - POST HARVEST HANDLING OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Harvesting and Handling Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Harvesting** is the systematic operation of gathering mature agricultural produce (fruits, vegetables, grains, and other food materials) from the field or plant at the precise stage when it has attained optimal maturity, ensuring maximum edible quality, nutritional value, and shelf-life potential. **Handling techniques** refer to the chain of post-harvest operations — including collection, sorting, cleaning, pre-cooling, packaging, and transportation — that preserve the harvested commodity's physical, biochemical, and microbiological integrity from the point of harvest to the consumer's table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Harvesting and Handling Techniques form the **first critical control point (CCP-1)** in the post-harvest value chain. Mishandling at this stage accounts for an estimated **20–40%** of total quantitative losses and a significantly higher percentage of qualitative losses in perishables.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a freshly baked cake straight from the oven. The moment you take it out (harvest), how you place it on a rack, let it cool, slice it carefully, and store it in an airtight container (handling) determines whether it remains fluffy, moist, and delicious, or becomes dry, crumbly, and stale. Food materials, especially fruits and vegetables, are "living organisms" that continue to respire even after harvest. Gentle, timely, and hygienic handling is the equivalent of "carefully placing the cake on the cooling rack."

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Respiration Rate of Fresh Produce:** Typically ranges from **5 to 200 mL CO₂/kg·h** depending on commodity.
- **Recommended Field Heat Removal Rate:** Pre-cooling should reduce pulp temperature by **75–80%** within **4–6 hours** of harvest.
- **Standard Harvest Maturity Window:** Within **±5 days** of optimum maturity for most perishables.
- **Mechanical Damage Threshold:** Bruising threshold for apples ≈ **0.3–0.5 N/mm²** contact pressure.
- **KTU Reference Metric:** **Respiratory Quotient (RQ) = CO₂ released / O₂ consumed ≈ 1.0** for aerobic respiration of carbohydrates.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Respiratory Quotient (RQ)** is a critical KTU 2024 exam metric. RQ > 1.0 indicates anaerobic respiration (alcoholic fermentation), which is undesirable as it accelerates spoilage.

## 1.4 Classification of Harvesting and Handling Techniques

| Domain | Sub-Techniques | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Harvesting** | Manual, Mechanical, Semi-Mechanical, Robotic | Fruits, vegetables, cereals |
| **Primary Handling** | Field packing, sorting, grading, washing | All perishables |
| **Secondary Handling** | Pre-cooling, curing, ripening control | Fruits, root crops |
| **Tertiary Handling** | Storage, transport, distribution | All food materials |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Maturity Indices — The "When to Harvest" Decision

Maturity indices are physiological, chemical, physical, and chronological markers that signal the optimum harvest time. KTU 2024 emphasizes the following classifications:

### A. Physical Indices
- **Color development:** Skin color change (e.g., banana: green → yellow)
- **Size and Shape:** Attainment of varietal dimensions
- **Firmness:** Penetration force measured by penetrometer (e.g., **8–12 lbf** for ripe apples)
- **Aroma:** Development of characteristic volatile compounds

### B. Chemical Indices
- **Total Soluble Solids (TSS):** Measured in **°Brix**; e.g., mango: **12–15°Brix**
- **Titratable Acidity (TA):** Decreases during ripening
- **TSS/TA Ratio:** Critical quality indicator (e.g., mango: **>30:1** at edible ripe stage)
- **Starch content:** Iodine test (e.g., apple starch-iodine chart)

### C. Physiological Indices
- **Respiration rate pattern:** Climacteric vs. non-climacteric fruits
- **Ethylene production rate:** Climacteric fruits show a sharp rise

### D. Chronological/Computed Indices
- **Days from flowering** (e.g., mango: **90–110 days**)
- **Heat units / Growing Degree Days (GDD):**

$$\text{GDD} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{T_{\max} + T_{\min}}{2} - T_{\text{base}}$$

## 2.2 Harvesting Methods — The "How to Harvest" Decision

### A. Manual Harvesting
- **Use:** Fruits (mango, apple), vegetables (cauliflower, cabbage), delicate produce
- **Tools:** Hand, secateurs, picking poles with bags, ladders
- **Advantages:** Selective, minimal mechanical damage, suitable for fragile produce
- **Disadvantages:** Labor-intensive, slow, dependent on skilled workforce

### B. Mechanical Harvesting
- **Use:** Cereals, nuts, root crops, processing tomatoes
- **Tools:** Combine harvesters, shakers, straddle harvesters
- **Advantages:** Fast, cost-effective for large-scale operations
- **Disadvantages:** Higher bruising, non-selective, high capital investment

### C. Semi-Mechanical Harvesting
- **Use:** Citrus, grapes, olives
- **Tools:** Mechanical shakers + manual collection

### D. Robotic/Precision Harvesting
- **Use:** High-value greenhouse produce (strawberries, tomatoes)
- **Tools:** Computer-vision-guided robotic arms, soft grippers

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{m_{\text{O}_2}}$ | Respiratory Quotient | dimensionless | Spoilage detection |
| 2 | $\text{GDD} = \sum \left(\frac{T_{\max} + T_{\min}}{2} - T_{\text{base}}\right)$ | Growing Degree Days | °C·day | Harvest scheduling |
| 3 | $\text{TSS/TA} = \frac{\text{Brix}}{\text{\% Acid}}$ | Quality Ratio | dimensionless | Fruit maturity |
| 4 | $Q_{10} = \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Temperature coefficient of respiration | dimensionless | Storage design |
| 5 | $\text{Loss}\,\% = \frac{W_{\text{initial}} - W_{\text{final}}}{W_{\text{initial}}} \times 100$ | Post-harvest loss calculation | % | Quality assessment |
| 6 | $E = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{t}$ | Pre-cooling energy requirement | kJ/h | Equipment sizing |
| 7 | $v_{\text{critical}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \sigma}{\rho \cdot d}}$ | Critical drop velocity (bruise limit) | m/s | Package design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Q₁₀ Rule (KTU High-Yield):** For every **10 °C** rise in temperature, the respiration rate of fresh produce roughly **doubles or triples** (Q₁₀ ≈ 2–3). This is the single most important principle justifying rapid pre-cooling.

## 2.4 Post-Harvest Handling Chain — Stepwise Logic

1. **Field Harvesting** → selective picking at optimal maturity
2. **Field Collection** → into clean, ventilated field crates (avoid overfilling)
3. **Pre-sorting** → removal of damaged, diseased, over-ripe units
4. **Pre-cooling** → remove field heat within 4–6 hours
5. **Cleaning / Washing** → chlorinated water (50–100 ppm free chlorine)
6. **Sorting and Grading** → by size, weight, color (manual or electronic)
7. **Waxing / Surface Treatment** → reduce transpiration (e.g., apple wax: 2–3 µm coating)
8. **Packaging** → field crates, corrugated boxes, MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging)
9. **Cold Storage / Transportation** → maintain cold chain at recommended temperature

### Real-World Engineering Utility
This systematic chain is applied in **Cold Chain Logistics**, **Food Processing Industries (FPI)**, **Export Houses (APEDA-regulated)**, and **Smart Agriculture (IoT-based harvest scheduling)**. Understanding the Q₁₀ and RQ principles enables engineers to design **optimum cold storage dimensions, refrigerant loads, and ventilation systems** for horticultural produce.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Pre-Cooling Time (Newton's Law of Cooling)

Newton's Law of Cooling is the foundational equation governing field-heat removal in post-harvest handling. The derivation proceeds as follows.

### Step 1: Statement of the Physical Law
The rate of heat loss from a produce body is proportional to the temperature difference between the produce surface and the surrounding cooling medium.

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = -k \cdot (T - T_a)$$

where $T$ is the produce temperature at time $t$, $T_a$ is the ambient cooling medium temperature, and $k$ is the cooling rate constant (units: $\text{h}^{-1}$).

### Step 2: Separation of Variables
Rearranging the differential equation to isolate $T$ and $t$:

$$\frac{dT}{T - T_a} = -k \cdot dt$$

### Step 3: Integration
Integrating both sides from initial produce temperature $T_i$ at $t = 0$ to produce temperature $T$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{T_i}^{T} \frac{dT}{T - T_a} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

### Step 4: Solving the Integral
Applying the standard logarithmic integral result:

$$\ln\left(\frac{T - T_a}{T_i - T_a}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

### Step 5: Exponential Form
Exponentiating both sides to obtain the explicit solution:

$$T(t) = T_a + (T_i - T_a) \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

### Step 6: Half-Cooling Time (HCT) — KTU Critical Concept
The **Half-Cooling Time** is the time required to reduce the temperature difference by **50%**. Setting $T - T_a = \frac{T_i - T_a}{2}$:

$$\frac{T_i - T_a}{2} = (T_i - T_a) \cdot e^{-k \cdot t_{1/2}}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k \cdot t_{1/2}}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -k \cdot t_{1/2}$$

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Important Result:** HCT is **independent** of initial temperature. It depends only on the cooling constant $k$, which in turn depends on produce geometry, thermal conductivity, and cooling medium velocity.

### Step 7: Seven-Eighths Cooling Time
For practical pre-cooling targets (e.g., reducing field heat by 87.5%), the **Seven-Eighths Cooling Time** is used:

$$t_{7/8} = \frac{3 \cdot \ln 2}{k} = \frac{2.079}{k} \approx 3 \cdot t_{1/2}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Q₁₀ Derivation for Respiration Rate

### Step 1: Definition
The Q₁₀ temperature coefficient is defined as the factor by which the respiration rate increases for a 10 °C rise in temperature:

$$Q_{10} = \frac{R_{T+10}}{R_T}$$

### Step 2: Arrhenius-Based Generalization
For an arbitrary temperature difference $\Delta T$:

$$Q_{10}^{\Delta T / 10} = \frac{R_{T+\Delta T}}{R_T}$$

### Step 3: Solving for Final Respiration Rate
Rearranging for $R_{T+\Delta T}$:

$$R_{T+\Delta T} = R_T \cdot Q_{10}^{\Delta T / 10}$$

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** Apples stored at 0 °C have a respiration rate of **8 mL CO₂/kg·h**. Calculate the respiration rate at 20 °C if Q₁₀ = 2.5.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta T = 20 - 0 = 20\,°\text{C}$$

$$R_{20} = 8 \cdot (2.5)^{20/10} = 8 \cdot (2.5)^2 = 8 \cdot 6.25$$

$$R_{20} = 50\,\text{mL CO}_2\text{/kg·h}$$

**Interpretation:** A 20 °C rise causes respiration rate to increase by **6.25-fold**, dramatically accelerating senescence. This justifies the **cold-chain mandate** for perishables.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mechanical Damage (Bruise Volume)

The Hertz contact theory, adapted for biological tissues, estimates bruise volume:

$$V_{\text{bruise}} \approx \frac{k \cdot F^{1.5}}{R^{0.5} \cdot E}$$

where $F$ is the impact force, $R$ is the radius of curvature of the contact zone, $E$ is the modulus of elasticity, and $k$ is an empirical tissue constant.

**Practical Implication (KTU):** Drop height in the handling chain must be limited so that impact velocity remains below the **critical bruise velocity**:

$$v_{\text{critical}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \sigma_{\text{crush}}}{\rho \cdot \tan^2\theta}}$$

where $\sigma_{\text{crush}}$ is the crushing stress, $\rho$ is the produce density, and $\theta$ is the contact geometry angle.

For apples, $\sigma_{\text{crush}} \approx 0.3\,\text{MPa}$ and $\rho \approx 850\,\text{kg/m}^3$, yielding $v_{\text{critical}} \approx 1.5\,\text{m/s}$, corresponding to a **drop height of approximately 11 cm**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Post-Harvest Handling Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Field: Mature Crop] --> B[Manual Mechanical Harvest]
    B --> C[Field Collection in Vented Crates]
    C --> D[Pre-Sorting Damaged Units]
    D --> E[Pre-Cooling Remove Field Heat]
    E --> F[Cleaning Chlorinated Wash]
    F --> G[Sorting Grading]
    G --> H[Surface Treatment Waxing]
    H --> I[Packaging MAP or Vented Box]
    I --> J[Cold Storage 0 to 13 C]
    J --> K[Refrigerated Transport]
    K --> L[Retail Display]
    L --> M[Consumer Table]

    C -.->|Reject| N[Waste Stream Compost]
    D -.->|Reject| N
    G -.->|Reject| N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Maturity Index Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Crop Ready for Assessment] --> M2{Maturity Type}
    M2 -->|Physical| P1[Color Size Firmness]
    M2 -->|Chemical| C1[TSS Brix Acidity Starch]
    M2 -->|Physiological| PH1[Respiration Ethylene Peak]
    M2 -->|Chronological| T1[Days after Flowering GDD]

    P1 --> Q1{Index at Optimum}
    C1 --> Q1
    PH1 --> Q1
    T1 --> Q1

    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[Proceed to Harvest]
    Q1 -->|No| M1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture — Cold Chain Handling System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FIELD[Field Operation Stage]
        F1[Harvesting Unit]
        F2[Field Crate Assembly]
        F3[Mobile Pre-Cooling Unit]
    end

    subgraph PACKHOUSE[Pack House Stage]
        P1[Hydrocooler]
        P2[Sizing Grader]
        P3[Wax Applicator]
        P4[MAP Packaging Line]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE[Cold Chain Stage]
        S1[Cold Storage Room]
        S2[Reefer Transport]
        S3[Distribution Hub]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[Quality Control Layer]
        Q1[Temperature Logger IoT]
        Q2[Respiration Rate Monitor]
        Q3[Bruise Detection Sensor]
    end

    F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3
    Q1 -.monitor.-> S1
    Q2 -.monitor.-> P1
    Q3 -.monitor.-> P2
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description (Mermaid): Climacteric vs Non-Climacteric Fruit Behavior

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Time Post-Harvest] --> B[Respiration Rate]
    style A fill:#e0f7fa
    style B fill:#fff9c4
```

**Conceptual Plot (described):**
- **Climacteric fruits** (banana, mango, apple, tomato): Respiration rate shows a sharp **peak (climacteric peak)** accompanied by a burst of **ethylene** production. They can ripen after harvest.
- **Non-climacteric fruits** (citrus, grapes, strawberry, cherry): Respiration rate declines steadily with no peak. They must ripen on the plant.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define maturity index. List any four physical maturity indices used for fruits and vegetables.**

**Model Answer:**

A maturity index is a measurable attribute (physical, chemical, physiological, or chronological) that indicates when a fruit or vegetable has reached the stage at which it should be harvested to ensure optimal quality, shelf-life, and consumer acceptance.

Four physical maturity indices are:
1. **Skin color** — e.g., banana changes from green to yellow; tomato from green to red.
2. **Size and shape** — varietal dimensions are attained.
3. **Firmness** — measured using a penetrometer; ripe apple firmness is typically **8–12 lbf**.
4. **Aroma / Volatile production** — characteristic aroma develops indicating ripeness (e.g., mango, pineapple).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the term "field heat" and why its rapid removal is critical in post-harvest handling.**

**Model Answer:**

**Field heat** is the sensible heat carried by harvested produce due to exposure to ambient solar and environmental conditions. It represents the thermal energy that must be removed to bring the produce down to the storage temperature.

Rapid removal is critical because:
1. **Q₁₀ effect:** Every 10 °C rise doubles or triples the respiration rate, accelerating senescence.
2. **Microbial growth:** Higher temperatures favor proliferation of spoilage organisms.
3. **Moisture loss:** Elevated produce temperature increases transpiration, leading to shrivelling.
4. **Shelf-life:** Pre-cooling within 4–6 hours can extend shelf-life by 2–3 times.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Classify the different methods of harvesting fruits and vegetables. Discuss the merits and demerits of manual and mechanical harvesting with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat flowchart, describe the complete post-harvest handling chain for a climacteric fruit such as mango. Explain the role of pre-cooling and packaging in extending shelf-life. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Classification of Harvesting Methods:**

1. **Manual Harvesting:** Done by hand or simple tools (secateurs, picking poles, ladders). Examples: mango, apple, capsicum, flowers.
   - *Merits:* Selective, gentle, low bruising, suitable for delicate produce.
   - *Demerits:* Slow, labor-intensive, costly in regions with labor shortages.

2. **Mechanical Harvesting:** Uses machinery such as combine harvesters, straddle carriers, shakers. Examples: wheat, groundnut, processing tomatoes, potatoes.
   - *Merits:* Fast, economical for large areas, low per-unit cost.
   - *Demerits:* Higher bruising, non-selective, high capital cost, not suitable for delicate fruits.

3. **Semi-Mechanical Harvesting:** Combines mechanical shaking with manual collection. Examples: citrus, olives, cherries.

4. **Robotic Harvesting:** AI-vision-guided arms with soft grippers. Examples: strawberries in greenhouses, capsicum.

| Criterion | Manual | Mechanical |
|---|---|---|
| Cost per kg | High | Low (at scale) |
| Damage % | 2–5% | 8–15% |
| Selectivity | Excellent | Poor |
| Capital | Low | Very High |
| Speed | Slow | Very Fast |

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Flowchart for Mango Post-Harvest Handling:**

```
Harvest at Optimum Maturity (TSS/TA > 20:1)
        ↓
Field Collection in Vented Plastic Crates
        ↓
De-sapping (24 h on wire mesh)
        ↓
Pre-Cooling (Hydro-cooling to 12–13 °C)
        ↓
Hot Water Treatment (52 °C, 5 min) for Quarantine
        ↓
Sorting & Grading (Size, Color, Weight)
        ↓
Waxing (Carnauba Wax 2–3 µm coating)
        ↓
MAP Packaging (3–5% O₂, 5–8% CO₂)
        ↓
Cold Storage (12–13 °C, 85–90% RH)
        ↓
Refrigerated Transport → Market
```

**Role of Pre-cooling:**
- Removes field heat, reducing respiration rate (Q₁₀ effect).
- Delays ripening by suppressing ethylene action.
- Reduces microbial load and transpiration.

**Role of Packaging:**
- Provides mechanical protection against bruising.
- Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) modifies internal O₂/CO₂ to slow respiration.
- Reduces moisture loss and contamination.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Define maturity index. Explain in detail the chemical and physiological maturity indices used for fruits, citing at least two examples for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for Newton's Law of Cooling as applied to pre-cooling of harvested produce. Define Half-Cooling Time (HCT) and Seven-Eighths Cooling Time, and compute the pre-cooling time required to reduce the pulp temperature of freshly harvested mangoes from 32 °C to 13 °C in a hydro-cooler with cooling constant k = 0.9 h⁻¹ (ambient water temperature = 8 °C). **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a)

(See detailed answer in Part A Question 1 model answer for definition.)

**Chemical Maturity Indices:**

1. **Total Soluble Solids (TSS / °Brix):** Measured using a refractometer. Indicates sugar accumulation. *Example:* Mango should reach 12–15°Brix for optimal harvest; grapes 16–20°Brix.

2. **Titratable Acidity (TA):** Decreases during ripening as organic acids are respired. *Example:* Mango TA drops from ~3% to ~0.5% during ripening.

3. **TSS/TA Ratio:** A more reliable indicator than either parameter alone. *Example:* Mango at edible ripe stage: TSS/TA > 30:1.

4. **Starch Content (Iodine Test):** Starch converts to sugar during ripening. *Example:* Apple starch-iodine chart shows 70–80% starch cleared at harvest maturity.

**Physiological Maturity Indices:**

1. **Respiration Pattern:** Climacteric fruits show a peak; non-climacteric do not.
   - *Climacteric:* Banana, mango, apple, tomato.
   - *Non-climacteric:* Citrus, grapes, cherry, strawberry.

2. **Ethylene Production Rate:** Climacteric fruits produce a burst of ethylene (1–100 µL/kg·h) at ripening initiation.

3. **Specific Gravity:** For example, potato specific gravity correlates with dry matter content.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation:**

(As shown in Section 3.1 above.)

**Final Pre-Cooling Equation:**

$$T(t) = T_a + (T_i - T_a) \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given:
- $T_i = 32\,°\text{C}$
- $T_a = 8\,°\text{C}$
- $T = 13\,°\text{C}$
- $k = 0.9\,\text{h}^{-1}$

Substituting into the equation:

$$13 = 8 + (32 - 8) \cdot e^{-0.9 \cdot t}$$

$$13 - 8 = 24 \cdot e^{-0.9 \cdot t}$$

$$5 = 24 \cdot e^{-0.9 \cdot t}$$

$$e^{-0.9 \cdot t} = \frac{5}{24} = 0.2083$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$-0.9 \cdot t = \ln(0.2083)$$

$$-0.9 \cdot t = -1.5686$$

$$t = \frac{1.5686}{0.9} \approx 1.74\,\text{hours}$$

**Verification using HCT:** $t_{1/2} = 0.693/0.9 = 0.77\,\text{h}$. Reaching 13 °C (which is a reduction of 79% of temperature difference) lies between $t_{1/2}$ and $t_{7/8} = 2.31\,\text{h}$, and our answer 1.74 h fits within this range. ✓

**Answer:** Approximately **1.74 hours (≈ 1 h 45 min)** are required to pre-cool mangoes from 32 °C to 13 °C.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to take the **natural logarithm** of the ratio $(T-T_a)/(T_i-T_a)$ when solving for $t$. Always show: $\ln(\cdot) = -k \cdot t$. Marks are lost if intermediate steps are skipped. Also, ensure units of $k$ and $t$ are consistent (e.g., both in hours).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Maturity Index** is the deciding factor for harvest timing; combine at least two indices for reliable decisions.
- **Climacteric fruits** (mango, banana, apple, tomato) can ripen after harvest; **non-climacteric** (citrus, grape, strawberry) cannot.
- **Field heat** removal within **4–6 hours** of harvest is non-negotiable for perishables.
- **Q₁₀ ≈ 2–3** is the foundational principle behind cold-chain design.
- **Newton's Law of Cooling:** $T(t) = T_a + (T_i - T_a) \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$.
- **Half-Cooling Time:** $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$, **Seven-Eighths Cooling Time:** $t_{7/8} = 2.079/k$.
- **Respiratory Quotient (RQ)** > 1.0 indicates anaerobic spoilage — undesirable.
- **Manual harvesting** suits delicate produce; **mechanical harvesting** suits cereals and processing crops.
- **Critical bruise velocity** for apples ≈ 1.5 m/s; drop heights must be controlled accordingly.
- **Chlorinated wash water:** 50–100 ppm free chlorine is standard for surface disinfection.
- **MAP packaging:** typically 3–5% O₂, 5–8% CO₂ for climacteric fruits.
- **Cold storage humidity:** maintained at **85–95% RH** for most fruits and vegetables.
- **KTU 2024 high-yield keywords:** Pre-cooling, Q₁₀, Half-Cooling Time, TSS/TA ratio, Climacteric peak, MAP, Critical bruising velocity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coatings and Treatments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT614 - POST HARVEST HANDLING OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coatings and Treatments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Edible Coatings and Surface Treatments** are defined as thin, continuous layers of edible material applied directly onto the surface of fresh or minimally processed food products to provide a selective barrier against the transfer of moisture, gases (O₂, CO₂), aroma compounds, and lipids, while simultaneously conferring mechanical protection, microbial inhibition, and improved aesthetic appeal during post-harvest handling, storage, and distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):** *Coatings and treatments constitute a primary unit operation in post-harvest technology wherein protective edible or non-edible films, sanitizing agents, and physical treatments are applied to horticultural, cereal, dairy, and marine produce to extend shelf-life, reduce physiological disorders, suppress respiration, and maintain textural and nutritional integrity.*

### 1.2 Classification of Post-Harvest Coatings & Treatments

| Category | Sub-Type | Typical Examples |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| **Edible Coatings** | Polysaccharide-based | Alginate, Chitosan, CMC, Starch, Pectin |
| | Protein-based | Whey protein, Casein, Zein, Gluten, Soy protein |
| | Lipid-based | Beeswax, Carnauba wax, Shellac, Fatty acids |
| | Composite (multi-layer) | Alginate–Beeswax bilayer; CMC–Lipid |
| **Wax Coatings** | Natural | Carnauba, Beeswax, Candelilla |
| | Synthetic | Polyethylene-based, Paraffin |
| **Surface Chemical Treatments** | Sanitizers | Chlorine (NaOCl, ClO₂), Ozonated water, H₂O₂ |
| | Acidulants | Citric acid, Ascorbic acid, Lactic acid |
| | Calcium-based | CaCl₂, Ca-lactate, Ca-propionate |
| | Anti-browning | 4-Hexylresorcinol, Erythorbic acid, N-acetylcysteine |
| | Anti-ethylene | 1-MCP (1-Methylcyclopropene) |
| **Physical / Non-Thermal Treatments** | Irradiation | UV-C, Gamma, Electron beam |
| | Atmospheric | Modified/Controlled Atmosphere packaging |
| | Hot water | Dipping (45–55 °C) |
| | Cold plasma | Dielectric barrier discharge |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Invisible Raincoat" for Produce**
> Imagine you buy an apple from the market. Although it looks intact, it is constantly **breathing** (respiring), **sweating** (transpiring water vapor), and **oxidizing** internally. A coating acts like an **invisible, edible raincoat** wrapped around the apple that:
> 1. Slows down the **sweating** (moisture loss → prevents shrivelling),
> 2. Controls the **breathing rate** (selective permeability to O₂ and CO₂ → delays ripening),
> 3. Acts as a **protective shield** against microbes and physical bruising.
>
> Without the raincoat, the apple dehydrates and rots fast. **Coatings = +Raincoat; Treatments = +Disinfectant + Antiseptic + Vitamin D spray.**

### 1.4 Why Are Coatings & Treatments Critical in Post-Harvest?

- **Shelf-life extension** — typically **2× to 4×** longer for coated fresh produce.
- **Reduction in post-harvest losses** — globally, ~**30–40%** of fresh produce is lost post-harvest; coatings can reduce this by **15–25%**.
- **Consumer appeal** — gloss, uniform color, fresh appearance (commercial waxing of apples/citrus).
- **Carrier function** — incorporation of antioxidants, antimicrobials, nutraceuticals.
- **Reduced dependence on refrigeration** — partial barrier to gases enables MAP without chilling injury.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of an Edible Coating Formulation

A typical edible coating system is a **composite matrix** comprising:

1. **Film-forming polymer (structural matrix)** — provides mechanical strength (e.g., alginate, chitosan, zein).
2. **Plasticizer** — improves flexibility, reduces brittleness (e.g., glycerol, sorbitol at **10–30% w/w** of polymer).
3. **Solvent/dispersion medium** — usually water or ethanol.
4. **Functional additives** — antimicrobials (e.g., nisin, natamycin), antioxidants (BHA, BHT, ascorbic acid), anti-browning agents, nutraceuticals, colorants.
5. **Emulsifiers/surfactants** — for lipid incorporation (e.g., Tween 80, Span 80).

### 2.2 Ideal Functional Requirements of a Coating

- Acceptable **organoleptic properties** (tasteless, odorless, transparent/glossy).
- **Water vapor barrier** to retard transpiration.
- **Selective gas permeability** — high CO₂/O₂ selectivity ratio (β = P_CO₂ / P_O₂).
- **Mechanical strength** — puncture resistance, flexibility.
- **Adhesion** to product surface.
- **Microbiological safety** — pathogen inhibition.
- **Regulatory compliance** (GRAS — Generally Recognized As Safe).

### 2.3 Film Formation Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Description | Polymer Examples |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| **Solvent evaporation (casting)** | Polymer dissolved, solvent evaporates → film | Alginate, CMC, Chitosan |
| **Coacervation (complex coacervation)** | Opposite-charged polymers interact, phase separate | Gelatin + Gum Arabic |
| **Thermal gelation** | Heat induces conformational change (coil → helix) | Whey protein, Egg albumen |
| **Ionic cross-linking** | Divalent cations (Ca²⁺) bridge anionic groups | Alginate (Ca²⁺), Low-methoxyl pectin |
| **Enzymatic cross-linking** | Transglutaminase, laccase form covalent bonds | Proteins |
| **Lipid melt-solidification** | Melted lipid solidifies on cooling | Waxes, Shellac |

### 2.4 Permeability — The Core Engineering Concept

The **permeability coefficient (P)** of a coating to a permeant is governed by Fick's First Law and Henry's Law of sorption:

$$J = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$

where:

- $J$ = steady-state permeation flux (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹ or mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹)
- $P$ = permeability coefficient (kg·m⁻¹·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹)
- $\Delta p$ = partial pressure (or concentration) difference across the film (Pa)
- $L$ = film thickness (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** For fruits and vegetables, the **oxygen transmission rate (OTR)** and **water vapor transmission rate (WVTR)** are the two single most important coating performance metrics because they directly control respiration rate and transpiration rate.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbols & Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|--------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $J = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$ | $P$: permeability (kg·m⁻¹·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹); $\Delta p$: pressure difference (Pa); $L$: thickness (m) | Steady-state permeation flux |
| 2 | $WVTR = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$ | $\Delta m$: mass change (kg); $A$: area (m²); $t$: time (s) | Water Vapor Transmission Rate |
| 3 | $WVP = \dfrac{WVTR \cdot L}{\Delta p}$ | $WVP$: water vapor permeability (kg·m⁻¹·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹) | Material-specific barrier property |
| 4 | $P = D \cdot S$ | $D$: diffusion coeff. (m²·s⁻¹); $S$: solubility coeff. (kg·m⁻³·Pa⁻¹) | Solution-diffusion model |
| 5 | $\beta = \dfrac{P_{CO_2}}{P_{O_2}}$ | $P_{CO_2}, P_{O_2}$: permeabilities of gases | Selectivity — Modified Atmosphere design |
| 6 | $RR = r_{O_2} \cdot \dfrac{[O_2]_{ext} - [O_2]_{int}}{[O_2]_{ext}}$ | $RR$: respiration rate; $r_{O_2}$: O₂ consumption rate | Control of respiration by coating |
| 7 | $\eta_{coat} = \dfrac{m_{loss, uncoated} - m_{loss, coated}}{m_{loss, uncoated}} \times 100$ | $\eta_{coat}$: % mass loss reduction | Coating efficacy metric |
| 8 | $P_{pickup} = \dfrac{m_{coated} - m_{uncoated}}{m_{uncoated}} \times 100$ | — | Pickup percentage of coating |
| 9 | $X = \dfrac{L^2}{D \cdot t}$ | $X$: dimensionless number; $D$: diffusivity | Time required to reach sorption equilibrium |
| 10 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $\sigma$: tensile stress (Pa); $F$: force (N) | Mechanical strength evaluation |

### 2.6 Functions of Specific Coatings — Engineering Perspective

- **Chitosan coatings:** Antimicrobial (effective against *E. coli*, *Salmonella*, *Botrytis cinerea*); activate chitinase in host tissue.
- **Alginate coatings:** Excellent ionic gelation with Ca²⁺; high O₂ barrier.
- **Whey protein coatings:** High O₂ barrier (excellent for nuts), low moisture barrier.
- **Lipid/wax coatings:** Best moisture barrier (low polarity); poor gas selectivity.
- **Composite bilayer coatings:** Combine lipid (outer) for moisture control + polysaccharide (inner) for gas control and adhesion.
- **Shellac:** Used commercially on apples (e.g., "applesauce fruit"); gives high gloss, good moisture barrier.

### 2.7 Surface Chemical Treatments — Mechanism Summary

| Treatment | Mode of Action | Concentration / Dose |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Chlorine (NaOCl)** | Oxidative destruction of microbial cell wall; HOCl is the active species | 50–200 ppm free Cl₂ |
| **Chlorine dioxide (ClO₂)** | Stronger oxidizer than Cl₂; does not form chloramines | 3–5 ppm |
| **Hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂)** | Produces reactive oxygen species (•OH) | 1–3% |
| **Ozonated water** | Strong oxidant; decomposes to O₂ — no residue | 1–5 ppm |
| **CaCl₂ dip** | Strengthens middle lamella (cross-links pectate) → firmness | 1–4% |
| **1-MCP** | Irreversibly binds ethylene receptors → blocks ripening | 0.5–1.0 μL·L⁻¹ |
| **Ascorbic acid / Citric acid** | Chelates Cu²⁺/Fe²⁺; reduces quinones back to phenolics → anti-browning | 0.5–2% |
| **Hot Water Treatment (HWT)** | Kills surface pathogens, induces heat-shock proteins | 45–55 °C, 5–15 min |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Citrus industry:** Waxing (carnauba + shellac) for shelf-life and gloss → mandatory in many export chains.
- **Apple industry:** 1-MCP treatment (SmartFresh™) + edible coating → enables long-distance shipping.
- **Strawberry industry:** Chitosan coating + MAP → extends shelf life 2–3×.
- **Marine products:** Alginate + essential oil coatings on salmon fillets.
- **Minimally processed fruits:** Ca-ascorbate dip + alginate coating → prevents browning & softening.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Permeation Through an Edible Coating

Starting from the fundamental principle of **solution-diffusion mechanism** (Fick's 1st Law + Henry's Law), we derive the standard permeation equation used universally in coating characterization.

**Step 1 — Fick's First Law of Diffusion.**
The flux $J$ of a permeant (e.g., water vapor) through a thin film is proportional to its concentration gradient:

$$J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$

**Text Row 1:** We start with Fick's First Law, where $D$ is the diffusion coefficient of the permeant in the coating matrix and $\dfrac{dC}{dx}$ is the concentration gradient in the thickness direction $x$.

**Step 2 — Apply at steady state.**
At steady state, $J$ is constant across the thickness, and $C$ varies linearly from $C_1$ (upstream, $x = 0$) to $C_2$ (downstream, $x = L$):

$$J = D \cdot \frac{(C_1 - C_2)}{L}$$

**Text Row 2:** We integrate Fick's Law across the thickness $L$. The negative sign vanishes because the gradient is negative (concentration falls from $C_1$ to $C_2$ as $x$ increases).

**Step 3 — Apply Henry's Law of Sorption.**
At each surface, equilibrium is assumed, so concentrations relate to partial pressures through the solubility coefficient $S$:

$$C_i = S \cdot p_i \quad (i = 1, 2)$$

**Text Row 3:** Henry's Law converts the gradient from concentration-based to pressure-based using the partition (solubility) coefficient $S$ of the permeant in the polymer.

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$\begin{aligned}
J &= D \cdot \frac{S(p_1 - p_2)}{L} \\
J &= \frac{D \cdot S \cdot (p_1 - p_2)}{L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Define permeability $P$.**

$$P = D \cdot S$$

Therefore:

$$J = \frac{P \cdot (p_1 - p_2)}{L}$$

**Text Row 5:** The product $D \cdot S$ defines the **permeability coefficient $P$**, which is a fundamental material property independent of film geometry. This is the final canonical equation used for all coating barrier characterization.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Coating Permeability Calculation

**Problem (KTU Style):** A polysaccharide coating of thickness $L = 0.10\ \text{mm}$ is applied to a mango. The coating's permeability to water vapor at 25 °C is $P = 2.5 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}\cdot\text{Pa}^{-1}$. The partial pressure difference across the coating (driving force) is $\Delta p = 1500\ \text{Pa}$. Calculate the **steady-state water vapor flux** through the coating.

**Given:**

$$P = 2.5 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}\cdot\text{Pa}^{-1}$$

$$\Delta p = 1500\ \text{Pa} = 1.5 \times 10^{3}\ \text{Pa}$$

$$L = 0.10\ \text{mm} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 1 — Write the permeation equation.**

$$J = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-11}) \times (1.5 \times 10^{3})}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}} \\
J &= \frac{3.75 \times 10^{-8}}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}} \\
J &= 3.75 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to engineering units (g·m⁻²·h⁻¹).**

$$\begin{aligned}
J &= 3.75 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1} \\
J &= 3.75 \times 10^{-1}\ \text{g}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1} \\
J &= 3.75 \times 10^{-1} \times 3600\ \text{g}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{h}^{-1} \\
J &= 1350\ \text{g}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

> The coating permits a water vapor flux of $1350\ \text{g}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$, which is **typical for a polysaccharide-only coating** (poor moisture barrier). Compare this with a carnauba wax coating ($WVTR \approx 50$–$200\ \text{g}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$) to understand why **composite coatings** are used.

### 3.3 Coating Efficacy (η) — Worked Example

**Problem:** Uncoated tomatoes lose **8.0%** of their initial mass over 7 days. Coated tomatoes lose **3.2%** over the same period. Calculate the **% mass-loss reduction** achieved by the coating.

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{coat} &= \frac{m_{loss, uncoated} - m_{loss, coated}}{m_{loss, uncoated}} \times 100 \\
\eta_{coat} &= \frac{8.0 - 3.2}{8.0} \times 100 \\
\eta_{coat} &= \frac{4.8}{8.0} \times 100 \\
\eta_{coat} &= 60\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The coating reduces post-harvest mass loss by **60%**, indicating a strong barrier effect.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Coating Types vs. Properties

| Coating Type | WVP Barrier | O₂ Barrier | CO₂/O₂ Selectivity | Antimicrobial | Mechanical Strength |
|--------------|-------------|------------|--------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Alginate** | Poor | Excellent | Moderate | None | Moderate |
| **Chitosan** | Poor | Excellent | Moderate | Excellent (intrinsic) | Good |
| **CMC** | Poor | Good | Moderate | None | Good |
| **Starch** | Poor | Moderate | Moderate | None | Moderate |
| **Whey protein** | Poor | Excellent | High | None | Good (when plasticized) |
| **Zein (corn)** | Poor-Moderate | Excellent | High | None | Moderate |
| **Carnauba wax** | Excellent | Poor | Low | None | Moderate |
| **Shellac** | Excellent | Poor | Low | None | Moderate |
| **Composite (Alginate + Beeswax)** | Good | Excellent | Moderate | None | Good |
| **Chitosan + Essential oil** | Poor | Excellent | High | Excellent | Good |

### 3.5 Layer-by-Layer (LBL) Coating Formation — Mathematical Framework

In LBL assembly, each polyelectrolyte layer thickness increment $\Delta L$ per bilayer is given by:

$$\Delta L = \frac{m_{ads}}{A \cdot \rho_{dry}}$$

where $m_{ads}$ is the mass of polymer adsorbed per bilayer, $A$ is the substrate area, and $\rho_{dry}$ is the dry polymer density.

For an $n$-bilayer assembly, total thickness is:

$$L_{total} = n \cdot \Delta L + L_{substrate}$$

**Text Note:** LBL allows **nanometer-precision** tuning of coating thickness, barrier, and surface charge — useful in advanced post-harvest research.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture of a Composite Bilayer Edible Coating

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AirEnv["External Air Environment"] --> OuterLipid["Outer Lipid Layer Waxes / Shellac / Fatty Acids Function: Moisture Barrier"]
    OuterLipid --> InnerPolymer["Inner Polymer Layer Alginate / Chitosan / Protein Function: Gas Barrier + Adhesion"]
    InnerPolymer --> AdditivesZone["Functional Additive Zone Antimicrobials / Antioxidants / Anti-browning Agents"]
    AdditivesZone --> ProduceSurface["Produce Surface Tissue"]
    ProduceSurface --> ProduceBulk["Bulk Food Matrix Fruit / Vegetable / Fish"]

    classDef env fill:#e8f1ff,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef lipid fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef polymer fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef additive fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#831843,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef food fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000

    class AirEnv env
    class OuterLipid lipid
    class InnerPolymer polymer
    class AdditivesZone additive
    class ProduceSurface,ProduceBulk food
```

> [!NOTE]
> The bilayer architecture resolves the classical trade-off in edible coatings: **lipid layers** give excellent moisture barriers but poor gas selectivity, while **polysaccharide/protein layers** provide good gas selectivity but weak moisture barriers. Combining them gives balanced performance.

### 4.2 Functional Flow — From Coating Formulation to Shelf-Life Extension

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Selection of Polymer Alginate Chitosan Whey Protein Zein"] --> B["Solvent Preparation Aqueous or Ethanolic"]
    B --> C["Addition of Plasticizer Glycerol Sorbitol"]
    C --> D["Incorporation of Additives Antimicrobial Antioxidant Nutrient"]

    E --> F["Drying or Setting Air Drying Cooling Crosslinking Ca ions"]
    F --> G["Active Coated Product"]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Q1 -- Yes --> LipidPath["Lipid or Wax Coating Carnauba Shellac Beeswax"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is Microbial Inhibition Required?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> AntiPath["Chitosan or Coating with Essential Oils Natamycin Nisin"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Is Mechanical Strength Critical?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ProteinPath["Protein Based Coating Whey Protein Zein Casein"]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Is High Gas Barrier Critical?"}
```
