## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "edible coating" used in post-harvest technology. List any four ideal functional properties required of an edible coating.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** An edible coating is a thin, continuous layer of edible material (polysaccharide, protein, lipid, or composite) applied directly onto the surface of a food product to act as a selective barrier against mass transfer (moisture, gases, aroma) and to extend shelf-life while being consumed safely with the product.
>
> **Four ideal properties:**
> 1. High water vapor barrier to retard transpiration and weight loss.
> 2. Selective gas permeability — preferably high CO₂/O₂ permeability ratio for MAP effect.
> 3. Good mechanical strength (puncture resistance, flexibility).
> 4. Excellent adhesion to the produce surface and GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe) status.
> *(Additional acceptable: antimicrobial activity, transparency/gloss, biodegradability, no off-flavor.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between "edible coating" and "edible film" with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

> | Aspect | Edible Coating | Edible Film |
> |--------|----------------|-------------|
> | **Form** | Applied as liquid/solution/emulsion directly onto food surface | Pre-formed, free-standing solid sheet |
> | **Application** | Dipping, spraying, brushing — forms film in situ | Wrapped or laminated around the product |
> | **Adhesion** | Intimate contact with surface | Loose wrapping |
> | **Examples** | Wax coating on apples, chitosan dip on strawberries | Starch-based biodegradable film wrapping a sandwich |
> | **Thickness** | Typically 5–100 μm | Typically 50–250 μm |
> | **Typical use** | Fresh produce (fruits, vegetables, fish) | Processed foods, sandwich wraps, sachet |
>
> *Coatings = "formed-on-product"; Films = "formed-then-applied."*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split as sub-part (a) = 7 marks and sub-part (b) = 7 marks. Internal choice is mandatory.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify edible coatings used in post-harvest handling of foods based on their chemical nature. Describe the film-forming mechanism of **alginate-based coatings** with the help of the "egg-box model."

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polysaccharide coating of thickness 0.08 mm has a water vapor permeability of $P = 3.6 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}\cdot\text{Pa}^{-1}$. The partial pressure difference across the coating during storage is 1800 Pa. Calculate the steady-state water vapor flux through the coating in $\text{g}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification and Alginate Mechanism**

**Classification of edible coatings based on chemical nature:**

1. **Polysaccharide-based coatings** — Alginate, Chitosan, CMC, Starch, Pectin, Pullulan, Carrageenan. Function: good gas barrier, moderate mechanical properties, poor moisture barrier.

2. **Protein-based coatings** — Whey protein, Casein, Zein, Soy protein isolate, Wheat gluten, Egg albumen. Function: excellent O₂ barrier, moderate moisture barrier, good mechanical flexibility.

3. **Lipid-based coatings** — Beeswax, Carnauba wax, Shellac, Paraffin, Fatty acids, Resins. Function: excellent moisture barrier, poor gas barrier, used as outer layer in composites.

4. **Composite coatings** — Combination of two or more classes (e.g., alginate + beeswax bilayer; CMC + lipid); designed to overcome individual limitations.

**Egg-Box Model of Alginate Gelation:**

Alginate is a linear copolymer of **β-D-mannuronic acid (M)** and **α-L-guluronic acid (G)** residues. When **Ca²⁺ ions** are added, they preferentially bind to the GG blocks of two adjacent alginate chains, forming an **"egg-box"** junction zone — analogous to eggs fitting into the cavities of an egg carton.

- The G-blocks create **buckled, zig-zag conformations** that present cavities of appropriate geometry for Ca²⁺.
- The Ca²⁺ ions **coordinate with the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups** of the G-residues of two opposing chains.
- This results in **ionic cross-linking** and rapid gelation, producing a strong, transparent hydrogel film.

This is the primary reason alginate is the most commonly used polysaccharide coating in commercial post-harvest practice.

**(b) Permeation Flux Calculation**

**Given:**

$$L = 0.08\ \text{mm} = 8.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$$

$$P = 3.6 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}\cdot\text{Pa}^{-1}$$

$$\Delta p = 1800\ \text{Pa} = 1.8 \times 10^{3}\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 1 — Write the flux equation.**

$$J = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{(3.6 \times 10^{-11}) \times (1.8 \times 10^{3})}{8.0 \times 10^{-5}} \\
J &= \frac{6.48 \times 10^{-8}}{8.0 \times 10^{-5}} \\
J &= 8.10 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to g·m⁻²·h⁻¹.**

$$J = 8.10 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 \times 3600 = 2916\ \text{g}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$$

**Result:** The water vapor flux is **2916 g·m⁻²·h⁻¹**, indicating this is a **high-permeability (poor moisture barrier)** coating — typical of pure polysaccharide films. A lipid or composite layer would be required to improve moisture protection.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of the following post-harvest treatments with their mode of action:
   (i) 1-Methylcyclopropene (1-MCP)
   (ii) Calcium chloride (CaCl₂) dip
   (iii) Chlorine dioxide (ClO₂) sanitization

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **composite bilayer edible coating** system used for fresh fruits. Explain why a bilayer is preferred over a single-component coating.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Post-Harvest Treatments**

**(i) 1-MCP (1-Methylcyclopropene):**
- A synthetic cyclic olefin that **irreversibly binds to ethylene receptors** in plant tissues.
- Mechanism: Ethylene receptors (ETR1, EIN4, etc.) bind 1-MCP at ~10,000× greater affinity than ethylene.
- Effect: **Blocks ethylene perception** → delays ripening, softening, color change, senescence.
- Used commercially as **SmartFresh™** at **0.5–1.0 μL·L⁻¹** for apples, pears, bananas, tomatoes.
- Limitation: Effect is irreversible; once applied, fruit may not ripen normally afterward (issue for climacteric fruits).

**(ii) Calcium Chloride (CaCl₂) Dip:**
- Used typically at **1–4% w/v** for 5–15 min.
- Mechanism: Ca²⁺ ions **cross-link carboxyl groups of pectate** in the middle lamella of cell walls → forms **calcium pectate (egg-box-like) networks**.
- Effect: Strengthens cell wall → maintains **firmness, reduces softening**; also reduces postharvest decay by maintaining structural integrity.
- Commonly applied to apples, strawberries, melons, tomatoes.
- Also delays senescence and reduces respiration rate.

**(iii) Chlorine Dioxide (ClO₂) Sanitization:**
- A powerful oxidizing sanitizer used at **3–5 ppm** in wash water.
- Mechanism: Strong oxidant disrupts microbial cell membranes, denatures proteins, and inactivates enzymes; unlike chlorine (HOCl), it does **not form carcinogenic chloramines** or trihalomethanes.
- Effective against *E. coli* O157:H7, *Salmonella*, *Listeria*, fungal spores.
- Used on leafy greens, sprouts, fresh-cut produce.

**(b) Composite Bilayer Edible Coating — Block Diagram**

```
┌────────────────────────────────────┐
│  AIR ENVIRONMENT                   │
│  ──────────────────────────        │
│  OUTER LIPID LAYER                 │
│  (Carnauba/Beeswax/Shellac)        │
│  Function: Moisture Barrier        │
│  ──────────────────────────        │
│  INNER POLYMER LAYER               │
│  (Alginate/Chitosan/CMC/Protein)   │
│  Function: Gas Barrier + Adhesion  │
│  ──────────────────────────        │
│  PRODUCE SURFACE (Skin/Cuticle)    │
│  ──────────────────────────        │
│  PRODUCE BULK TISSUE               │
└────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Why bilayer is preferred over single-component:**

1. **Overcomes the polarity trade-off** — Pure lipid coatings are excellent moisture barriers but poor gas barriers; pure polysaccharide coatings are excellent gas barriers but poor moisture barriers. The bilayer combines both functions.
2. **Optimal gas composition** — The inner polysaccharide layer modifies internal O₂ and CO₂ levels (MAP effect), reducing respiration and delaying ripening.
3. **Mechanical robustness** — Polymer inner layer provides structural integrity; lipid outer layer provides flexibility and gloss.
4. **Improved adhesion** — Polysaccharide adheres well to the wet produce surface; lipid adheres poorly but provides the moisture barrier on the air side.
5. **Commercial relevance** — Industry uses this design in waxed apples, citrus, mangoes, and minimally processed fruits.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU 2024 Examiners' Panel):**
> 1. **Confusing "coating" with "film"** — Examiners strictly differentiate (formed-on-product vs. pre-formed sheet). Marks deducted if misused.
> 2. **Skipping units in numerical problems** — Always write SI units in **every step** of permeation/respiration calculations. Examiner's mandate under KTU 2024 OBE.
> 3. **Writing "wax" without specifying type** — Always specify carnauba, beeswax, shellac, or paraffin — generic "wax" is incomplete.
> 4. **Forgetting the "why"** — When asked to "explain" 1-MCP or CaCl₂, students often list only the effect; KTU demands the **mode of action (receptor binding / pectate cross-linking)** for full marks.
> 5. **No diagram in Part-B answers worth 14 marks** — A labeled block diagram or flow chart is **almost always expected** for full marks.
> 6. **Mixing MAP and coatings** — Modified Atmosphere Packaging is a *treatment*; edible coatings are *applied films*. Do not interchange them.
> 7. **Failure to mention GRAS status** — When asked about safety of coatings, always mention regulatory compliance (GRAS / FSSAI / FDA).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Coatings and Treatments**

- ✅ **Edible coating** = thin, edible barrier applied directly on food surface; **Edible film** = pre-formed sheet wrapped onto food.
- ✅ **Three major classes:** Polysaccharides, Proteins, Lipids; **composite bilayers** combine the best of each.
- ✅ **Alginate** gels by Ca²⁺ cross-linking of G-blocks — the **"egg-box" model**.
- ✅ **Chitosan** is the only natural polymer with intrinsic **antimicrobial activity** (cationic nature → interacts with microbial membranes).
- ✅ **Waxes** (carnauba, beeswax, shellac) are the **best moisture barriers** but poor gas barriers.
- ✅ **Permeability equation:** $J = P \cdot \Delta p / L$; **Permeability** $P = D \cdot S$ (solution-diffusion model).
- ✅ **WVP** is the key engineering metric for moisture barrier; **OTR/CO₂TR** for gas barrier.
- ✅ **Selectivity ratio** $\beta = P_{CO_2}/P_{O_2}$ determines MAP efficacy — typical target $\beta$ ≈ 3–10 for fresh produce.
- ✅ **1-MCP** blocks ethylene receptors irreversibly — used at ppb levels (0.5–1.0 μL·L⁻¹).
- ✅ **CaCl₂ dip** strengthens cell wall via calcium pectate cross-linking → maintains firmness.
- ✅ **ClO₂** (3–5 ppm) preferred over NaOCl due to no chloramine/THM formation.
- ✅ **HWT** (45–55 °C, 5–15 min) — controls surface pathogens and induces host defense responses.
- ✅ **Composite bilayer** = lipid outer (moisture barrier) + polysaccharide inner (gas barrier & adhesion) — preferred for high-value fruits.
- ✅ **Additives** (antimicrobials, antioxidants, anti-browning agents) are incorporated into coatings for **active packaging**.
- ✅ **Always state GRAS status** when justifying food-grade coatings.
- ✅ Numerical problems require **SI units at every step**, final answer in $\text{g}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$ (WVTR) or $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$ (flux).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Insect Control and Microbial Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT614 - POST HARVEST HANDLING OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Insect Control and Microbial Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛡️ Module 4 — Insect Control & Microbial Control

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Insect Control (Post-Harvest Entomology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> *Insect control* in post-harvest handling refers to the **integrated set of preventive, detective, and curative measures** employed to suppress infestation by stored-product insects in harvested, stored, and processed food commodities, while maintaining product quality, safety, and regulatory compliance.

**In simple words — the Analogy:**
Imagine your kitchen rice container. Over a few months, you sometimes see tiny black beetles crawling on the wall near the container. These are **stored-product insects** (e.g., *Sitophilus oryzae* — rice weevil). Now, you have three jobs:
- **Prevent them from entering** (prevention) — keep the container sealed.
- **Detect them early** (detection) — use pheromone traps.
- **Kill them if present** (curative) — expose the rice to low oxygen (controlled atmosphere) or use safe fumigants.

That three-pronged strategy — **Prevent, Detect, Eradicate** — is the essence of insect control in food warehousing.

---

### 1.2 Microbial Control (Food Microbiology & Preservation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**
> *Microbial control* refers to the **deliberate application of physical, chemical, and biological agents to reduce, inhibit, or eliminate the microbial load** (bacteria, yeasts, moulds, and viruses) on food surfaces or within food matrices, thereby extending shelf life and ensuring food safety.

**The Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of microbes as "uninvited guests" arriving on food. The food is the **house**, and microbes need **water, nutrients, warmth, and time** (the W-N-W-T framework) to multiply. Microbial control simply means *removing one or more of these comforts* — by drying (no water), refrigerating (no warmth), salting (no free water), or heating (killing them outright). It is essentially a **hospitality denial strategy** for micro-organisms.

---

### 1.3 Why These Two Topics are Co-Taught in KTU Module 4

> [!NOTE]
> Post-harvest losses in India (≈ **₹92,651 crore/year**, as per ICAR-CIPHET 2022 estimates) are caused by **insects (≈25%) and microorganisms (≈30%)** — the two largest contributors. Hence, KTU bundles them into a single control module.

**Key Term Standard Reference Metrics (per KTU 2024 Scheme):**

| Term | Standard Value | Significance |
|------|---------------|--------------|
| Safe Moisture Content (Cereals) | **≤ 12–14%** (w.b.) | Below this, insect activity is suppressed |
| Safe Water Activity ($a_w$) | **< 0.60** | Threshold for microbial growth inhibition |
| Standard Phosphine Fumigation Dose | **1–3 g/m³** for 5–7 days | Common stored-grain protocol |
| Codex Acceptable Thermal Process | **F₀ ≥ 3 min** for low-acid canned food | Commercial sterility |
| Ionising Irradiation Dose (Insect Disinfestation) | **0.2–0.5 kGy** | Permitted by FSSAI & Atomic Energy Act |

---

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot

> [!TIP]
> **Memorisation Frame — The "P-C-P-B" Acronym**
> - **P** — Physical
> - **C** — Chemical
> - **P** — Physico-chemical / Packaging
> - **B** — Biological / Bio-rational
>
> This same four-pillar structure governs *both* insect and microbial control.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Insect Control — Detailed Methods

### 2.1.1 Physical Methods
1. **Temperature Manipulation**
   - *Low temperature (< 13 °C)*: Insects become inactive (diapause). Cold disinfestation used in citrus, mangoes.
   - *High temperature (> 45 °C for > 24 h)*: Lethal through protein denaturation. Used in flour mills (heat treatment at 60–80 °C).
2. **Mechanical Methods**
   - Sieving, aspiration, brushing.
   - Impact-entoleter mills for flour (≥ 12,000 rpm) to kill insect fragments and eggs.
3. **Modified / Controlled Atmosphere (MA / CA)**
   - Reduce O₂ below **2%** OR raise CO₂ above **60%** → insects die via asphyxiation.
   - Hermetic storage (e.g., Purdue Improved Crop Storage — PICS bags) — triple-bag technology.
4. **Irradiation**
   - Gamma rays ($^{60}$Co / $^{137}$Cs) or e-beam at **0.2–0.5 kGy** sterilises adult and eggs.
   - Approved by **FSSAI (2016)**, **Codex Alimentarius**, and **WHO**.

### 2.1.2 Chemical Methods
1. **Fumigation** (KTU High-Priority)
   - **Phosphine (PH₃)** — generated from aluminium phosphide tablets (e.g., Celphos). 1–3 g/m³ for 5–7 days.
   - **Methyl Bromide (CH₃Br)** — phased out under Montreal Protocol (ozone-depleting) except for quarantine.
   - **Ethylene Oxide (EtO)** — for spices, dry fruits.
2. **Contact Insecticides**
   - Pyrethroids (deltamethrin, bifenthrin) — residual surface sprays on godown walls, dunnage.
   - Organophosphates (malathion) — limited use.

### 2.1.3 Biological & Bio-rational Methods
- **Pheromone traps** (e.g., *Trécé™ Storgard*) — detection and mass-trapping using *Sitophilus*, *Tribolium* lures.
- **Parasitoids** — *Anisopteromalus calandrae* parasitises weevils.
- **Entomopathogenic fungi** — *Beauveria bassiana*, *Metarhizium anisopliae*.
- **Insect Growth Regulators (IGRs)** — methoprene, hydroprene (juvenile hormone analogues).

---

## 2.2 Microbial Control — Detailed Methods

### 2.2.1 Physical Methods
1. **Thermal Processing**
   - *Pasteurisation* (HTST — 72 °C / 15 s).
   - *Sterilisation* (121 °C / 15 psi / 15 min for low-acid foods).
   - *Blanching* (95–100 °C / 2–5 min) for fruits/veg pre-freezing.
2. **Non-Thermal Physical Methods**
   - High-Pressure Processing (HPP) — **400–600 MPa**, room temp.
   - Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) — **20–80 kV/cm**.
   - Ultraviolet (UV-C) — **254 nm**, surface sterilisation.
   - Cold Plasma, Ultrasound, Ozone.

### 2.2.2 Chemical Methods
1. **Sanitisers & Disinfectants**
   - Sodium hypochlorite (**50–200 ppm** free Cl₂).
   - Quaternary Ammonium Compounds (QACs) — benzalkonium chloride.
   - Peracetic acid, H₂O₂ vaporised, Ozonated water.
2. **Food Preservatives (FSSAI-permitted)**
   - *Class I (Natural)*: Salt, sugar, vinegar, lemon, spices.
   - *Class II (Chemical)*: **Sorbic acid (0.1%)**, **Benzoic acid (0.1%)**, **Sodium benzoate (0.1%)**, **Sulphites (≤ 450 ppm)**, **Nitrites (≤ 200 ppm)**, **BHA/BHT (≤ 200 ppm)**.

### 2.2.3 Biological Methods
- Lactic acid bacteria producing **bacteriocins** (Nisin from *Lactococcus lactis* — 12.5 ppm max).
- **Bio-preservation** through natural fermentation.
- **Probiotic & competitive exclusion** (e.g., *Lactobacillus* in meat).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All standard KTU 2024 Scheme formulas relevant to Module 4 are consolidated below.** These appear frequently in ESE numericals.

### Table 2.1 — High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Range | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | **Decimal Reduction Time (D-value)** | $D = \dfrac{t}{\log N_0 - \log N}$ | minutes | Time to reduce microbial count by 90% (1 log) at constant T |
| 2 | **Z-value (Thermal Resistance)** | $Z = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{\log D_1 - \log D_2}$ | °C | Temp increase needed to reduce D by 90% |
| 3 | **Sterilising Value (F-value)** | $F = D \times (\log N_0 - \log N)$ | minutes | Equivalent time at reference T |
| 4 | **Reference F-value (Low-acid)** | $F_0 = \Delta t \times 10^{\frac{T - 121.1}{z}}$ | minutes | At reference T = 121.1 °C, z = 10 °C |
| 5 | **Q₁₀ Rule** | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | dimensionless | 2–3 for biological reactions |
| 6 | **Water Activity** | $a_w = \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | 0 to 1 | $P$ = vapour pressure of food; $P_0$ = pure water |
| 7 | **Moisture Content (w.b.)** | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | For safe storage of cereals: $\leq 12$% |
| 8 | **Fumigation Concentration-Time Product (CT)** | $C \times t = K$ | (mg/L)·h | Used in HCN, PH₃ dosimetry |
| 9 | **Phosphine Dose Required** | $M = C \times V$ | mg | $C$ = 1–3 g/m³; $V$ = volume of enclosure |
| 10 | **Radiation D-value (Microbes)** | $\log \dfrac{N_0}{N} = \dfrac{D}{D_{10}}$ | kGy | $D_{10}$ ≈ 0.2–0.5 kGy for insects |
| 11 | **Recontamination Rate** | $R = \dfrac{dN}{dt} = k \cdot N$ | cfu/h | First-order microbial growth kinetics |
| 12 | **Michaelis–Menten (Enzyme Inactivation)** | $v = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | — | For residual enzyme activity (e.g., peroxidase) |
| 13 | **Microbial Death Rate (D-curve)** | $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -k N$ | — | Solution: $N = N_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ |
| 14 | **Decimal log Reduction** | $\text{Reduction} = \log_{10} \left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)$ | log units | "5-log reduction" = 99.999% kill |
| 15 | **Heat Penetration (Surface Sterility)** | $T(r,t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_s) \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{h A}{m c_p} t\right)$ | °C | Newton's law of cooling in cans |

> [!TIP]
> **For the KTU exam:** Memorise **D-value, Z-value, F-value, Q₁₀, and $a_w$** — these are the *panchnama* (fatal five) of Module 4 numericals.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dairy Industry**: HTST pasteurisation parameters are derived from D & z values of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* and *Coxiella burnetii*.
- **Canning Industry**: F₀ ≥ 3 min for *Clostridium botulinum* control in low-acid canned foods.
- **Grain Warehousing (FCI/State Godowns)**: Phosphine fumigation is the national protocol — designed using **C × t = K** and gas-tightness tests (pressure drop < ½ inch in 3 min).
- **Meat & Poultry Export**: HPP + bio-preservation (Nisin) enables export-quality cold chain logistics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Truncation Mandate:** No derivation is summarised — *every algebraic step* is shown, with the *physical reason* for the transition.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — D-value (Decimal Reduction Time) from First-Order Microbial Death Kinetics

**Starting Premise:** Microbial inactivation under constant lethal conditions follows **first-order kinetics**.

### Step 1 — Define the differential form
The rate of microbial inactivation is proportional to the surviving population:

$$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N$$

**Why?** Microbes die *individually* by random "hits" (heat, radiation, chemical) — a stochastic Poisson process. Each microbe has an *equal, small* probability of being killed per unit time → first-order.

### Step 2 — Separate variables
Rearrange to integrate both sides:

$$\dfrac{dN}{N} = -k \, dt$$

### Step 3 — Integrate from initial state to final state
Integrate from $t = 0$ (population $N_0$) to $t = t$ (population $N$):

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \dfrac{dN}{N} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln N - \ln N_0 = -kt$$

### Step 4 — Convert to exponential form

$$\ln \left(\dfrac{N}{N_0}\right) = -kt$$

$$\dfrac{N}{N_0} = e^{-kt}$$

### Step 5 — Take log base 10 (engineering convention)

$$\log_{10} \left(\dfrac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\dfrac{kt}{2.3026}$$

### Step 6 — Define the D-value
D-value is defined as the time required for a **90% (1 log₁₀) reduction**, i.e., $\log_{10}(N_0/N) = 1$.

Set $\log_{10}(N_0/N) = 1$ and $t = D$:

$$1 = \dfrac{k \cdot D}{2.3026} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \dfrac{2.3026}{D}$$

### Step 7 — Generalised D-value formula
Substitute $k$ back into the log equation:

$$\log_{10} \left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = \dfrac{t}{D}$$

Rearranging (this is the *standard KTU form*):

$$\boxed{D = \dfrac{t}{\log N_0 - \log N}}$$

**Dimensional check:** time (min) ÷ dimensionless = **min** ✓

**Physical interpretation:** If $D_{121.1} = 2.5 \text{ min}$ for *C. botulinum*, then it takes 2.5 min at 121.1 °C to kill 90% of the spores.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Z-value from Thermal Death Time Curve

**Starting Premise:** Log D-value decreases linearly with temperature T (empirical observation).

### Step 1 — State the empirical relationship
The log of the D-value falls linearly as T increases:

$$\log D = -m \cdot T + c$$

where $m$ is slope and $c$ is intercept on the log-D axis.

### Step 2 — Evaluate at two temperature points ($T_1$, $D_1$) and ($T_2$, $D_2$)
Subtracting:

$$\log D_1 - \log D_2 = -m(T_1 - T_2)$$

$$m = \dfrac{\log D_2 - \log D_1}{T_1 - T_2}$$

### Step 3 — Define Z-value
By definition, **Z** is the temperature rise (in °C) required for the log D-value to drop by **1 unit** (i.e., 90% reduction in D).

Set $\log D_1 - \log D_2 = 1$ and $T_1 - T_2 = Z$:

$$1 = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{Z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \dfrac{1}{Z}$$

### Step 4 — Final KTU Standard Z-Formula

$$\boxed{Z = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{\log D_1 - \log D_2}}$$

**Dimensional check:** °C ÷ dimensionless = **°C** ✓

**Physical interpretation:** $Z = 10 \text{ °C}$ is standard for *C. botulinum* — every 10 °C increase reduces D by 90%.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — F-value (Sterilising Value)

### Step 1 — Recall D-value equation
From §3.1:

$$t = D \cdot \log \left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)$$

### Step 2 — The F-value is the *time* required at a **reference temperature** to achieve the same log reduction
Thus, the F-value is simply the time $t$ at reference T:

$$\boxed{F = D_{ref} \cdot \log \left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)}$$

### Step 3 — Generalised expression for variable temperature
At any instant, lethality contribution = $\Delta t \times 10^{(T-T_{ref})/z}$:

$$F_0 = \sum \Delta t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - 121.1}{z}}$$

where $T_{ref} = 121.1 \text{ °C}$, $z = 10 \text{ °C}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Phosphine Dosage for a Godown

**Scenario:** A rectangular godown $20 \text{ m} \times 10 \text{ m} \times 5 \text{ m}$ (L × B × H) is to be fumigated with PH₃ at $2 \text{ g/m}^3$. Calculate the mass of aluminium phosphide (AlP) tablets required.

### Step 1 — Compute volume

$$V = L \times B \times H = 20 \times 10 \times 5 = 1000 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 2 — Compute required mass of phosphine gas

$$M_{PH_3} = C \times V = 2 \text{ g/m}^3 \times 1000 \text{ m}^3 = 2000 \text{ g} = 2 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 3 — Stoichiometric conversion
Aluminium phosphide reacts with atmospheric moisture:

$$2 \text{ AlP} + 6 \text{ H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2 \text{ Al(OH)}_3 + 2 \text{ PH}_3 \uparrow$$

**Molecular masses:** AlP = 57.96 g/mol; PH₃ = 33.99 g/mol.

**Stoichiometric mass ratio:**

$$\dfrac{2 \cdot M_{AlP}}{2 \cdot M_{PH_3}} = \dfrac{2 \times 57.96}{2 \times 33.99} = \dfrac{115.92}{67.98} \approx 1.705$$

### Step 4 — Mass of AlP required (purity ≈ 56% for commercial tablets)

$$M_{AlP} = 2000 \times 1.705 = 3410 \text{ g of pure AlP}$$

$$M_{tablet} = \dfrac{3410}{0.56} = 6089 \text{ g} \approx 6.09 \text{ kg of commercial AlP tablets}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{M_{AlP} \approx 6.1 \text{ kg}}$ of 56%-purity commercial tablets.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Examples (Analytical Matrix)

### Example 3.1 — Thermal Death
A bacterial suspension has $N_0 = 10^6$ cfu/mL. After 4 min at 60 °C, survivors = $10^2$ cfu/mL. Find D-value.

**Solution:**

$$D = \dfrac{t}{\log N_0 - \log N} = \dfrac{4}{\log 10^6 - \log 10^2} = \dfrac{4}{6 - 2} = \dfrac{4}{4} = 1 \text{ min}$$

### Example 3.2 — Z-value Determination
At 60 °C, $D = 1$ min; at 70 °C, $D = 0.1$ min. Find Z.

$$Z = \dfrac{70 - 60}{\log 1 - \log 0.1} = \dfrac{10}{0 - (-1)} = \dfrac{10}{1} = 10 \text{ °C}$$

### Example 3.3 — Q₁₀ of Spoilage
A fruit spoils in 8 days at 25 °C and 3 days at 35 °C. Find Q₁₀.

$$Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{8}{3}\right)^{\frac{10}{35-25}} = \left(\dfrac{8}{3}\right)^1 = 2.667$$

### Example 3.4 — Water Activity
A dried fruit has vapour pressure $P = 0.4$ kPa; pure water $P_0 = 2.34$ kPa at 20 °C. Find $a_w$.

$$a_w = \dfrac{0.4}{2.34} \approx 0.171$$

Below **0.60**, this fruit is **microbially safe** at ambient.

### Table 3.1 — Microbial Growth $a_w$ Thresholds (Critical Reference)

| Microorganism | Minimum $a_w$ for Growth |
|---------------|-------------------------|
| Most bacteria | 0.91 |
| Most yeasts | 0.88 |
| Most moulds | 0.80 |
| *Staphylococcus aureus* | 0.86 |
| *Salmonella* spp. | 0.93 |
| *Aspergillus flavus* (aflatoxin) | 0.78 |
| *Xeromyces bisporus* (xerophilic) | 0.61 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Reducing $a_w$ below 0.60 yields **microbially stable foods** (intermediate-moisture foods) — the principle behind jam, dried fruits, honey, etc.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Insect vs Microbial Control

| Feature | Insect Control | Microbial Control |
|---------|---------------|-------------------|
| Primary target | Macro-organisms (1 mm–10 mm) | Micro-organisms (0.1 µm–10 µm) |
| Detection | Pheromone traps, Berlese funnel | Plate count, MPN, ATP swab |
| Dose standard | 0.2–0.5 kGy (irradiation) | $F_0$ ≥ 3 min (thermal) |
| Penetrative method | Fumigation, irradiation | Heat, HPP, radiation |
| Log-reduction standard | 100% mortality | 5-log reduction |
| Regulatory body in India | CIBRC, FSSAI, Plant Quarantine | FSSAI, Codex, BIS |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams are **Mermaid** (KTU-preferred), with safe node IDs (alphanumeric, no reserved keywords) and clean plain-text labels.

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Integrated Insect Control Strategy (Macro-Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Harvested Commodity Entry] --> B[Pre-storage Inspection]
    B --> C{Infestation Status}
    C -->|Clean| D[Storage in Hermetic Bags or Silos]
    C -->|Suspect| E[Pheromone Trap Monitoring]
    E -->|Trap Hit| F[Targeted Treatment]
    E -->|No Hit| D
    F --> G[Treatment Selection Node]
    G --> H1[Physical: Heat at 60C]
    G --> H2[Chemical: PH3 Fumigation]
    G --> H3[Biologics: Beauveria bassiana]
    G --> H4[Radiation: 0.3 kGy Gamma]
    H1 --> I[Re-inspection]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I -->|Clean| J[Safe Storage with PICS Bags]
    I -->|Residual| G
    D --> K[Periodic Monitoring Loop]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Final Dispatch]
```

**Engineering Interpretation:** This is the standard **IPM (Integrated Pest Management)** protocol for FCI godowns. The control loop K → re-inspection → I ensures *continuous* quality assurance, not just one-time treatment.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Microbial Control Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Raw Food Material] --> Inspect[Microbial Load Assay]
    Inspect --> Decision{Target Organism}
    Decision -->|Vegetative Bacteria| P1[Heat: HTST 72C 15s]
    Decision -->|Bacterial Spores| P2[Heat: 121C 15 min Autoclave]
    Decision -->|Yeasts Moulds| P3[Reduce aw below 0.65]
    Decision -->|Surface Microbes| P4[UV-C 254 nm]
    Decision -->|Heat-sensitive| P5[HPP 600 MPa]
    P1 --> Chem[Chemical Hurdle]
    P2 --> Chem
    P3 --> Chem
    P4 --> Chem
    P5 --> Chem
    Chem --> Pack[Modified Atmosphere Packaging MAP]
    Pack --> Cold[Cold Chain 4C]
    Cold --> Out[Safe Shelf Life Extension]
```

**Engineering Interpretation:** Modern food preservation uses the **Hurdle Technology** principle (Leistner, 1995) — multiple *sub-lethal* hurdles (pH, $a_w$, preservatives) combine to give a *lethal* combined effect, while preserving sensory quality.

---

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Choosing a Microbial Control Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Food is liquid?] -->|Yes| Q2[Heat sensitive?]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Solid surface?]
    Q2 -->|No| H[HTST Pasteurisation]
    Q2 -->|Yes| HPP[HPP 600 MPa]
    Q3 -->|Yes| UV[UV-C 254 nm]
    Q3 -->|No| DR[Drying to aw less than 0.65]
    H --> C
    HPP --> C[Cool and Pack]
    UV --> C
    DR --> C
    C --> Final[Microbially Safe Product]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are framed as per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** — Bloom's Level: *Apply (L3) / Analyse (L4) / Evaluate (L5)*.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define D-value and Z-value. State their units and explain their significance in thermal processing of food.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> - **D-value (Decimal Reduction Time):** The time, in minutes, required at a given temperature to reduce the microbial population by 90% (1 log₁₀).  
>   *Unit: minutes.*  
>   *Significance:* Used to calculate the sterilising time required to achieve a target log reduction (e.g., 12-D for *C. botulinum*).
>
> - **Z-value:** The temperature rise (°C) required to reduce the D-value by 90% (1 log).  
>   *Unit: °C.*  
>   *Significance:* Indicates the *thermal resistance* of a microbe; high Z means the organism is more heat-resistant. Standard $z = 10 \text{ °C}$ for *C. botulinum*.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for each definition, 1 mark for unit + significance.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is fumigation? Name two commonly used fumigants for stored grain insect control. State one major drawback of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> - **Fumigation** is the process of releasing a volatile chemical (fumigant) in gaseous form within a sealed enclosure to kill insects, mites, and other pests in stored products.
> - **Two fumigants:**
>   1. **Phosphine (PH₃)** — generated from aluminium phosphide tablets. Drawback: *slow-acting* (requires 5–7 days exposure) and *resistance* is reported in *Sitophilus* and *Tribolium*.
>   2. **Methyl Bromide (CH₃Br)** — broad-spectrum. Drawback: *Ozone-depleting* (Montreal Protocol ban except quarantine use) and *toxic* to operators.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice (Module 4)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> - Students often *confuse* D-value with D₁₀ radiation value. They are numerically similar but **physically different** (thermal vs radiative).  
> - In fumigation numericals, students forget to **convert g/m³ × m³ to mg/L** when comparing with exposure-time tables — losing 2–3 marks.  
> - Always **state units** at every step; missing units = −1 mark in KTU valuation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Internal Choice Option)*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the various physical methods of insect control in stored food products. Discuss the principle of hermetic storage with special reference to PICS bags.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Define $a_w$. Explain its role in microbial control of foods. List the $a_w$ thresholds for bacteria, yeasts, moulds, and xerophilic fungi.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Alternative Choice)*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the principles, methods, and KTU-recommended dose of phosphine fumigation for insect control in stored grains. How is the dose calculated for a given godown volume?** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of hurdle technology. Discuss how combining sub-lethal physical and chemical hurdles achieves microbial stability with better sensory retention in foods.**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### 5.3.1 Model Solution — Question A, Part (a)

**Physical methods of insect control in stored food products (7 marks):**

1. **Temperature manipulation (2 marks)**
   - *Low temperature (< 13 °C):* Insects enter diapause; metabolism halts. Used for cold-stored grains, apples, citrus.
   - *High temperature (> 45 °C for > 24 h):* Heat denatures insect proteins. *Heat disinfestation* of flour mills at 60–80 °C for 16–24 h is an FSSAI-recommended protocol.

2. **Mechanical / Physical exclusion (2 marks)**
   - **Sieving and aspiration** remove insects and broken grains (heavily infested kernels).
   - **Impact entoleter** at ≥ 12,000 rpm in flour mills pulverises insect fragments and eggs to < 75 µm (invisible to consumer).
   - **Hermetic storage (PICS bags):** Triple-layer (HDPE + PP + HDPE) airtight bags create a self-generated modified atmosphere. Insects and grain respire → O₂ drops below 5% in 2–4 weeks → insects die via asphyxiation. **PICS** = *Purdue Improved Crop Storage* (developed by Purdue University, 2007). Effectiveness proven across cowpea, maize, rice in sub-Saharan Africa and Indian tribal grain storage (Telangana, Odisha).

3. **Modified / Controlled Atmosphere (1 mark)**
   - O₂ < 2% OR CO₂ > 60% maintained for > 14 days — kills all life stages of stored-product insects. Used in sealed steel silos for export-grade wheat.

4. **Ionising radiation (1 mark)**
   - **Gamma (⁶⁰Co)** or **e-beam** at **0.2–0.5 kGy** sterilises adults and eggs without raising temperature. Approved under FSSAI Atomic Energy (Radiation Processing of Food and Allied Products) Rules, 2012.

5. **Diatomaceous earth (DE) (1 mark)**
   - Fossilised silica (amorphous SiO₂) abrades insect cuticle, causing dehydration. Used at 1–2 g/kg grain. OMRI-listed, residue-free.

---

### 5.3.2 Model Solution — Question A, Part (b)

**Water Activity ($a_w$) and microbial control (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**  
Water activity is the ratio of partial vapour pressure of water in a food $(P)$ to the vapour pressure of pure water $(P_0)$ at the same temperature:

$$a_w = \dfrac{P}{P_0}$$

It is the *available* water for microbial growth, not the total moisture. A dried food may contain 10% water, but if it is *bound* to sugars/salts, $a_w$ is very low.

**Range:** 0 → 1; pure water = 1.0; perfectly dry food = 0.0.

**Microbial growth thresholds (3 marks):**

| Group | Minimum $a_w$ |
|-------|--------------|
| Most spoilage bacteria | 0.91 |
| Most pathogens (*Salmonella*, *E. coli*, *S. aureus*) | 0.86 – 0.93 |
| Most yeasts | 0.88 |
| Most moulds | 0.80 |
| *Aspergillus flavus* (aflatoxin producer) | 0.78 |
| Xerophilic mould (*Xeromyces bisporus*) | 0.61 |

**Role in microbial control (2 marks):**
- $a_w < 0.60$ → microbially stable (Intermediate Moisture Foods — IMF: jam, honey, dried fruit).
- $a_w < 0.91$ → inhibits spoilage bacteria (dried milk, noodles).
- Adding salt/sugar *binds* water → lowers $a_w$ → preserves food (osmotically).
- Combined with pH and preservatives → **hurdle technology** (Leistner).

---

### 5.3.3 Model Solution — Question B, Part (a)

**Phosphine Fumigation in Stored Grains (7 marks):**

1. **Principle (1.5 marks):** Phosphine (PH₃) is a colourless, flammable gas (ignition temp. 38 °C). It is generated in situ by the reaction of aluminium phosphide (AlP) tablets with atmospheric moisture:

$$2 \text{ AlP} + 6 \text{ H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2 \text{ Al(OH)}_3 + 2 \text{ PH}_3 \uparrow$$

PH₃ acts on the insect's mitochondrial electron transport chain, inhibiting cytochrome c oxidase → cellular asphyxiation.

2. **Procedure (1.5 marks):**
   - Sealed godown or fumigation chamber.
   - Place AlP tablets on trays *above* grain surface.
   - Seal all openings; conduct *gas-tightness test* (pressure drop < ½ inch in 3 min).
   - **Dose:** **1–3 g PH₃ per m³** for **5–7 days** (FAO standard).
   - Ventilate for ≥ 24 h before human entry.

3. **Dose calculation for godown (2 marks):**
   - Volume $V = L \times B \times H$ (m³).
   - Required PH₃ mass $M = C \times V$ (g).
   - Mass of commercial AlP tablets = $M \times 1.705 / \text{purity}$.

4. **Advantages & Disadvantages (2 marks):**
   - **Advantages:** Penetrates bulk grain, kills eggs and adults, residue-free (decomposes to phosphoric acid), economical.
   - **Disadvantages:** Slow (5–7 days), resistance reported, flammable, *not* suitable for commodities with moisture > 14% (fire risk).

---

### 5.3.4 Model Solution — Question B, Part (b)

**Hurdle Technology (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (1.5 marks):**  
   Proposed by Lothar Leistner (1995). Combines *multiple sub-lethal* preservation factors (hurdles) so that their *synergistic* action makes the food *microbially safe and stable* while retaining sensory and nutritional quality.

2. **Common hurdles (2 marks):**

| Hurdle Type | Example |
|-------------|---------|
| Physical | Heat, refrigeration, irradiation, HPP |
| Physico-chemical | $a_w$, pH, redox potential, Eₕ |
| Microbial | Competitive flora (lactic acid bacteria) |
| Chemical | Nisin, sorbate, smoke, spices |
| Packaging | MAP, vacuum, active packaging |

3. **Hurdle principle (1.5 marks):**
   - Each hurdle alone would be *insufficient* (e.g., mild heat + mild salt).
   - Their *synergy* (multiplicative effect) inhibits > log 5 reduction of spoilage + pathogens.
   - Each hurdle can be *gentler*, preserving *texture, colour, vitamins* better than a single harsh treatment.

4. **Examples of hurdle-stabilised foods (2 marks):**
   - **Sausage** → pH (< 5.0) + $a_w$ (< 0.95) + nitrite + smoke.
   - **Bread** → $a_w$ (< 0.94) + pH (< 5.5) + propionic acid.
   - **High-pressure + Nisin + low pH** — for fresh-tasting chilled meats (HPP + bio-preservation).
   - **Intermediate moisture pet food** — $a_w$ 0.75 + sorbate + mild pasteurisation.

---

## 5.4 Quick-Reference Topic Recap & Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Pin this on your study wall before ESE.**

- ✅ **D-value** = time for 90% kill → $D = t / (\log N_0 - \log N)$.
- ✅ **Z-value** = temperature for 90% drop in D → $Z = \Delta T / \Delta \log D$.
- ✅ **F-value (F₀)** = equivalent sterilisation time at $T_{ref} = 121.1 \text{ °C}$, $z = 10 \text{ °C}$.
- ✅ **Q₁₀** = ratio of reaction rates for 10 °C rise; biological reactions ≈ 2–3.
- ✅ **Water activity $a_w$** < 0.60 → microbially stable; safe moisture for cereals < 12–14%.
- ✅ **Insect control hierarchy:** Prevent → Detect → Eradicate → Monitor.
- ✅ **Phosphine dose:** $C = 1–3 \text{ g/m}^3$ for 5–7 days; AlP tablets contain 56% purity.
- ✅ **Fumigation gas-tightness test:** pressure drop < ½ inch water gauge in 3 min.
- ✅ **FSSAI-permitted irradiation dose:** ≤ 1.0 kGy (general), ≤ 0.5 kGy (insect disinfestation).
- ✅ **PICS bags** = hermetic triple-bag (HDPE/PP/HDPE) — kills insects via self-generated MA.
- ✅ **Hurdle technology** = synergistic sub-lethal preservation factors (Leistner, 1995).
- ✅ **Nisin** (E234) = 12.5 ppm max, from *Lactococcus lactis* — a Class II bio-preservative.
- ✅ **F₀ ≥ 3 min** = commercial sterility standard for low-acid canned food.
- ✅ **Microbial growth minima:** Bacteria 0.91, Yeasts 0.88, Moulds 0.80, Xerophiles 0.61.
- ✅ **Phosphine resistance** is a real and rising problem in Indian godowns — rotate with controlled atmosphere.

> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Insect Control & Microbial Control**
> *— Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT614 - POST HARVEST HANDLING OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Post-Harvest Treatments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Post-Harvest Treatments** refer to the sequence of physical, chemical, thermal, and biological unit operations applied to freshly harvested agricultural commodities **between the moment of harvest and the point of final consumption or further processing**. These treatments are aimed at **preserving intrinsic quality, minimizing quantitative and qualitative losses, extending shelf life, ensuring food safety, and preparing the produce for downstream operations** such as storage, packaging, transport, and marketing.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (Course Outcome mapping: **CO4 – Apply appropriate post-harvest treatments to enhance shelf life and quality of food materials**), post-harvest treatments are classified into the following functional families:

| Family | Core Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Sanitation Treatments** | Remove field dirt, pesticide residues, microbial load |
| **Conditioning Treatments** | Remove field heat, initiate curing, stabilize metabolism |
| **Protective Treatments** | Create barriers against moisture loss, gas exchange, pathogens |
| **Therapeutic Treatments** | Kill insects, fungi, pathogens, inhibit sprouting |
| **Surface Treatments** | Waxing, coating, film application |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a harvested fruit as a hospital patient that has just undergone major surgery.**
> 
> The field heat inside the produce is like a **fever** — it accelerates decay. The bruises and micro-wounds from harvesting are like **open surgical incisions** vulnerable to infection. The natural respiration is a **metabolic burn** consuming the patient's reserves.
> 
> **Post-harvest treatments are the ICU protocols** applied to stabilize the patient:
> - *Pre-cooling* = bringing down the fever (lowering field heat)
> - *Cleaning/Washing* = antiseptic wash of the wounds
> - *Curing* = bandaging and skin grafting (healing the cuticle)
> - *Waxing* = applying a protective dressing
> - *Heat treatment* = antibiotic course
> - *Cold storage* = placing in a controlled, sterile environment
> 
> Just as a doctor chooses treatments based on the patient's condition, the food technologist selects post-harvest treatments based on the **commodity, target market distance, intended shelf life, and regulatory constraints**.

## 1.3 Why Post-Harvest Treatments Are Critical — The Loss Statistics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Quantitative post-harvest losses in developing countries (including India): **5–25 %** for cereals, **20–40 %** for fruits and vegetables, and **up to 50 %** for perishables in tropical climates.
> - WHO/FAO acceptable post-harvest loss benchmark: **≤ 5 %**.
> - The **United Nations Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 12.3** targets a **50 % reduction in food waste and losses by 2030** — making post-harvest treatments a strategic global priority.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics

- **Specific heat of fresh produce (Cp):** typically **3.0 – 3.7 kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹** (close to that of water).
- **Respiration rate (R):** measured in **mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹** (classification: very low < 5; low 5–10; moderate 10–20; high 20–40; very high > 40).
- **Thermal conductivity of produce:** approximately **0.3 – 0.5 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹**.
- **Surface heat transfer coefficient (h) in cold storage:** typically **5 – 25 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹** (forced convection).
- **Standard chlorination level for wash water:** **50 – 200 ppm free chlorine**.
- **Standard wax application rate:** **1 – 2 mg·cm⁻²** of fruit surface.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Post-Harvest Treatments — Structured Logic Tree

### A. Cleaning and Sanitation Treatments
**Why needed:** Field-harvested produce carries soil, dust, insect fragments, pesticide residues, latex, and a high microbial load (10⁴ – 10⁷ CFU·g⁻¹ on surfaces).

**How it works:**
1. **Dry cleaning** – brushing, air blasting, sieving (used for root crops, dry grains).
2. **Wet washing** – spray washing, immersion washing, flotation washing.
3. **Disinfectant wash** – chlorinated water, ozonated water, hydrogen peroxide, peroxyacetic acid, organic acids (citric, lactic).
4. **Ultrasonic cleaning** – high-frequency sound waves (20–40 kHz) generate cavitation bubbles that dislodge contaminants.

### B. Sorting and Grading
**Why needed:** Ensures uniformity, removes defective units, facilitates marketing and price realization.

**How it works:**
- **Size grading** – using perforated screens, diverging belts, roller sorters.
- **Weight grading** – using load cells.
- **Color grading** – using computer vision / optical sorters.
- **Defect sorting** – manual or machine vision (canker, bruise, insect damage).
- **Density grading** – flotation in brine/sugar solutions.

### C. Pre-cooling
**Why needed:** Field heat removal is the **single most important step** for perishables. For every 1-hour delay at 30 °C, the shelf life can reduce by **1 day** (for highly perishable items).

**Methods:**
1. **Room cooling** – produce placed in cold room (slow, 20–24 h).
2. **Forced-air cooling** – chilled air forced through produce stack (4–6 h).
3. **Hydrocooling** – cold water shower/immersion (fastest, 0.5–1 h).
4. **Vacuum cooling** – produce placed in vacuum chamber, water evaporates at low pressure absorbing latent heat (15–30 min for leafy vegetables).
5. **Ice cooling** – crushed ice or slurry ice (suitable for broccoli, sweet corn).
6. **Package icing** – ice in shipping container.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7/8ths Cooling Time** (or **Half-Cooling Time, HCT**) is the standard metric for pre-cooler performance — defined in the formula sheet below.

### D. Curing
**Why needed:** Root, tuber, and bulb crops suffer skin cuts and abrasions during harvest. Curing promotes **wound healing** through suberization (deposition of suberin) and formation of wound periderm.

**How it works:**
- Potatoes: **10–15 °C, 85–90 % RH, 10–14 days**.
- Sweet potatoes: **30–32 °C, 85–90 % RH, 4–7 days**.
- Onions and garlic: **35–45 °C, 60–75 % RH, 12–24 h** (external curing for neck tightening).
- **Curing loss = Weight loss during curing = (W_initial − W_final) / W_initial × 100 %**

### E. Drying / Dehydration
**Why needed:** Reduces water activity (a_w) below the threshold for microbial and enzymatic activity. Critical for grains, pulses, spices, fruits, and vegetables.

**Methods:** sun drying, hot air drying, fluidized bed drying, spray drying, freeze drying, osmotic dehydration, microwave drying, infrared drying.

### F. Waxing and Coating
**Why needed:** Replaces the natural cuticle wax removed during washing; creates a **modified atmosphere (MA)** around the fruit, reducing transpiration and respiration.

**Common waxes:** Carnauba wax, beeswax, paraffin wax, shellac, polyethylene wax.

### G. Chemical Treatments
**Why needed:** Control postharvest diseases, delay ripening, inhibit sprouting, regulate ethylene.

**Examples:**
- **Fungicides:** TBZ (thiabendazole), imazalil, ortho-phenylphenol.
- **Growth regulators:** 1-MCP (1-methylcyclopropene) — blocks ethylene receptors.
- **Sprout inhibitors:** CIPC (chlorpropham) — used on potatoes.
- **Ripening agents:** Ethephon (2-chloroethylphosphonic acid) — releases ethylene.

### H. Heat Treatments
**Why needed:** Disinfestation of quarantine pests, disease control, delay ripening, improve fruit quality.

**Methods:**
1. **Hot Water Treatment (HWT)** – mangoes at **52–55 °C for 5–15 min**.
2. **Vapor Heat Treatment (VHT)** – fruits heated by saturated water vapor at **40–46 °C for 4–8 h** (for mangoes against fruit fly).
3. **Hot Air Treatment (HAT)** – **38–43 °C for 12–96 h** (for citrus, papaya).
4. **Radio Frequency / Microwave Heating** – volumetric heating for rapid, uniform temperature rise.

### I. Cold Treatments
**Why needed:** Insect disinfestation for quarantine purposes and extension of shelf life.

**Examples:** Citrus fruits at **0–2 °C for 14–21 days** to kill Mediterranean fruit fly eggs/larvae.

### J. Irradiation
**Why needed:** Phytosanitary disinfestation, sprout inhibition, microbial decontamination.

**Dose categories (per IAEA/Codex Alimentarius):**
- Low dose (**≤ 1 kGy**) – sprout inhibition, insect disinfestation.
- Medium dose (**1 – 10 kGy**) – pathogen reduction, shelf-life extension.
- High dose (**> 10 kGy**) – sterilization (industrial use only).

### K. Edible Coatings and Films
**Why needed:** Eco-friendly alternative to waxing; uses biopolymers (chitosan, alginate, starch, carboxymethyl cellulose, pullulan).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — Memorise these equations for Part A and Part B questions.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture Content (wet basis, w.b.) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100$ | % | Fresh fruits/veg: 70–95 % |
| 2 | Moisture Content (dry basis, d.b.) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d} \times 100$ | % | Used in drying calculations |
| 3 | Conversion between bases | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$ | % | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{db}}{100 + M_{db}} \times 100$ |
| 4 | Water Activity | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | dimensionless | Microbial growth threshold: 0.60–0.91 |
| 5 | Half-Cooling Time (HCT / 7/8 Cooling Time) | $\dfrac{T_t - T_f}{T_i - T_f} = 0.5^{\,t / HCT}$ | dimensionless | $t_{7/8} = 3 \times HCT$ |
| 6 | Cooling Time (general Newtonian) | $t = \dfrac{m \cdot C_p}{h \cdot A} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_i - T_a}{T_t - T_a}\right)$ | s | Used in pre-cooling design |
| 7 | Respiration Heat Generation | $q_{resp} = R \cdot \Delta H_{CO_2}$ | W·kg⁻¹ | $\Delta H_{CO_2} \approx 10.7$ kJ·L⁻¹ CO₂ |
| 8 | Curing Loss | $CL = \dfrac{W_i - W_f}{W_i} \times 100$ | % | Acceptable: 3–6 % |
| 9 | Drying Rate (thin layer) | $\dfrac{dM}{dt} = -k \cdot (M - M_e)$ | % moisture / h | Lewis/Newton model |
| 10 | Heat Transfer — Conduction (Fourier) | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{L}$ | W | $k$ produce ≈ 0.4 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| 11 | Latent Heat of Vaporization (water) | $\lambda_w \approx 2400 - 2.4\,T$ | kJ·kg⁻¹ | At 0 °C: ~2500 kJ·kg⁻¹ |
| 12 | Modified Atmosphere — Steady State O₂ | $[O_2]_{ss} = \dfrac{R_{O_2}}{k_{O_2}}$ | % | Through film permeability |
| 13 | Steady State Respiration Ratio (R/R₀) | $\dfrac{R}{R_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{\,k_1([O_2] - [O_2]_{c})}}$ | – | Chinnici modification |
| 14 | Weibull Inactivation (pathogen kill) | $\log(N/N_0) = -\left(\dfrac{t}{\delta}\right)^p$ | log CFU | Used in HWT validation |
| 15 | Clausius–Clapeyron (vapor pressure over food) | $\ln\!\dfrac{p}{p_0} = -\dfrac{\lambda}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)$ | Pa | Used in isotherm modeling |
| 16 | Reynolds Number (airflow in cooler) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Laminar < 2300, Turbulent > 4000 |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pre-cooling + MA packaging** is the backbone of the global fresh produce cold chain.
- **HWT + VHT** allows Indian mangoes to access **USA, Japan, Australia, New Zealand** markets by satisfying their phytosanitary regulations.
- **Curing** is the single reason Indian onions and potatoes can be stored for **3–6 months** under ambient conditions.
- **1-MCP treatment** has revolutionized apple storage, extending controlled-atmosphere (CA) storage life to **up to 12 months**.
- **Irradiation** enables **onion sprout inhibition** commercially in India (KRUSHAK facility, Lasalgaon, Nashik).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Half-Cooling Time (HCT) from Newton's Law of Cooling

**Starting Principle:** The rate of temperature change of a body is proportional to the temperature difference between the body and the surrounding medium.

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = -k\,(T - T_a)$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange and integrate with initial condition $T(0) = T_i$:**

$$\int_{T_i}^{T_t} \frac{dT}{T - T_a} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 2 — Perform the integration:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T_t - T_a}{T_i - T_a}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate to obtain the standard Newtonian cooling equation:**

$$\frac{T_t - T_a}{T_i - T_a} = e^{-k t}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the definition of Half-Cooling Time (HCT), i.e., the time required for the temperature difference to reduce to one-half of its initial value:**

At $t = HCT$, $\dfrac{T_t - T_a}{T_i - T_a} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k \cdot HCT}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for HCT:**

$$HCT = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}$$

**Step 6 — Generalize by substituting back the expression for $k$:**

$$k = \frac{\ln\!\left(\frac{T_i - T_a}{T_t - T_a}\right)}{t} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t = \frac{0.693}{HCT} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{T_i - T_a}{T_t - T_a}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Derive the 7/8 Cooling Time:**

For $\dfrac{T_t - T_a}{T_i - T_a} = \dfrac{1}{8}$:

$$t_{7/8} = \frac{\ln 8}{k} = \frac{3 \ln 2}{k} = 3 \times HCT$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** The 7/8 cooling time is a standard industry benchmark — a produce pallet is considered "adequately cooled" when 7/8 of the field-to-storage temperature difference has been removed, i.e., three HCT cycles have elapsed.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Pre-cooling of Mangoes by Forced Air

**Problem Statement:** A batch of **1000 kg of mangoes** is to be pre-cooled from an initial pulp temperature $T_i = 30\ ^\circ\text{C}$ to a final temperature $T_t = 10\ ^\circ\text{C}$ using forced-air cooling. The cold air temperature is $T_a = 5\ ^\circ\text{C}$. The half-cooling time is experimentally determined as $HCT = 1.5$ hours. Specific heat of mango $C_p = 3.5$ kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹.

**Given Data Table:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass of mangoes | $m$ | 1000 | kg |
| Initial temperature | $T_i$ | 30 | °C |
| Final temperature | $T_t$ | 10 | °C |
| Air temperature | $T_a$ | 5 | °C |
| Half-cooling time | $HCT$ | 1.5 | h |
| Specific heat | $C_p$ | 3.5 | kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| Respiration rate | $R$ | 25 | mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹ |
| Heat of respiration | $\Delta H$ | 10.7 | kJ·L⁻¹ CO₂ |

**Step 1 — Compute the 7/8 cooling time:**

$$t_{7/8} = 3 \times HCT = 3 \times 1.5 = 4.5\ \text{h}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the actual time needed to reach 10 °C:**

The dimensionless temperature ratio is:

$$\frac{T_t - T_a}{T_i - T_a} = \frac{10 - 5}{30 - 5} = \frac{5}{25} = 0.20$$

From the general formula:

$$t = \frac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_i - T_a}{T_t - T_a}\right)}{0.693 / HCT} = \frac{HCT \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_i - T_a}{T_t - T_a}\right)}{0.693}$$

$$t = \frac{1.5 \times \ln(25/5)}{0.693} = \frac{1.5 \times \ln(5)}{0.693} = \frac{1.5 \times 1.6094}{0.693}$$

$$t = \frac{2.414}{0.693} \approx 3.48\ \text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the sensible heat to be removed:**

$$Q_s = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_i - T_t) = 1000 \times 3.5 \times (30 - 10) = 70{,}000\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the heat generated by respiration during cooling:**

The volumetric respiration heat per kg per hour:

$$q_{resp,\,per\,kg} = R \times \Delta H = \frac{25 \times 10.7}{1000} = 0.2675\ \text{kJ}\cdot\text{kg}^{-1}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$$

Over the cooling time $t = 3.48$ h:

$$Q_{resp} = m \times q_{resp,\,per\,kg} \times t = 1000 \times 0.2675 \times 3.48$$

$$Q_{resp} = 930.9\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the total refrigeration load:**

$$Q_{total} = Q_s + Q_{resp} = 70{,}000 + 930.9 = 70{,}931\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the required refrigeration capacity:**

$$P = \frac{Q_{total}}{t} = \frac{70{,}931}{3.48 \times 3600} = \frac{70{,}931}{12{,}528} \approx 5.66\ \text{kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The respiration heat accounts for only ~1.3 % of the total load in this example. However, for **highly perishable commodities** (strawberries, mushrooms, broccoli) with very high respiration rates, the respiration load can rise to **5–10 %** of the total refrigeration load and must not be ignored.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Drying Rate from Thin-Layer Drying Model

**Starting Principle:** The Lewis / Newton thin-layer drying model assumes that the drying rate is proportional to the free moisture content:

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = -k\,(M - M_e)$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables:**

$$\frac{dM}{M - M_e} = -k\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $M_0$ (at $t=0$) to $M$ (at $t$):**

$$\int_{M_0}^{M} \frac{dM}{M - M_e} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}\right) = -k\,t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the moisture ratio $MR$:**

$$MR = \frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = e^{-k\,t}$$

**Step 5 — Express in logarithmic form for graphical analysis:**

$$-\ln(MR) = k \cdot t$$

A plot of $-\ln(MR)$ versus $t$ yields a straight line of slope $k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** The drying constant $k$ depends on **drying air temperature, velocity, and relative humidity**. Higher $T$ and higher $v$ → larger $k$ → faster drying. The Page model adds an empirical exponent $n$: $MR = e^{-k t^n}$, providing better fit for many biological materials.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Curing Loss of Onions

**Problem Statement:** A lot of **500 kg of freshly harvested red onions** is subjected to curing at **40 °C and 70 % RH for 24 h**. After curing, the lot weighs **478 kg**.

**Step 1 — Calculate the curing loss:**

$$CL = \frac{W_i - W_f}{W_i} \times 100 = \frac{500 - 478}{500} \times 100 = \frac{22}{500} \times 100 = 4.4\ \%$$

**Step 2 — Verdict:**
- Acceptable range: **3 – 6 %** → curing was successful.
- A higher loss (> 8 %) would indicate over-curing, undesirable for storage.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Post-Harvest Treatment

| Commodity | Primary Spoilage Cause | Recommended Treatment | Operating Conditions | Expected Benefit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mango | Anthracnose, fruit fly, ripening | Wash → HWT → VHT → Wax → Cold storage | HWT 52 °C, 10 min; VHT 46 °C, 4 h; 13 °C, 85–90 % RH | Shelf life 14–28 days |
| Apple | Scald, senescence | 1-MCP treatment + CA storage | 1 µL·L⁻¹ 1-MCP, 18 h; 0–1 °C, 2 % O₂, 1 % CO₂ | Storage 6–12 months |
| Potato | Sprouting, weight loss | Curing + CIPC treatment + cold storage | 10–13 °C, 90–95 % RH; CIPC 25 g·t⁻¹ | Storage 5–6 months |
| Onion | Sprouting, rotting | Curing + low-dose irradiation | 0–2 °C, 65–70 % RH; 60–90 Gy irradiation | Storage 5–8 months |
| Citrus | Green/blue mold, stem-end rot | Wax + fungicide (TBZ/imazalil) + cold storage | 4–8 °C, 85–90 % RH; 500–1000 ppm TBZ | Storage 2–4 months |
| Grape | Gray mold (Botrytis) | SO₂ fumigation + MAP | 0 °C, 90–95 % RH; SO₂ 0.5 % for 20 min, then 0.05 % | Storage 2–3 months |
| Tomato | Over-ripening, decay | Sorting → Pre-cooling → MAP | 12–15 °C (mature green); 10–12 °C (pink) | Storage 2–4 weeks |
| Banana | Crown rot, chilling injury | Crown wash + chemical treatment + ripening | 13–15 °C, 90–95 % RH; 1000 ppm TBZ | Shelf life 7–14 days |
| Pomegranate | Internal breakdown, gray mold | Hot water dip + MA storage | HWT 45 °C, 4 min; 5 °C, 95 % RH | Storage 3–4 months |
| Mushroom | Browning, wilting | Vacuum cooling + MAP | 0–2 °C; 100–150 µm PE film | Shelf life 7–10 days |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define pre-cooling. List any four methods of pre-cooling with one-line applications.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Pre-cooling** is the rapid removal of field heat from freshly harvested produce prior to storage, transport, or processing, in order to slow down respiration, retard microbial growth, and reduce transpiration losses.

**Four methods with applications:**

| Method | Application |
|---|---|
| 1. Room cooling | General purpose; slow; suitable for apples, citrus |
| 2. Forced-air cooling | Fast; suitable for strawberries, grapes, mushrooms |
| 3. Hydrocooling | Fastest for dense produce; sweet corn, melons, mangoes |
| 4. Vacuum cooling | Ultra-fast for leafy vegetables; lettuce, spinach |

**Marks Distribution (as per KTU 2024 pattern):**
- Definition – 1 mark
- Four methods with one-line application – 2 marks

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is curing? Explain its significance in the storage of onions and potatoes.**

**Model Answer:**

**Curing** is a post-harvest treatment involving the exposure of freshly harvested root, tuber, and bulb crops to specific temperature and humidity conditions for a defined period to promote wound healing through suberization and periderm formation.

**Significance:**
- For **onions**: Curing tightens the neck, dries the outer scales, reduces microbial entry, and extends storage life from 1 month to 5–8 months.
- For **potatoes**: Curing heals harvest injuries, reduces water loss, prevents infection by soft-rot bacteria, and forms 2–3 layers of wound periderm.
- Typical weight loss during curing: **3–6 %** (acceptable).

**Marks Distribution:**
- Definition – 1 mark
- Significance for onions – 1 mark
- Significance for potatoes – 1 mark

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the principles, methods, and applications of **post-harvest heat treatments** for horticultural crops. Discuss the mechanism of insect disinfestation and disease control achieved by these treatments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **forced-air pre-cooling** operation. Derive the **half-cooling time** equation and calculate the time required to cool 1000 kg of grapes from 28 °C to 5 °C in a forced-air cooler operating at 2 °C. The half-cooling time for grapes is 0.8 h. Take $C_p = 3.6$ kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹ and respiration rate = 12 mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Principles:**
- Heat treatments rely on the differential sensitivity of host tissue and target pest/pathogen to elevated temperatures.
- Insects in quarantine undergo thermal death at temperatures that are **sub-lethal** to the host commodity.
- Heat accelerates wound healing, induces synthesis of heat-shock proteins (HSPs), and triggers production of antifungal compounds like PR-proteins.

**Methods:**

| Method | Temperature | Duration | Commodity | Target |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hot Water Dip (HWT) | 52–55 °C | 5–15 min | Mango, papaya, citrus | Anthracnose, fruit fly eggs |
| Vapor Heat (VHT) | 40–46 °C | 4–8 h | Mango, papaya | Mediterranean fruit fly, melon fly |
| Hot Air (HAT) | 38–43 °C | 12–96 h | Citrus, apple | Quarantine pests |
| Radio Frequency | Internal 48–52 °C | Seconds–minutes | In-shell nuts, legumes | Internal insects |

**Mechanism of Insect Disinfestation:**
- Heat denatures structural and enzymatic proteins of insects.
- Disrupts membrane integrity causing electrolyte leakage.
- Depletes ATP reserves and causes cellular dehydration.
- Causes thermal death of eggs, larvae, and pupae stages.

**Mechanism of Disease Control:**
- Heat inactivates fungal spores and bacterial cells.
- Induces host resistance through PR-proteins (chitinase, β-1,3-glucanase).
- Activates phenylpropanoid pathway → phenolics, lignin.

**Marks Distribution (7 marks):**
- Principles – 2 marks
- Methods table – 2 marks
- Mechanism of insect kill – 1.5 marks
- Mechanism of disease control – 1.5 marks

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Flow Diagram for Forced-Air Pre-cooling:**

(As in Section 4.2 of these notes.)

**Derivation of HCT:**

Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. Final formula:

$$HCT = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = 0.693 / k$$

**Step 1 — Given data table:**

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $m$ | 1000 | kg |
| $T_i$ | 28 | °C |
| $T_t$ | 5 | °C |
| $T_a$ | 2 | °C |
| $HCT$ | 0.8 | h |
| $C_p$ | 3.6 | kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| $R$ | 12 | mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹ |
| $\Delta H$ | 10.7 | kJ·L⁻¹ CO₂ |

**Step 2 — Temperature ratio:**

$$\frac{T_t - T_a}{T_i - T_a} = \frac{5 - 2}{28 - 2} = \frac{3}{26} = 0.1154$$

**Step 3 — Cooling time:**

$$t = \frac{HCT \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_i - T_a}{T_t - T_a}\right)}{\ln 2} = \frac{0.8 \times \ln(26/3)}{0.693}$$

$$\ln(8.667) = 2.159$$

$$t = \frac{0.8 \times 2.159}{0.693} = \frac{1.727}{0.693} \approx 2.49\ \text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Sensible heat load:**

$$Q_s = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 1000 \times 3.6 \times (28 - 5) = 1000 \times 3.6 \times 23 = 82{,}800\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Respiration heat load:**

$$q_{resp} = R \times \Delta H = \frac{12 \times 10.7}{1000} = 0.1284\ \text{kJ}\cdot\text{kg}^{-1}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$$

$$Q_{resp} = 1000 \times 0.1284 \times 2.49 = 319.7\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 6 — Total load:**

$$Q_{total} = 82{,}800 + 319.7 = 83{,}119.7\ \text{kJ}$$

**Marks Distribution (7 marks):**
- Flow diagram – 2 marks
- Derivation – 1 mark
- Calculation of cooling time – 2 marks
- Calculation of refrigeration load – 2 marks

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**(a)** What is **waxing**? List the commonly used waxes in the food industry. Discuss the benefits and limitations of waxing with respect to fresh fruits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **principles, working, and applications of irradiation** as a post-harvest treatment. Discuss the dose classifications as per **Codex Alimentarius / IAEA** guidelines. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:**
**Waxing** is a post-harvest surface treatment in which a thin layer (typically 1–2 mg·cm⁻²) of edible wax is applied to the surface of fresh fruits to replace the natural cuticle removed during washing, reduce transpiration, enhance gloss, and extend shelf life.

**Commonly Used Waxes:**

| Wax | Source | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Carnauba wax | Palm leaves | Apples, citrus, mangoes |
| Beeswax | Honeycomb | Apples, pears |
| Paraffin wax | Petroleum | Citrus |
| Shellac | Lac insect secretion | Apples, citrus |
| Polyethylene wax | Synthetic | Citrus, melons |
| Candeuba | Modified carnauba | Tomatoes, cucumbers |

**Benefits:**
- Reduces weight loss by **30–50 %**.
- Enhances visual gloss and consumer appeal.
- Reduces shrinkage and surface abrasion.
- Acts as a carrier for fungicides (e.g., TBZ, imazalil).
- Modifies internal atmosphere → delays senescence.

**Limitations:**
- May cause anaerobic respiration if applied too thickly → off-flavor development.
- Blocks natural gas exchange in some commodities.
- May trap heat and accelerate spoilage if applied before cooling.
- Wax removal at consumer end may be necessary (regulatory in some countries).
- Allergic concerns in sensitive individuals.

**Marks Distribution (7 marks):**
- Definition – 1 mark
- List of waxes – 1.5 marks
- Benefits – 2.5 marks
- Limitations – 2 marks

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Definition:**
**Irradiation** is a non-thermal post-harvest treatment that exposes food to controlled doses of ionizing radiation (gamma rays from Co-60 or Cs-137, X-rays, or electron beams) to control spoilage, eliminate pathogens, and extend shelf life.

**Principles:**
- Ionizing radiation interacts with water in the food, producing free radicals (·OH, ·H, e⁻_aq).
- These free radicals damage the DNA of microorganisms and insect cells.
- Damaged DNA cannot replicate → cell death or reproductive failure.
- In plants, irradiation disrupts meristematic cells → inhibits sprouting (onion, potato).

**Working Procedure:**
1. **Source:** Co-60 gamma source (most common) or e-beam / X-ray.
2. **Dose delivery:** Product is conveyed past the source on a mono-rail at controlled speed.
3. **Dosimetry:** Fricke or alanine dosimeters measure absorbed dose.
4. **Process validation:** Dose mapping, dose uniformity ratio (DUR ≤ 1.5).
5. **Labeling:** Treated foods must display the **"Radura" international logo** in many countries.

**Dose Classification (Codex Alimentarius / IAEA):**

| Category | Dose Range | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Low dose | ≤ 1 kGy | Sprout inhibition (potato, onion), insect disinfestation, delay ripening |
| Medium dose | 1 – 10 kGy | Pathogen reduction, shelf-life extension, parasite control |
| High dose | > 10 kGy | Sterilization (industrial / pet food use only) |

**Applications:**
- India: **KRUSHAK irradiation facility at Lasalgaon, Nashik** for onion and potato.
- USA, Mexico, Brazil: Commercial irradiation of spices, meat, poultry.
- International trade: Phytosanitary irradiation for mango, citrus, guava.

**Marks Distribution (7 marks):**
- Principle – 2 marks
- Working – 2 marks
- Dose classifications – 1.5 marks
- Applications – 1.5 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Confusing moisture content bases** (w.b. vs. d.b.) — always check the basis stated in the problem. Marks deducted if the wrong base is used in drying calculations.
> 2. **Forgetting the respiration heat term** in refrigeration load — examiners specifically look for $Q_{resp}$. Omitting it leads to a -1 mark deduction.
> 3. **Using T in °C inside logarithm arguments** — temperature in $e^{-k t}$ must be in **K** (or use temperature differences in °C, which is valid since the conversion cancels).
> 4. **Conflating HWT and VHT** — HWT is hot water; VHT is hot vapor (different heat transfer coefficients, different temperature ranges).
> 5. **Skipping the flow diagram** in pre-cooling questions — diagrams carry **2 marks** and are non-negotiable in KTU 2024.
> 6. **Memorising only names, not doses/conditions** — examiners expect specific values (e.g., 52 °C / 10 min for mango HWT). Vague answers lose 50 % marks.
> 7. **Writing "irradiation is radioactive" in definition** — this is a conceptual error. The food does **not become radioactive**; only its biological components are affected.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 4 — Post-Harvest Treatments: Last-Minute Revision**

- ✅ **Cleaning** is the first sanitization step; **chlorine (50–200 ppm)** is the most common sanitizer.
- ✅ **Sorting and grading** precede treatments; visual/optical/electronic sorters are industry standard.
- ✅ **Pre-cooling** removes **field heat**; methods ranked by speed: Vacuum > Hydro > Forced-air > Room.
- ✅ **Half-Cooling Time (HCT)** formula: $HCT = \ln 2 / k$; **7/8 cooling time = 3 × HCT**.
- ✅ **Curing** is for **root/tuber/bulb crops**; promotes suberin and periderm formation; typical loss 3–6 %.
- ✅ **Waxing** replaces natural cuticle; carnauba, beeswax, paraffin, shellac, PE wax are common.
- ✅ **HWT** for mango: **52–55 °C / 5–15 min**; **VHT**: **46 °C / 4 h** — required for **US/Japan market access**.
- ✅ **1-MCP** blocks ethylene receptors → delays ripening in apples, bananas, tomatoes.
- ✅ **CIPC (chlorpropham)** is the standard **sprout inhibitor** for potatoes.
- ✅ **Irradiation dose bands** (per Codex): Low ≤ 1 kGy; Medium 1–10 kGy; High > 10 kGy.
- ✅ **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)** uses **perforated PE/PP films** to maintain **2–5 % O₂, 3–10 % CO₂** in-package.
- ✅ **Respiration heat** must always be added to **sensible heat** when computing refrigeration load: $Q_{total} = Q_s + Q_{resp}$.
- ✅ **Water activity (a_w)** thresholds: Bacteria < 0.91, Yeasts < 0.88, Moulds < 0.80, Xerophilic moulds < 0.60.
- ✅ **Curing temperature for potatoes** is **10–15 °C** (not 30 °C) — common confusion with sweet potato.
- ✅ **Vapour heat treatment (VHT)** uses **saturated steam** — high humidity prevents surface drying and weight loss.
- ✅ **Hot water treatment (HWT)** must be **followed by hydrocooling** to avoid cooking injury.
- ✅ **Green bananas** are stored at **13–15 °C** (NOT below 13 °C — they suffer **chilling injury**).
- ✅ **Citrus waxes + TBZ/imazalil** is the **standard commercial combination** for post-harvest decay control.
- ✅ **Pineapples** have very low respiration; **strawberries** have very high respiration — treat differently.
- ✅ **Irradiated food does NOT become radioactive** — clarify this in any viva/definition.
- ✅ **Edible coatings** (chitosan, alginate, starch) are **eco-friendly** alternatives to waxing and align with **SDG 12.3** targets.
- ✅ **Forced-air cooling** reduces cooling time by **4–10×** compared to room cooling.
- ✅ **HCT for grapes** in cold air is typically **0.5 – 1.5 h**; for apples **3 – 6 h**; for mangoes **1 – 2 h**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GAP, GHP, GMP, HACCP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT614 - POST HARVEST HANDLING OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** GAP, GHP, GMP, HACCP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Good Agricultural Practices (GAP)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
**Good Agricultural Practices (GAP)** are a collection of principles, regulations, and technical recommendations applicable to on-farm production and post-production processes. They are designed to ensure food safety, quality, environmental sustainability, and worker welfare from the **pre-harvest stage through harvest**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** GAP applies to the **farm-level** activities, including soil management, water use, pest control, harvesting, and on-farm storage. It is the **first line of defense** in the food safety chain.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of GAP as the **"discipline of the farmer"**. Just like a disciplined student maintains hygiene, notes, and health to perform well in exams, a disciplined farmer follows protocols on irrigation water quality, pesticide usage limits, and harvest hygiene. If the farmer cuts corners (uses dirty water, sprays excessive pesticides, harvests into dirty containers), the food becomes unsafe before it even reaches the factory. GAP is essentially the **"rules of the field"**.

---

## 1.2 Good Hygienic Practices (GHP)

### Formal Definition
**Good Hygienic Practices (GHP)** are the foundational sanitary and hygiene conditions that must be maintained throughout the **food chain** to produce safe and suitable food. GHP covers personal hygiene of food handlers, cleaning and sanitation of equipment, pest control, and prevention of cross-contamination.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **hospital operating room**. Before surgery, doctors scrub their hands, sterilize instruments, and wear gloves and masks. GHP is the **"operating theatre protocol"** of the food industry. Even the best surgeon cannot operate safely without hygiene protocols. Similarly, even the best recipe fails without GHP.

---

## 1.3 Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP)

### Formal Definition
**Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP)** are the operational and procedural requirements (often codified by WHO, USFDA, FSSAI, Codex Alimentarius) that govern the **manufacturing, packaging, storage, and distribution** of food products. GMP ensures that food products are consistently produced and controlled to quality standards appropriate to their intended use.

### Conceptual Analogy
GMP is like the **"rulebook of a factory"**. Just as an automobile factory has standardized assembly-line steps to ensure every car leaving the line is roadworthy, GMP ensures every food product leaving the processing facility is **safe, consistent, and correctly labeled**.

---

## 1.4 Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP)

### Formal Definition
**HACCP** is a **systematic, science-based, preventive** food safety management system that identifies, evaluates, and controls hazards that are significant for food safety. It follows the **seven principles** established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission and focuses on **preventing** rather than detecting food safety problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin:** HACCP was developed in the 1960s by **Pillsbury, NASA, and the US Army Natick Laboratories** to ensure 100% food safety for space missions. It is now globally mandated under FSSAI 2006 (India), US FDA 21 CFR Part 117, EU Regulation 852/2004, and Codex Alimentarius.

### Conceptual Analogy
HACCP is like the **"checklist of a pilot before takeoff"**. A pilot does not just hope the plane is safe — they verify fuel, instruments, controls, and weather at each **critical point**. If any check fails, the flight is aborted. HACCP works identically: it identifies **critical control points (CCPs)** and ensures hazards are controlled *before* they reach the consumer.

---

## 1.5 The Relationship Hierarchy

These four systems are **NOT interchangeable**. They form a **layered, hierarchical pyramid** of food safety assurance:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy (Bottom-to-Top):**
> 1. **GAP** (Farm-level foundation)
> 2. **GHP** (Operational hygiene foundation)
> 3. **GMP** (Manufacturing compliance foundation)
