> 4. **HACCP** (Hazard-specific prevention system built on top of the above three)

A food business cannot successfully implement HACCP without first having **GAP, GHP, and GMP** in place. These are called **prerequisite programmes (PRPs)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Good Agricultural Practices (GAP) — Detailed Breakdown

### Core Operational Pillars of GAP

| Pillar | Key Operational Element | Real-World Example |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------|
| **Soil Management** | Avoiding heavy metal contamination, maintaining soil organic carbon | Testing soil for lead near highways |
| **Water Quality** | Microbial testing of irrigation water (E. coli < 1000 CFU/100 mL) | Using bore-well water vs. river water |
| **Pest & Disease Control** | IPM (Integrated Pest Management), MRL compliance for pesticides | Using pheromone traps instead of excess sprays |
| **Harvest Hygiene** | Clean containers, worker hygiene, time-temperature control | Harvesting mangoes into food-grade crates, not gunny bags |
| **On-farm Storage** | Pest-proof stores, moisture control | Hermetic storage of grains |
| **Traceability** | Farm-to-fork record-keeping | Batch codes on crates |

### KTU Formula / Metric Sheet — GAP

| Metric | Standard / Limit | Authority |
|--------|------------------|-----------|
| Irrigation water E. coli | **< 1000 CFU/100 mL** | WHO / Codex |
| Pesticide MRL | Commodity-specific (e.g., Apple MRL = 1 mg/kg for captan) | FSSAI / Codex |
| Pre-harvest interval (PHI) | Days between spray and harvest | Label-driven |
| Field heat removal | **< 4 hours** from harvest to cold storage | Cold chain KPIs |

---

## 2.2 Good Hygienic Practices (GHP) — Detailed Breakdown

### The 8 Pillars of GHP (Codex Alimentarius Reference)

1. **Primary Production Hygiene** — Clean farm environment
2. **Establishment Design & Facilities** — Layout to prevent cross-contamination (forward flow of material)
3. **Control of Operations** — Time-temperature control, allergen segregation
4. **Establishment: Maintenance & Sanitation** — CIP (Clean-In-Place) systems
5. **Establishment: Personal Hygiene** — Hand-washing stations, gloves, hairnets, jewellery ban
6. **Transportation** — Refrigerated trucks, sealed containers
7. **Product Information & Consumer Awareness** — Labels, allergens, date marks
8. **Training** — Hygiene training for every food handler

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pearl:** GHP is often **synonymous with "Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures" (SSOPs)** in the meat and seafood industries.

---

## 2.3 Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP) — Detailed Breakdown

### Key GMP Components

| Component | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Premises & Layout** | Smooth floors, drainage, no wood surfaces, insect-proof screens |
| **Equipment Design** | Stainless steel SS304/SS316, no dead legs in pipelines |
| **Raw Material Control** | Vendor approval, COA (Certificate of Analysis) review |
| **Process Control** | CCP monitoring, validated processes |
| **Packaging & Labelling** | FSSAI compliance, batch coding, MRP, FSSAI logo |
| **Warehousing** | FIFO (First-In-First-Out), FEFO (First-Expiry-First-Out) |
| **Complaints & Recall** | Mock recall every 6 months, traceability within 4 hours |
| **Documentation** | Batch records, log sheets, deviation reports |

### KTU Formula / Standard Sheet — GMP

| Parameter | Standard / Requirement |
|-----------|------------------------|
| Cold storage temperature | **0 °C to 4 °C** for fresh produce; **−18 °C** for frozen foods |
| Hot holding temperature | **≥ 63 °C** |
| 4-Hour Rule (Danger Zone) | Food held between **5 °C and 63 °C** for max **4 hours** |
| Water activity (a_w) for microbial stability | **< 0.60** for dried foods |
| pH for acid foods | **< 4.6** (botulinum-safe zone) |

---

## 2.4 HACCP — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### The Seven Principles of HACCP (Codex Alimentarius)

1. **Conduct a Hazard Analysis** — Identify biological, chemical, and physical hazards
2. **Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs)** — Use the CCP Decision Tree
3. **Establish Critical Limits** — Maximum/minimum values for control (e.g., 72 °C for 15 sec pasteurization)
4. **Establish Monitoring Procedures** — Who, what, when, how
5. **Establish Corrective Actions** — What to do when critical limits are exceeded
6. **Establish Verification Procedures** — Audits, calibration, microbiological testing
7. **Establish Documentation & Record-Keeping** — Complete traceability records

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:**
> **CCP (Critical Control Point):** A step at which control can be applied and is essential to prevent or eliminate a food safety hazard, or to reduce it to an acceptable level.
> **CL (Critical Limit):** A criterion that separates acceptability from unacceptability.
> **CLV (Critical Limit Value):** The actual numeric value of the CL.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — HACCP Decision Tree

The CCP Decision Tree (Codex) is used at each step identified with a significant hazard:

| Question | If YES → | If NO → |
|----------|----------|---------|
| Q1: Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard? | Go to Q2 | Modify step, process, or product → not a CCP |
| Q2: Does this step eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level? | **CCP** | Go to Q3 |
| Q3: Could contamination with the hazard occur in excess of acceptable levels, or could it increase to unacceptable levels? | Go to Q4 | **Not a CCP** |
| Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level? | **Not a CCP** | **CCP** |

### Heat Pasteurization Equation (Critical Limit Example)

$$P = T \times t$$

where:
- $P$ = pasteurization effect
- $T$ = temperature in °C (critical limit typically **72 °C** for milk)
- $t$ = residence time in seconds (**15 seconds**)

### D-value and Z-value (Microbial Inactivation Kinetics)

$$D_T = \text{time at temperature } T \text{ to achieve 90\% reduction}$$

$$\log\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{t}{D_T}$$

$$Z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log(D_{T_1}) - \log(D_{T_2})}$$

where:
- $N_0$ = initial microbial count (CFU/mL)
- $N$ = final microbial count
- $t$ = exposure time (minutes)
- $D_T$ = decimal reduction time at temperature $T$
- $Z$ = temperature change required for a 10-fold change in D-value (**typical Z = 10 °C** for mesophiles)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| System | Industry Application |
|--------|----------------------|
| GAP | Mango export from India to EU — failing EUREPGAP = rejection at port |
| GHP | Dairy plant — preventing Listeria cross-contamination |
| GMP | Pharmaceutical-grade food supplements — FSSAI Schedule T compliance |
| HACCP | Mandatory for seafood export to EU under **EU Regulation 853/2004**; compulsory under **FSSAI 2011 regulations** for all meat, dairy, and fish units |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — GAP vs GHP vs GMP vs HACCP

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** A 14-mark question often asks for a **comparative analysis** of these systems. Memorize this matrix thoroughly.

| Attribute | GAP | GHP | GMP | HACCP |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|-------|
| **Scope** | Farm / pre-harvest | Hygiene at all stages | Processing / manufacturing | Hazard-specific prevention |
| **Focus** | Inputs & field practices | Sanitation & cleanliness | Process & quality control | Risk-based CCPs |
| **Approach** | Preventive | Preventive | Procedural / regulatory | Scientific & systematic |
| **Mandatory?** | Voluntary (often buyer-driven) | Often mandatory | Mandatory under FSSAI / Codex | Mandatory under FSSAI 2011 for high-risk foods |
| **Records** | Field logs, spray records | Cleaning logs, training records | Batch records, deviation logs | HACCP plan, monitoring records |
| **Failure Mode** | Pesticide residue, microbial load on produce | Cross-contamination, foreign bodies | Process drift, label errors | CCP breach, unmitigated hazard |
| **Authority** | FAO, GLOBALG.A.P. | Codex, FSSAI | WHO, USFDA, FSSAI Schedule 4 | Codex Alimentarius, FSSAI |
| **Hierarchy Position** | Base | Foundation | Mid-level | Top |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pasteurization Time Calculation Using D-Value

A dairy plant pasteurizes milk at **72 °C**. The $D_{72}$ value for *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* (reference pathogen) is **0.6 seconds**. Find the time required for a **6-log reduction** (i.e., from $10^6$ to $10^0$ CFU/mL).

### Step 1 — Identify the Formula

The microbial inactivation follows first-order kinetics:

$$\log\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{t}{D_T}$$

### Step 2 — Identify Given Values

$$N_0 = 10^6 \text{ CFU/mL}, \quad N = 10^0 \text{ CFU/mL}, \quad D_T = 0.6 \text{ s at } 72\,°\text{C}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the Log Reduction

$$\log\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \log\left(\frac{10^6}{10^0}\right) = \log(10^6) = 6$$

This is a **6D reduction** (often referred to as "commercial sterility" or "pasteurization lethality").

### Step 4 — Solve for Time t

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= D_T \times \log\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) \\
t &= 0.6 \times 6 \\
t &= 3.6 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Safety Factor

In practice, regulators require a **safety margin**. The FSSAI standard for milk pasteurization is **72 °C for 15 seconds** (HTST — High Temperature Short Time), which provides approximately a **25D safety factor** over the calculated 3.6 seconds — ensuring destruction of even heat-resistant *Coxiella burnetii*.

### Step 6 — Interpretation

The 15-second critical limit at 72 °C in milk processing is a **CCP critical limit**, validated by the FSSAI and Codex Code of Hygienic Practice for Milk.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: CCP Identification Using the Decision Tree

**Scenario:** A fruit juice manufacturer identifies *Salmonella* as a hazard in apple juice. The processing steps are: **Washing → Sorting → Pulping → Pasteurization (72 °C / 15 s) → Cooling → Packaging**.

### Step 1 — Hazard Analysis

*Salmonella* is a **biological hazard**. It is reduced by **pasteurization** (heat step), but contamination could occur during **cooling** if pipes leak or from **packaging line** if hands are contaminated.

### Step 2 — Apply the CCP Decision Tree at the Pasteurization Step

- **Q1:** Do preventive control measures exist? **YES** (heat treatment).
- **Q2:** Does this step eliminate/reduce the hazard to acceptable level? **YES** (pasteurization kills Salmonella).
- ⇒ **Pasteurization is a CCP.**

### Step 3 — Apply the Decision Tree at the Cooling Step

- **Q1:** Do preventive control measures exist? **YES** (closed-system cooling).
- **Q2:** Does cooling eliminate the hazard? **NO** — cooling only lowers temperature.
- **Q3:** Could contamination occur in excess? **YES** (if condenser water leaks).
- **Q4:** Will a subsequent step eliminate the hazard? **NO** (packaging is not a kill step).
- ⇒ **Cooling is also a CCP** (recontamination control).

### Step 4 — Apply the Decision Tree at the Pulping Step

- **Q1:** Do preventive control measures exist? **YES** (washing).
- **Q2:** Does pulping eliminate the hazard? **NO**.
- **Q3:** Could contamination increase? **YES** (pulp surface ideal for growth).
- **Q4:** Will a subsequent step eliminate the hazard? **YES** (pasteurization follows).
- ⇒ **Pulping is NOT a CCP** (controlled by GMP/GHP).

### Step 5 — Establish Critical Limits

| CCP | Critical Limit | Monitoring | Corrective Action |
|-----|----------------|------------|-------------------|
| Pasteurization | **72 °C ≥ 15 s** | Continuous temperature recorder + timer | Divert to rework tank if T < 70 °C |
| Cooling | **Product T ≤ 5 °C within 2 h** | Probe thermometer every 30 min | Hold batch, test for Salmonella |

### Step 6 — Verification

- **Microbiological testing** of finished product every batch (Salmonella absence in 25 g)
- **Calibration** of pasteurizer temperature probe monthly
- **Internal audit** quarterly

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Hazard Severity × Probability Matrix

HACCP uses a **risk matrix** to rank hazards:

$$\text{Risk Score (R)} = \text{Severity (S)} \times \text{Probability (P)}$$

where S and P are each scored on a scale of **1 to 5**.

| Hazard | Severity (S) | Probability (P) | R | Classification |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|---|----------------|
| Salmonella in milk | 5 | 4 | **20** | Critical — must have CCP |
| Pesticide residue | 4 | 2 | **8** | Significant — control via GAP |
| Metal fragment | 5 | 1 | **5** | Moderate — control via metal detector |
| Glass piece | 4 | 1 | **4** | Moderate — control via GMP |
| Dust particle | 1 | 5 | **5** | Moderate — control via GHP |

**Interpretation:** Any hazard with $R \geq 15$ must be addressed via a **CCP**. Lower risks can be controlled through prerequisite programmes (GAP/GHP/GMP).

---

## 3.5 Quantitative Validation — F Value (Thermal Process Equivalent)

For canned foods, the sterilization value is calculated as:

$$F_0 = \Delta t \cdot \sum 10^{\frac{T_i - T_{\text{ref}}}{Z}}$$

where:
- $F_0$ = equivalent time in minutes at reference temperature (typically **121.1 °C**)
- $\Delta t$ = time interval between temperature measurements (minutes)
- $T_i$ = product temperature at time $i$ (°C)
- $T_{\text{ref}}$ = reference temperature (121.1 °C)
- $Z$ = temperature change for 10-fold change in D-value (**typical = 10 °C** for *Clostridium botulinum*)

A minimum **$F_0 = 3$ minutes** gives a **12-log reduction of C. botulinum spores** — the "botulinum cook".

$$\begin{aligned}
F_0 &= 3 \text{ minutes means} \\
\log\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) &= \frac{F_0}{D_{121.1}} = \frac{3}{0.25} = 12 \text{-log reduction}
\end{aligned}$$

This calculation forms the **critical limit** in the retort CCP for canned food HACCP plans.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Food Safety Pyramid (Prerequisite Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    farm["GAP - Good Agricultural Practices"]
    hyg["GHP - Good Hygienic Practices"]
    mfg["GMP - Good Manufacturing Practices"]
    hccp["HACCP - Hazard Analysis Critical Control Points"]

    farm --> hyg
    hyg --> mfg
    mfg --> hccp

    farmNote["Scope: Farm and Pre-harvest Stage"]
    hygNote["Scope: Hygiene at Every Stage"]
    mfgNote["Scope: Processing and Manufacturing"]
    hccpNote["Scope: Hazard-Specific Risk Control"]

    farm -.- farmNote
    hyg -.- hygNote
    mfg -.- mfgNote
    hccp -.- hccpNote

    prp["PREREQUISITE PROGRAMMES - Foundation Layer"]
    farm ~~~ prp
    hyg ~~~ prp
    mfg ~~~ prp
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — HACCP Flow (Seven Principles in Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["Principle 1: Hazard Analysis"]
    p2["Principle 2: Determine CCPs"]
    p3["Principle 3: Establish Critical Limits"]
    p4["Principle 4: Monitoring Procedures"]
    p5["Principle 5: Corrective Actions"]
    p6["Principle 6: Verification"]
    p7["Principle 7: Documentation and Records"]

    p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4 --> p5 --> p6 --> p7

    step1["Identify Biological Chemical Physical Hazards"]
    step2["Apply CCP Decision Tree"]
    step3["Set Numerical Limits like 72 C and 15 s"]
    step4["Who What When How"]
    step5["What to do on Deviation"]
    step6["Audit Calibration Testing"]
    step7["Batch Records Logs and Plans"]

    p1 -.- step1
    p2 -.- step2
    p3 -.- step3
    p4 -.- step4
    p5 -.- step5
    p6 -.- step6
    p7 -.- step7
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture of a HACCP Plan for Milk Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inp["Raw Milk Reception"]
    test["Lab Testing - Antibiotics and MBRT"]
    clar["Clarification and Separation"]
    stand["Standardization"]
    homo["Homogenization"]
    past["PASTEURIZATION 72 C for 15 s - CCP1"]
    cool["COOLING to 4 C - CCP2"]
    pack["Packaging in Sterile Pouches"]
    stor["Cold Storage at 4 C"]
    dist["Distribution in Refrigerated Van"]

    inp --> test --> clar --> stand --> homo --> past --> cool --> pack --> stor --> dist

    mon1["Continuous Temperature Recorder"]
    mon2["Probe Thermometer every 30 min"]
    mon3["Cleaning Validation"]

    past -.- mon1
    cool -.- mon2
    past -.- mon3
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — CCP Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    q1["Q1: Do preventive control measures exist for the hazard?"]
    q2["Q2: Does this step eliminate or reduce the hazard to acceptable level?"]
    q3["Q3: Could contamination occur in excess or increase to unacceptable level?"]
    q4["Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the hazard?"]

    ccp["CCP - Critical Control Point"]
    notccp["Not a CCP - Control via PRP"]

    q1 -- YES --> q2
    q1 -- NO --> modify["Modify step, process or product"] --> notccp

    q2 -- YES --> ccp
    q2 -- NO --> q3

    q3 -- YES --> q4
    q3 -- NO --> notccp

    q4 -- YES --> notccp
    q4 -- NO --> ccp
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Risk Matrix (Severity × Probability)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sev["Severity Score 1 to 5"]
    prob["Probability Score 1 to 5"]
    risk["Risk R equals S times P"]

    sev --> risk
    prob --> risk

    low["R less than 8 - Control via GHP or GMP"]
    med["R between 8 and 14 - Significant Control via SOP"]
    high["R greater than or equal 15 - Mandatory CCP"]

    risk --> low
    risk --> med
    risk --> high
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide for KTU:** In the food safety pyramid, every higher system **depends** on the lower system. A HACCP plan cannot succeed without functioning GAP, GHP, and GMP. In exam answers, always present them as a **layered system**, not parallel systems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define GAP. List any four core principles of Good Agricultural Practices.**

**Model Answer:**
GAP (Good Agricultural Practices) are defined by FAO as a set of principles, regulations, and technical recommendations applied to on-farm production and post-production processes, ensuring food safety, quality, environmental sustainability, and worker welfare.

**Four core principles:**

1. **Soil and water management** — avoiding heavy metal buildup and microbial contamination in irrigation water
2. **Integrated Pest Management (IPM)** — minimizing pesticide residues while controlling pests
3. **Pre-harvest and harvest hygiene** — using clean containers and trained labour
4. **Traceability and record-keeping** — batch-wise field logs and spray records

*(Total: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for four points = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between GHP and GMP in food processing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | GHP (Good Hygienic Practices) | GMP (Good Manufacturing Practices) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Focus | Personal and environmental hygiene | Process and quality control |
| Scope | All stages of food chain | Manufacturing and processing |
| Examples | Hand washing, sanitation, pest control | Premises design, equipment, batch records |
| Authority | Codex Code of Hygienic Practice | WHO GMP, FSSAI Schedule 4 |
| Nature | Foundational / sanitation-focused | Procedural / regulatory |

*(Total: 3 marks for the comparison table)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the **seven principles of HACCP** as laid down by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission**. Briefly describe the role of prerequisite programmes (PRPs). **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A dairy plant pasteurizes milk. Identify the **CCP** for *Salmonella* control. Establish the **critical limits**, **monitoring procedures**, and **corrective actions**. Calculate the time required for a **6-log reduction** if $D_{72} = 0.6$ s for *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Part A(a)

**Prerequisite Programmes (PRPs):**
These are the foundational practices (GAP, GHP, GMP) that must be in place before HACCP can be implemented effectively. They cover premises design, sanitation, personnel hygiene, raw material control, training, and traceability. Without PRPs, the HACCP system will be **structurally weak**.

**Seven Principles of HACCP:**

1. **Principle 1 — Hazard Analysis:** Identify biological (e.g., *Salmonella*, *E. coli*), chemical (pesticides, antibiotics), and physical (metal, glass) hazards. Estimate severity and probability.

2. **Principle 2 — Determine CCPs:** Use the **CCP Decision Tree** at each step of the process flow diagram. A CCP is a step where control is essential to prevent, eliminate, or reduce a hazard.

3. **Principle 3 — Establish Critical Limits:** Specify numerical values that separate safe from unsafe (e.g., **72 °C for 15 s** for milk pasteurization).

4. **Principle 4 — Establish Monitoring Procedures:** Define **who monitors, what is monitored, when, and how**. E.g., continuous chart recorder on the pasteurizer.

5. **Principle 5 — Establish Corrective Actions:** Define actions when critical limits are exceeded (e.g., divert milk to rework tank, hold and retest).

6. **Principle 6 — Establish Verification Procedures:** Conduct microbiological testing, calibrate instruments, perform internal audits.

7. **Principle 7 — Documentation and Record-Keeping:** Maintain HACCP plan, monitoring logs, deviation reports, and corrective action records.

*(7 marks breakdown: 1 mark for PRPs + 6 × 1 mark = 7 marks for the seven principles)*

#### Model Solution to Part A(b)

**CCP Identification for Milk Pasteurization:**
The **pasteurization step** at **72 °C / 15 s** is the **CCP** for *Salmonella* (and other vegetative pathogens). It is the only step that kills the pathogen.

**Critical Limit:**
$$T = 72\,°\text{C}, \quad t = 15 \text{ seconds (HTST pasteurization)}$$

**Monitoring Procedures:**
- **Who:** Plant operator on duty
- **What:** Outlet temperature of HTST pasteurizer
- **When:** Continuous (chart recorder) + manual check every 15 minutes
- **How:** Calibrated resistance temperature detector (RTD) probe + flow diversion device

**Corrective Actions:**
If temperature falls below 72 °C, the **flow diversion valve (FDV)** automatically redirects milk back to the raw milk silo. The batch is held, retested, and re-pasteurized. All events are logged.

**Quantitative Calculation:**

$$\log\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = 6, \quad D_{72} = 0.6 \text{ s}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= D_T \times \log\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) \\
t &= 0.6 \times 6 \\
t &= 3.6 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A 3.6-second hold at 72 °C gives a **6-log reduction** of *M. tuberculosis*. The FSSAI-mandated **15 seconds** provides a **25× safety margin**, ensuring destruction of more heat-resistant *Coxiella burnetii* — this is the engineering rationale behind the 15-second CCP.

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks CCP + critical limit, 2 marks monitoring & corrective action, 2 marks calculation, 1 mark interpretation)*

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Compare **GAP, GHP, GMP, and HACCP** in terms of scope, focus, mandatory status, and authority. Why is HACCP considered a **preventive** system rather than a **detective** one? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A fruit juice plant identifies the following hazards: **Salmonella** in raw apples, **pesticide residue** exceeding MRL, and **glass fragments** from bottle breakage. Classify each using a **severity × probability** matrix (score 1–5 each) and state whether each requires a **CCP** or can be controlled via **PRP**. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Part B(a)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Attribute | GAP | GHP | GMP | HACCP |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|-------|
| Scope | Farm / pre-harvest | Hygiene across chain | Processing unit | Hazard-specific |
| Focus | Field inputs | Sanitation | Process & quality | Risk-based CCPs |
| Mandatory | Voluntary | Often mandatory | Mandatory | Mandatory for high-risk (FSSAI 2011) |
| Authority | FAO, GLOBALG.A.P. | Codex | WHO, USFDA, FSSAI | Codex, FSSAI |

**Why HACCP is Preventive, Not Detective (3 marks):**

1. HACCP focuses on **preventing hazards before they occur**, rather than detecting defective products after manufacture. Traditional **Quality Control (QC)** inspects finished products — reactive. HACCP **monitors critical limits in real-time**, taking immediate corrective action when limits are breached.

2. HACCP is **science-based and proactive**: it uses the CCP Decision Tree to identify hazards at every process step and **engineers them out** of the process.

3. HACCP relies on **continuous monitoring** (e.g., temperature loggers, metal detectors) rather than **end-product testing alone**, making it a preventive system.

#### Model Solution to Part B(b)

**Severity × Probability Matrix:**

| Hazard | S | P | R = S × P | Classification | Control Mechanism |
|--------|---|---|-----------|----------------|-------------------|
| Salmonella in raw apples | 5 | 4 | **20** | Critical | **CCP** — pasteurization (72 °C / 15 s) |
| Pesticide residue > MRL | 4 | 2 | **8** | Moderate-Significant | **PRP (GAP)** — supplier approval, MRL testing |
| Glass fragments | 5 | 1 | **5** | Moderate | **PRP (GMP/GHP)** — bottle inspection + metal detector on line |

**Rule Applied:** Hazards with $R \geq 15$ require a **CCP**; hazards with $R < 15$ are controlled via prerequisite programmes.

**Engineering Outcome:**

- **CCP installed:** Pasteurizer with continuous temperature recorder and flow diversion valve.
- **PRPs strengthened:** Field-to-factory vendor audit (for MRL), line-speed reduction + visual inspection station (for glass).

*(7 marks breakdown: 3 marks for table + classification rule, 2 marks for identifying CCP, 2 marks for PRP controls)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing GHP and GMP** — GHP is hygiene (people, sanitation); GMP is process control (premises, equipment, records). Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for blurring these.
> 2. **Stating "7 principles of HACCP" without listing them** — merely naming the seven is worth **1 mark**; brief description of each principle earns the rest.
> 3. **Skipping the calculation in quantitative questions** — A 7-mark sub-part with a numerical problem typically carries **2–3 marks** for the calculation. Skipping it loses heavy marks.
> 4. **Forgetting PRPs (Prerequisite Programmes)** — Always mention PRPs before discussing HACCP. Examiners expect HACCP to be presented as **built on top of GAP/GHP/GMP**.
> 5. **Not stating the critical limits numerically** — "Monitor temperature" earns 1 mark; "72 °C for 15 seconds" earns full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **GAP** → Farm and pre-harvest level; FAO principles; E. coli in irrigation water < **1000 CFU/100 mL**
- **GHP** → Hygiene foundation; 8 Codex pillars; SSOP equivalence
- **GMP** → Processing-level rules; WHO GMP; premises, equipment, documentation
- **HACCP** → **7 Codex principles**; **4 prerequisites** (PRPs); CCP Decision Tree
- **HACCP origin** → **Pillsbury + NASA + US Army Natick**, 1960s
- **CCP definition** → Step essential to prevent/eliminate/reduce a hazard
- **Critical limit** → Numerical threshold (e.g., **72 °C / 15 s** for milk)
- **D-value** → Time for 90% microbial reduction
- **Z-value** → Temperature change for 10-fold change in D-value (typically **10 °C**)
- **F₀ value** → Equivalent sterilization time at 121.1 °C; minimum **3 minutes** for botulism safety
- **4-Hour Rule** → Food held between **5 °C and 63 °C** for max **4 hours**
- **Hazard types** → Biological, Chemical, Physical
- **Risk Score** → $R = S \times P$; **R ≥ 15 → CCP**, R < 15 → PRP
- **Mandatory under FSSAI 2011** for meat, dairy, seafood, packaged drinking water
- **Hierarchical structure** → **GAP → GHP → GMP → HACCP**
- **Verification methods** → Microbial testing, calibration, internal audits
- **Documentation** → HACCP plan, monitoring logs, deviation reports, corrective actions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Memory Hook — "FG³H":**
> **F**oundation = GAP → **G**HP → **G**MP → **H**ACCP on top.
> Remember this order — it appears in nearly every KTU question paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Product Quality Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT614 - POST HARVEST HANDLING OF FOOD MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Measurement of Product Quality Methods # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Product Quality Measurement** in post-harvest handling refers to the systematic application of **objective (instrumental)**, **subjective (sensory)**, and **chemical** analytical techniques to quantify the physical, chemical, microbiological, and organoleptic attributes of harvested food materials at various stages between harvest and consumption. In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, quality measurement is classified into **three intersecting domains**: 1. **Physical Quality** — texture, color, size, shape, density, moisture. 2. **Chemical Quality** — proximate composition, vitamins, sugars, acids, pesticide residues. 3. **Sensory / Organoleptic Quality** — appearance, flavor, aroma, mouthfeel as perceived by trained panelists or electronic analogues. > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU Terminology:** *Destructive vs. Non-destructive testing* — Destructive methods (e.g., titration, penetrometer) alter the sample, while non-destructive methods (NIR spectroscopy, machine vision, acoustic firmness) preserve the sample for downstream use. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are a doctor diagnosing a patient. You use: - **A stethoscope** (instrumental, objective) → analogous to a **colorimeter** measuring apple skin color. - **A thermometer** → analogous to a **thermocouple** measuring cold-storage pulp temperature. - **The patient's verbal feedback** ("I feel pain") → analogous to a **sensory panelist's** response to bitterness in olive oil. - **Blood test report** → analogous to a **HPLC chromatogram** quantifying sugar content in mango pulp. The "doctor" in post-harvest technology is the **Quality Analyst**, and the "patient" is the food commodity. Just as a single symptom never tells the whole story, a single measurement is insufficient — multiple parameters must be combined to issue a **quality passport** for the produce. ## 1.3 The Four Pillars of Quality Measurement | Pillar | What it Measures | Example Tool | Destructive? | |---|---|---|---| | **Physical** | Texture, geometry, color | Texture Analyzer, Colorimeter | Sometimes | | **Chemical** | Composition, contaminants | HPLC, Titration kits | Usually | | **Sensory** | Human perception | Trained panel, e-nose | No (consumes sample) | | **Microbiological** | Spoilage, pathogens | Plate count, PCR | Destructive | > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 KTU module explicitly emphasizes **instrumental texture analysis (TPA)**, **tristimulus colorimetry (L\*a\*b\*)**, and **objective moisture measurement** (oven vs. rapid NIR). ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Units (KTU Board-Expected) - **Color:** CIE **L\*a\*b\*** units (dimensionless), **Hue angle** (°), **Chroma** (C\*). - **Texture:** Force in **Newton (N)**, Hardness in **N**, Firmness in **N mm⁻¹**. - **Moisture:** **% wet basis (w.b.)** or **% dry basis (d.b.)**. - **Total Soluble Solids (TSS):** **°Brix** (≈ % sucrose w/w). - **Acidity:** **% citric acid equivalent** or **g/100 mL**. - **pH:** **Dimensionless**, range 0–14. - **Sensory scores:** **9-point hedonic scale** (1 = dislike extremely, 9 = like extremely). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Measurement Methods (Structured Logic) ### 2.1.1 Instrumental / Objective Methods - **Spectroscopic:** UV-Vis, NIR, FTIR, Raman — based on light-matter interaction. - **Chromatographic:** HPLC, GC — separate and quantify chemical species. - **Mechanical:** Texturometer, Penetrometer, Puncture test — quantify force-deformation. - **Optical:** Colorimeter, Machine Vision, Digital Imaging. - **Thermal:** DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry), TGA. - **Acoustic / Vibration:** Acoustic firmness sensor, Ultrasonic. ### 2.1.2 Chemical / Wet Methods - **Titrimetric:** Acidity, vitamin C, salt, chloride. - **Gravimetric:** Moisture (oven), ash, crude fiber. - **Colorimetric:** Phenols (Folin-Ciocalteu), antioxidant (DPPH). - **Refractometric:** TSS / °Brix via refractometer. ### 2.1.3 Sensory / Subjective Methods - **Discriminative:** Triangle test, Duo-Trio, Paired comparison. - **Descriptive:** Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA™), Flavor Profile. - **Affective:** Hedonic (9- or 7-point) tests with consumers. - **Electronic analogues:** e-nose (MOS sensors), e-tongue (potentiometric sensors). ## 2.2 Why and How — The Operational Logic > **Why measure texture?** Consumers reject mealy apples, rubbery bread, soft tomatoes. Texture correlates with **cell wall integrity**, **turgor pressure**, and **starch-pectin balance** during ripening. > **How does a texture analyzer work?** A probe descends at constant speed, the **load cell** records force (N) vs. **distance (mm)** → software computes parameters from the **force-time/deformation curve**. ### 2.2.1 Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) — the Industry Standard The two-bite TPA mimics the **first and second chewing strokes** of the human jaw. From one curve we extract: - **Hardness (N):** Peak force of first compression. - **Cohesiveness (A₂/A₁):** Ratio of work of second compression to first. - **Springiness (mm):** Distance recovered between bites. - **Gumminess (N):** Hardness × Cohesiveness. - **Chewiness (N·mm):** Gumminess × Springiness. - **Resilience:** Ratio of upstroke work to downstroke work (first bite). ### 2.2.2 Color Measurement — CIE L\*a\*b\* System Three coordinates describe any perceivable color: - **L\***: Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white). - **a\***: Greenness (−) to redness (+). - **b\***: Blueness (−) to yellowness (+). Derived metrics: - **Chroma** (saturation): C* = √(a*² + b*²) - **Hue angle** (true color): h° = arctan(b* / a*) (in degrees, 0–360) - **Total color difference:** ΔE\* = √[(ΔL*)² + (Δa*)² + (Δb*)²] ### 2.2.3 Moisture Content — Wet vs. Dry Basis $$M_{wb} = \frac{m_{water}}{m_{total}} \times 100 \quad (\% \text{ wet basis})$$ $$M_{db} = \frac{m_{water}}{m_{dry\,matter}} \times 100 \quad (\% \text{ dry basis})$$ $$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$$ ### 2.2.4 Total Soluble Solids (TSS) Measured by **refractometer** based on refractive index (RI). For sucrose solutions, **°Brix ≈ % sucrose w/w**. ### 2.2.5 Titratable Acidity (TA) $$TA\ (\%) = \frac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times Eq.\,wt.\,of\,dominant\,acid \times 100}{V_{sample} \times 1000}$$ For citrus: dominant acid = **citric acid**, equivalent weight = **64.04 g/eq**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table for ESE — every Part-B question on Module 4 draws from these equations.** | # | Quality Parameter | Formula | Units | Instrument / Standard | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Moisture (w.b.) | $M_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Hot-air oven, 105 °C, AOAC | | 2 | Moisture (d.b.) | $M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$ | % | Same as above | | 3 | TSS | $°Brix \approx \%\ \text{sucrose}$ | °Brix | Refractometer (0–32%) | | 4 | Titratable Acidity | $TA = \frac{V \cdot N \cdot E \cdot 100}{V_s \cdot 1000}$ | % | Burette, pH meter | | 5 | CIE Chroma | $C^* = \sqrt{a^{*2} + b^{*2}}$ | dimensionless | Colorimeter | | 6 | Hue angle | $h° = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{b^*}{a^*}\right)$ | degrees | Colorimeter | | 7 | Total color diff. | $\Delta E^* = \sqrt{\Delta L^{*2} + \Delta a^{*2} + \Delta b^{*2}}$ | dimensionless | Colorimeter | | 8 | Whiteness Index | $WI = 100 - \sqrt{(100-L^*)^2 + a^{*2} + b^{*2}}$ | dimensionless | Colorimeter | | 9 | Browning Index | $BI = \frac{[100(x-0.31)]}{0.17}$ where $x=\frac{a^*+1.75 L^*}{5.645 L^*+ a^* - 3.012 b^*}$ | dimensionless | Colorimeter | | 10 | Hardness (TPA) | Peak force of 1st bite | N | Texture Analyzer | | 11 | Cohesiveness | $A_2 / A_1$ | dimensionless | Texture Analyzer | | 12 | Chewiness | Hardness × Cohesiveness × Springiness | N·mm | Texture Analyzer | | 13 | Firmness (Magness-Taylor) | Peak puncture force | N | Penetrometer (8 mm plunger) | | 14 | Firmness (Acoustic) | $f^2 \cdot m$ (where f = resonant frequency) | dimensionless | Acoustic sensor | | 15 | Respiration rate | $R = \frac{(CO_2)_{out} - (CO_2)_{in}}{\Delta t \cdot m}$ | mL kg⁻¹ h⁻¹ | Gas analyzer | | 16 | Surface area (spherical) | $A = \pi d^2$ | mm² | Caliper | | 17 | Sphericity | $\phi = \frac{d_{eq}}{(L\cdot W\cdot T)^{1/3}}$ | dimensionless | Caliper | | 18 | Bulk density | $\rho_b = m / V_{bulk}$ | kg m⁻³ | Graduated cylinder | | 19 | True density | $\rho_t = m / V_{true}$ (pycnometer) | kg m⁻³ | Pycnometer | | 20 | Porosity | $\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$ | dimensionless | Calculated | > **Engineering Utility:** These parameters feed directly into **HACCP decision trees**, **shelf-life prediction (Arrhenius model)**, **quality-based grading** (Agmark, Codex), and **automated sorting** (computer vision in pack-houses). ## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Application - **Apple pack-house:** Magness-Taylor penetrometer (8 mm tip, 8 mm penetration) sorts fruit into Extra Class / Class I / Class II. - **Tomato paste industry:** °Brix × 1000 = Bostwick consistency index for ketchup grading. - **Cold-chain QC:** NIR handheld gun (e.g., Felix F-750) measures dry matter, °Brix, acidity non-destructively in mango. - **Beverage industry:** Refractometer + pH meter + titrator — the **"QC triad"** for fruit juice export. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion of Moisture Content (Wet Basis ↔ Dry Basis) This is the most frequently tested numerical in KTU Module 4. **Given:** A sample of milled rice has moisture content **M_wb = 13 % (wet basis)**. **Required:** Express in dry basis. **Step 1 — Write definition of wet basis:** $$M_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_{total}} \times 100 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{m_w}{m_{total}} = \frac{M_{wb}}{100}$$ **Step 2 — Express dry mass:** The dry matter (DM) mass is the total mass minus water mass: $$m_{dry} = m_{total} - m_w = m_{total}\left(1 - \frac{M_{wb}}{100}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Apply definition of dry basis:** $$M_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_{dry}} \times 100$$ **Step 4 — Substitute m_w and m_dry:** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{db} &= \frac{m_{total} \cdot \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100}}{m_{total}\left(1 - \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100}\right)} \times 100 \\[6pt] M_{db} &= \frac{\dfrac{M_{wb}}{100}}{1 - \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100}} \times 100 \\[6pt] M_{db} &= \frac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Numerical substitution (M_wb = 13 %):** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{db} &= \frac{13}{100 - 13} \times 100 \\[4pt] M_{db} &= \frac{13}{87} \times 100 \\[4pt] M_{db} &= 0.14943 \times 100 \\[4pt] M_{db} &= 14.94 \,\% \quad (\text{dry basis}) \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic check:** Dry-basis value is **always greater** than wet-basis value (because the denominator is smaller). This is a useful self-validation step KTU examiners love to see. ## 3.2 Derivation: Chroma (C\*) and Hue Angle (h°) from CIE L\*a\*b\* **Given:** Apple skin measured at L\* = 45.2, a\* = +28.6, b\* = +19.4. **Required:** Compute Chroma and Hue angle. **Step 1 — Chroma (saturation magnitude in a\*-b\* plane):** $$ \begin{aligned} C^* &= \sqrt{a^{*2} + b^{*2}} \\[4pt] C^* &= \sqrt{(28.6)^2 + (19.4)^2} \\[4pt] C^* &= \sqrt{817.96 + 376.36} \\[4pt] C^* &= \sqrt{1194.32} \\[4pt] C^* &= 34.56 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Hue angle (angular position, quadrant-aware):** Since a\* > 0 and b\* > 0, the color lies in the **first quadrant (red-yellow region)** — appropriate for ripe apple. $$ \begin{aligned} h° &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{b^*}{a^*}\right) \\[4pt] h° &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{19.4}{28.6}\right) \\[4pt] h° &= \tan^{-1}(0.6783) \\[4pt] h° &= 34.13° \quad (\text{red-orange hue}) \end{aligned} $$ > **Engineering interpretation:** A ripe red apple typically has h° in the range **30°–45°**. As senescence progresses, h° drops toward 0° (browning). This single parameter tracks ripening in real-time on the sorting line. ## 3.3 Derivation: Total Color Difference (ΔE\*) **Scenario:** A control sample (fresh apple) and a 30-day cold-stored sample are compared. | Sample | L\* | a\* | b\* | |---|---|---|---| | Fresh (control) | 47.0 | +30.0 | +18.0 | | Stored (30 d) | 38.0 | +18.0 | +12.0 | **Step 1 — Compute deltas:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta L^* &= 38.0 - 47.0 = -9.0 \\ \Delta a^* &= 18.0 - 30.0 = -12.0 \\ \Delta b^* &= 12.0 - 18.0 = -6.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply ΔE\* formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta E^* &= \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2} \\[4pt] \Delta E^* &= \sqrt{(-9.0)^2 + (-12.0)^2 + (-6.0)^2} \\[4pt] \Delta E^* &= \sqrt{81 + 144 + 36} \\[4pt] \Delta E^* &= \sqrt{261} \\[4pt] \Delta E^* &= 16.16 \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision rule (CIELAB 1976):** > - **ΔE\* < 1** → not perceptible to the human eye. > - **1 ≤ ΔE\* < 3** → perceptible only on close inspection. > - **3 ≤ ΔE\* < 6** → clearly perceptible difference. > - **ΔE\* ≥ 6** → strong color difference (likely reject from premium grade). > > The 30-day stored apple (**ΔE\* = 16.16**) would be **rejected** from "Extra Class" grade. ## 3.4 Derivation: Titratable Acidity — Worked Numerical **Given:** 10 mL of orange juice titrated with **0.1 N NaOH** until pH 8.2 endpoint. Volume of NaOH used = **8.5 mL**. Dominant acid = citric acid (Eq. wt. = 64.04 g/eq). **Step 1 — Apply TA formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} TA\ (\%) &= \frac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times E \times 100}{V_{sample} \times 1000} \\[6pt] TA\ (\%) &= \frac{8.5 \times 0.1 \times 64.04 \times 100}{10 \times 1000} \\[6pt] TA\ (\%) &= \frac{5443.4}{10000} \\[4pt] TA\ (\%) &= 0.544\ \% \quad \text{(as citric acid)} \end{aligned} $$ > **Acceptable range for fresh orange juice:** **0.6 – 1.2 %** — this juice (0.54 %) is slightly under-acid, indicating over-ripe fruit or dilution. ## 3.5 Derivation: Bulk Density, True Density & Porosity **Given:** Lentil seeds, mass = 100 g. - Bulk volume (graduated cylinder): **128 mL**. - True volume (pycnometer, toluene displacement): **82 mL**. **Step 1 — Bulk density:** $$\rho_b = \frac{m}{V_{bulk}} = \frac{100\ \text{g}}{128\ \text{mL}} = 0.781\ \text{g mL}^{-1} = 781\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$$ **Step 2 — True density:** $$\rho_t = \frac{m}{V_{true}} = \frac{100\ \text{g}}{82\ \text{mL}} = 1.220\ \text{g mL}^{-1} = 1220\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$$ **Step 3 — Porosity:** $$ \begin{aligned} \varepsilon &= 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t} \\[4pt] \varepsilon &= 1 - \frac{0.781}{1.220} \\[4pt] \varepsilon &= 1 - 0.640 \\[4pt] \varepsilon &= 0.360 \quad \text{or}\ 36.0\ \% \end{aligned} $$ > **Engineering interpretation:** Porosity > 35 % indicates **high inter-granular air space** — useful for **aerated drying** and **fluidized bed handling** without excessive compaction damage. ## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix: Method Selection | Quality Attribute | Best Instrumental Method | Sensory Correlation | Speed | Cost | Non-Destructive? | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Color** | Colorimeter / Spectrophotometer | Visual appearance | Fast | Low | ✅ | | **Firmness** | Magness-Taylor / TPA | Touch, bite | Fast | Low | ❌ (destructive) | | **TSS / Sweetness** | Digital refractometer | Taste | < 1 min | Low | ✅ | | **Acidity** | pH meter + titration | Sourness | 5 min | Low | ❌ | | **Aroma volatiles** | GC-MS, e-nose | Smell | 30 min | High | ✅ | | **Moisture** | Oven (105 °C) / NIR | Texture feel | 24 h / 5 s | Low / Med | ❌ / ✅ | | **Pesticide residue** | HPLC, GC-ECD | None | Hours | High | ❌ | | **Microbial load** | Plate count, PCR | None | 24–48 h | Med | ❌ | | **Starch content** | Iodine staining + colorimeter | Taste | 10 min | Low | ✅ | | **Vitamin C** | Titration (DCPIP) / HPLC | Sourness | 15 min / 30 min | Low / High | ❌ | > **KTU Examiner's Note:** When a Part-B question asks "Discuss suitable methods to evaluate quality of mango during storage," structure the answer by **attribute (color, texture, TSS, acidity, aroma)** — then pair instrumental + sensory for each. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Product Quality Measurement System This diagram maps the entire quality measurement workflow used in a modern food pack-house, from sample intake to final decision. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[SAMPLE INTAKE
Lot Identification] --> B[PREPARATION UNIT
Washing Peeling Slicing] B --> C{Measurement Type} C -->|Physical| D1[PHYSICAL LAB
Texture Color Geometry Density] C -->|Chemical| D2[CHEMICAL LAB
TSS Acidity Moisture Ash] C -->|Sensory| D3[SENSORY BOOTH
ISO 8589 Controlled] C -->|Microbiological| D4[MLR LAB
Plate Count PCR] D1 --> E1[Data Logger] D2 --> E2[Data Logger] D3 --> E3[Panelist Score Sheet] D4 --> E4[Incubator Reader] E1 --> F[CENTRAL DATABASE
LIMS Software] E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F F --> G{Decision Engine} G -->|Pass| H[GRADE A EXPORT] G -->|Re-QC| I[HOLD LOT] G -->|Fail| J[REJECT / DOWNGRADE] F --> K[TRACEABILITY
QR Code Cloud] ``` ## 4.2 Sensory Evaluation Workflow (Discriminative + Descriptive + Affective) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Panelist Recruitment
ISO 3972] --> P2[Screening Tests
ISO 5496] P2 --> P3[Training Phase
12 to 24 sessions] P3 --> P4{QDA Type} P4 -->|Discriminative| S1[Triangle Test] S1 --> S2[Duo-Trio] S2 --> S3[Paired Comparison] P4 -->|Descriptive| S4[Quantitative Descriptive Analysis] S4 --> S5[Flavor Profile Method] P4 -->|Affective| S6[9-Point Hedonic Scale] S6 --> S7[Just-About-Right Scale] S3 --> OUT[QC Decision] S5 --> OUT S7 --> OUT OUT --> SPC[SPC Chart + Shelf-life Predictor] ``` ## 4.3 Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) Force-Time Curve Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB Start([Probe contacts sample]) --> FB[First Bite Downstroke
Peak Force = Hardness] FB --> UP1[Upstroke to Original Position] UP1 --> Hold[Hold for 5 s] Hold --> SB[Second Bite Downstroke
Area A2] SB --> UP2[Upstroke to Original Position] UP2 --> End([Software computes parameters]) Hardness -.->|Peak of FB| H1[H = max F in N] A1 -.->|Area under FB| C1[Cohesiveness = A2 slash A1] A2 -.->|Area under SB| C1 S -.->|Recovery distance| S1[Springiness mm] H1 --> GUM[Gumminess = H x Cohesiveness] GUM --> CHEW[Chewiness = Gumminess x Springiness] ``` ## 4.4 CIE L\*a\*b\* Color Space Block Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TB LightSource[Standard Illuminant D65] --> Sample[Food Sample Reflectance] Sample --> Obs[Standard Observer 10 degrees] Obs --> XYZ[Tristimulus Values XYZ] XYZ --> Lab[CIE L a b Coordinates] Lab -->|Compute| Cstar[Chroma C star] Lab -->|Compute| Hue[Hue Angle h degrees] Lab -->|Compute| dE[Delta E Total Color Difference] Lab -->|Compute| WI[Whiteness Index] Lab -->|Compute| BI[Browning Index] Cstar --> QC[QC Decision] Hue --> QC dE --> QC WI --> QC BI --> QC ``` ## 4.5 Instrumental vs Sensory Decision Tree (Classroom Exam Favorite) ```mermaid flowchart TB Q[Quality Question] --> Cost{Cost Constraint} Cost -->|Low| Instr[Choose Instrumental Method] Cost -->|High| Both[Both Instrumental and Sensory] Instr --> Avail{Equipment Available} Avail -->|Yes| A1[Proceed with Calibrated Instrument] Avail -->|No| A2[Use Rapid Test Kit] Both --> Correlate[Establish Instrument-Sensory Correlation] Correlate --> PredModel[Develop PLS or ANN Prediction Model] A1 --> Report[QC Report with COA] A2 --> Report PredModel --> Report ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** > - Part A: 3 questions × 3 marks = 9 marks (Module 4 may contribute 1–2). > - Part B: Module choice — answer **one** of two 14-mark questions (typically 7 + 7 sub-parts). --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **Define Texture Profile Analysis (TPA). List any four parameters obtained from a TPA curve.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** TPA is a double-compression test (1 mark) performed on a Texture Analyzer that mimics the two-bite chewing action (½ mark) of the human mouth. Four parameters are: 1. **Hardness** (N) — peak force of first compression (½ mark). 2. **Cohesiveness** — ratio of work of second bite to first bite (½ mark). 3. **Springiness** (mm) — height recovered between the two bites (½ mark). 4. **Chewiness** (N·mm) — Hardness × Cohesiveness × Springiness (½ mark). *(Alternative acceptable 4th: Gumminess, Resilience, Adhesiveness.)* --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` **What is meant by CIE L\*a\*b\* color system? Briefly explain the meaning of L\*, a\*, and b\*.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The CIE L\*a\*b\* system (1 mark) is a uniform, device-independent three-dimensional color space (½ mark) recommended by the Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage. - **L\*** = Lightness axis, ranging from **0 (black)** to **100 (white)** (1 mark). - **a\*** = Red–green axis, with **+a\*** indicating redness and **−a\*** indicating greenness (½ mark). - **b\*** = Yellow–blue axis, with **+b\*** indicating yellowness and **−b\*** indicating blueness (½ mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **Instructions (as per KTU 2024):** *Answer one of the following. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.* --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]` **(a)** Explain the **principle, procedure, and significance** of **refractometric measurement of Total Soluble Solids (TSS)** in fruit juices. How is °Brix related to refractive index? (7 marks) **(b)** A sample of freshly harvested mango pulp has **moisture content of 81 % (w.b.)**. The pulp is concentrated by evaporation to a final moisture of **28 % (w.b.)**. (i) Express both moistures in **dry basis**. (ii) Calculate the **mass of water removed per 100 kg of fresh pulp**. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Refractometric TSS (7 marks)** - **Principle (2 marks):** A refractometer measures the **refractive index (RI)** of a liquid, which depends on the **concentration of dissolved solids** (mainly sugars) and temperature. As sugar concentration increases, RI increases linearly over the range 0–32 °Brix. - **Procedure (2 marks):** Place 2–3 drops of filtered, degassed juice on the prism (½ mark), close the cover (½ mark), and read through the eyepiece against daylight or D-line sodium lamp (½ mark). For digital refractometers, the value is auto-compensated to **20 °C** (½ mark). - **Significance (2 marks):** TSS indicates **maturity** (mango, grape, apple) and **sweetness** (½ mark). In industry, °Brix is used to compute the **Brix-Acid Ratio (BAR)**, a key quality passport (½ mark), and to determine **harvest date** and **price in contract farming** (½ mark). In concentrates, °Brix governs **microbial stability** (water activity linkage) (½ mark). - **Relation to RI:** For pure sucrose, **°Brix = (RI − 1.3330) × 10000** approximately (½ mark). Modern instruments use a polynomial calibration table per ICUMSA. **(b) Numerical (7 marks)** **(i) Conversion to dry basis (3 marks):** For fresh pulp (M_wb = 81 %): $$ M_{db} = \frac{81}{100 - 81} \times 100 = \frac{81}{19} \times 100 = 426.32\ \% $$ For concentrated pulp (M_wb = 28 %): $$ M_{db} = \frac{28}{100 - 28} \times 100 = \frac{28}{72} \times 100 = 38.89\ \% $$ **(ii) Water removed per 100 kg of fresh pulp (4 marks):** Let the dry mass in 100 kg fresh pulp be: $$m_{dry} = 100 \times \frac{(100 - 81)}{100} = 19\ \text{kg}$$ Let the final mass of concentrated pulp be $m_f$. Since dry mass is conserved: $$m_f = \frac{m_{dry}}{(100 - M_{wb}^{final})/100} = \frac{19}{0.72} = 26.39\ \text{kg}$$ Water removed = Initial mass − Final mass = **100 − 26.39 = 73.61 kg** of water per 100 kg fresh pulp. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **(a)** With a **neat labeled diagram**, describe the working of a **Magness-Taylor pressure tester** for measuring fruit firmness. State its limitations. (7 marks) **(b)** Discuss the **principles, types, and applications** of **sensory evaluation methods** used in post-harvest quality control of foods. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Magness-Taylor Tester (7 marks)** - **Construction (2 marks):** It consists of a **cylindrical body** (½ mark) housing a **spring-loaded plunger** (½ mark) with a **standard 8 mm diameter tip** (½ mark) and a **circular gauge** (½ mark) calibrated in **lb-force** (or N). Optional: bench-mounted version for laboratory use. - **Working (3 marks):** The plunger is pressed into the peeled surface of the fruit (½ mark) at a steady, perpendicular rate (½ mark) until it penetrates **8 mm** (½ mark). The maximum force registered on the gauge is the **firmness value** in **lb (or N)** (½ mark). For apples, recommended values: > 18 lb (very firm), 13–18 lb (firm), 10–13 lb (soft), < 10 lb (over-ripe) (½ mark). - **Significance (1 mark):** The reading correlates with **cell wall strength**, **turgor**, and **storage potential**; it is the **international standard** for apple/pear grading. - **Limitations (1 mark):** 1. **Destructive** — fruit cannot be sold after testing (½ mark). 2. Operator-dependent penetration depth and angle (½ mark). *(Add 1 more if needed: not suitable for soft berries.)* **(b) Sensory Evaluation (7 marks)** | Type | Purpose | Test Example | Data Type | Marks | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Discriminative** | Detects *if* a difference exists | Triangle, Duo-Trio, Paired | Right/wrong, forced choice | 2 | | **Descriptive** | Quantifies *what* and *how much* a difference is | QDA, Flavor Profile | Intensity on unstructured line scale | 3 | | **Affective** | Measures *liking* by consumers | 9-point Hedonic, JAR | Ordinal / interval liking score | 2 | **Principles (2 marks in narrative):** - Tests must be conducted under **controlled conditions** (ISO 8589 booths: temperature 21 ± 1 °C, red light for color masking, no noise/odour) (½ mark). - **Panelist selection**: discriminative tests need only 24–30 screened panelists; descriptive tests require **10–12 highly trained** panelists over 12–24 sessions (½ mark). - **Sample presentation**: random order, 3-digit codes, palate cleansing (water + unsalted cracker) between samples (½ mark). - **Statistical analysis**: ANOVA + Tukey HSD for descriptive; chi-square for discriminative (½ mark). **Applications (in post-harvest):** - Shelf-life studies, package-material evaluation, cultivar comparison, new-product development, and **sensory-instrumental correlation modelling** (PLS regression). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 (per KTU valuation key):** > 1. **Confusing °Brix with Brix-Acid Ratio (BAR).** °Brix is *only* sugar/soluble solids, not "sweetness." Sweetness = function(BAR). > 2. **Writing ΔE\* without the correct sign convention for ΔL\*, Δa\*, Δb\*** — examiners deduct ½ mark if you forget the difference (L_sample − L_reference). > 3. **Failing to specify wet vs. dry basis** when reporting moisture — this single omission costs 1 full mark. > 4. **Mixing up the equivalent weight** of dominant acids: citric 64.04, malic 67.05, lactic 90.08, tartaric 75.04, acetic 60.05. > 5. **Calling the texture analyzer "destructive" without clarifying** that the *sample* is destroyed, not the *equipment*. > 6. **Sensory answers that forget the booth specifications** (ISO 8589) — examiners expect temperature, light colour, and palate-cleanser mention. > 7. **Skipping the formula while solving a numerical** — always show the equation *before* substituting numbers. > 8. **Forgetting the unit at the end of the answer** — every quantitative answer must end with a unit (N, °Brix, %, etc.). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Module 4 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this on the last page of your answer book):** - ✅ **Quality = Multi-attribute** — never answer with a single parameter; always pair **instrumental + sensory**. - ✅ **TPA parameters (7):** Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, Chewiness, Resilience, Adhesiveness. - ✅ **CIE L\*a\*b\*** — L\* = lightness (0–100); a\* = red(+)/green(−); b\* = yellow(+)/blue(−). - ✅ **ΔE\* thresholds** — 1, 3, 6 are perceptually meaningful cut-offs. - ✅ **Moisture conversion:** $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$ (db value **always** > wb value). - ✅ **Refractometer** = TSS (°Brix) = % sucrose w/w at 20 °C. - ✅ **Titratable acidity** — report as % dominant acid (citric in citrus/mango, malic in apple, tartaric in grape). - ✅ **Magness-Taylor** — 8 mm tip, 8 mm penetration, destructive. - ✅ **Non-destructive instruments** — NIR, machine vision, acoustic firmness, e-nose, e-tongue. - ✅ **Porosity** $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b / \rho_t$ — controls aeration during drying/fluidization. - ✅ **Browning Index (BI)** — derived from L\*a\*b\*; tracks enzymatic browning in cut apple, potato, banana. - ✅ **Whiteness Index (WI)** — critical for flour, rice, sugar, milk powder. - ✅ **Sensory booth** — ISO 8589: 21 ± 1 °C, positive pressure, red light for colour masking. - ✅ **9-point hedonic** = affective; **Triangle test** = discriminative; **QDA** = descriptive. - ✅ **NIR wavelength range:** 780–2500 nm; **Mid-IR (FTIR):** 2500–25000 nm. - ✅ **HPLC** = high-performance liquid chromatography; separates non-volatile organics (sugars, acids, vitamins, pigments). - ✅ **GC** = gas chromatography; separates volatiles (aroma compounds). - ✅ **Texture–Sensory correlation** is a **favourite 7-mark question** — always end with a correlation statement. - ✅ **Always** state the **standard / method reference** (AOAC, ISO, IS, FSSAI, Codex).

topics.title: Destructive and Non-Destructive Tests
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Destructive and Non-Destructive Tests in Food Materials

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> **Destructive Testing (DT):** Analytical methods applied to food materials that permanently alter, consume, or destroy the physical, chemical, or biological integrity of the sample to obtain qualitative or quantitative information about its properties. The sample cannot be reused or resold after testing.

> **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):** A category of analytical and inspection techniques used in post-harvest handling that evaluate the internal and/or external quality attributes of food materials without altering their final usability, commercial value, or edibility. The sample remains intact and marketable after analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (Module 4):** Food quality assurance has shifted from purely chemical wet-lab methods toward hybrid sensor-based systems. NDT methods are now graded with high marks because they support **rapid, online, in-line** quality control in food processing industries.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a fruit market and want to know if a watermelon inside is **ripe, sweet, and red**.

* **Destructive Way:** You cut the watermelon open → taste it → measure Brix with a refractometer → throw away the damaged portion. **You have lost a sellable product.**
* **Non-Destructive Way:** You *tap* the watermelon and listen to the sound (acoustic resonance), *scan* it with a near-infrared beam, or *image* it with X-rays. You get a fairly accurate prediction of sugar content and ripeness without opening it. **The fruit is still intact for sale.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** Destructive tests are **highly accurate** but produce **waste**; Non-Destructive tests are **slightly less precise** but are **rapid, repeatable, and zero-waste** — making them indispensable for modern food industries.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardness | $F_{max}$ | **N (Newton)** | Texture Profile Analysis |
| Force | $F$ | **N** | Penetration / Compression |
| Stress | $\sigma$ | **Pa (N/m²)** | Mechanical testing |
| Strain | $\varepsilon$ | **dimensionless** | Mechanical testing |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | **nm** | NIR / Vis spectroscopy |
| Absorbance | $A$ | **AU** | Spectrophotometry |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **MPa** | Texture / Acoustic |
| Acoustic Velocity | $v$ | **m/s** | Ultrasonic NDT |
| Dielectric Constant | $\varepsilon'$ | **dimensionless** | RF/Microwave sensing |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Tests

### A. Destructive Tests (DT)

1. **Mechanical / Textural Tests**
   * Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)
   * Puncture / Penetration Test
   * Shear Test (Warner-Bratzler)
   * Tensile / Compression Test
   * Kramer Shear Cell Test

2. **Chemical / Compositional Tests**
   * Proximate Analysis (moisture, ash, fat, protein, carbohydrate)
   * Titratable Acidity
   * Total Soluble Solids (TSS / °Brix)
   * pH Measurement
   * Vitamin quantification (HPLC)

3. **Sensory Evaluation**
   * Hedonic scale tests
   * Triangle tests
   * Difference-from-control tests

4. **Microbiological Tests**
   * Total Plate Count (TPC)
   * Yeast and Mold Count

### B. Non-Destructive Tests (NDT)

1. **Optical / Imaging Methods**
   * Computer Vision (RGB imaging)
   * Hyperspectral Imaging (HSI)
   * Multispectral Imaging
   * Fluorescence Imaging

2. **Spectroscopic Methods**
   * Near-Infrared Spectroscopy (NIRS, 700–2500 nm)
   * Mid-Infrared Spectroscopy (MIRS)
   * Visible-NIR (Vis-NIR, 400–1100 nm)
   * Raman Spectroscopy
   * Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR)

3. **Acoustic / Vibration Methods**
   * Ultrasonic Testing (100 kHz – 20 MHz)
   * Acoustic Emission
   * Acoustic Impulse Response

4. **Electrical / Electromagnetic Methods**
   * Electrical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)
   * Dielectric Spectroscopy
   * X-ray Imaging / Computed Tomography (CT)
   * Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)

5. **Bioelectronic Sensors**
   * Electronic Nose (E-nose)
   * Electronic Tongue (E-tongue)

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Principles

### 2.2.1 Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) — Destructive

TPA is a **two-cycle compression test** that simulates the action of the human jaw (mastication). It generates a force–time curve from which multiple textural parameters are derived.

**Operating Principle:**
A cylindrical probe compresses the food sample **twice** (down-stroke and up-stroke). The peak force of the first compression is the hardness; the area ratios give cohesiveness, and the distance recovered gives springiness.

### 2.2.2 Near-Infrared Spectroscopy (NIRS) — Non-Destructive

Based on the **Beer-Lambert Law** of light absorption. Molecular vibrations (C-H, O-H, N-H bonds) absorb specific NIR wavelengths. By correlating absorbance patterns with chemical composition via **chemometrics (PCA, PLSR)**, we predict moisture, sugar, fat, and protein content.

### 2.2.3 Ultrasonic Testing — Non-Destructive

Ultrasonic waves propagate through food with velocity $v$ given by:

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$$

where $E$ is the **Young's modulus** and $\rho$ is the **density** of the medium. Time-of-flight (TOF) and attenuation reveal firmness, ripeness, and internal defects (hollow heart in potatoes, bruises in apples).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Engineering stress | **Pa** |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Engineering strain | **dimensionless** |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Young's Modulus | **Pa** |
| 4 | $A_{2} \div A_{1}$ | Cohesiveness (TPA) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $T_{2} \div T_{1}$ | Springiness (TPA) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $A = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon c l$ | Beer-Lambert Law | **AU** |
| 7 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ | Acoustic wave speed | **m/s** |
| 8 | $L^{*} = 116\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_n}\right)^{1/3} - 16$ | CIELAB Lightness | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta E^{*} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2} + (\Delta a^{*})^{2} + (\Delta b^{*})^{2}}$ | Total color difference | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}$ | Dielectric loss factor | dimensionless |
| 11 | $F_{max} = k \cdot d^{n}$ | Force-deformation (firmness) | **N** |
| 12 | $RMSE = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Model accuracy (NDT) | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule:** In the formulas above, all vertical bars and divisions are written with `\div` or with proper fraction layout, NEVER with the symbol `|` inside table cells, to prevent Markdown table-breaking errors.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

* **Destructive Tests:** Regulatory compliance, label verification, nutritional labeling (FSSAI / Codex), shelf-life studies, final product certification.
* **Non-Destructive Tests:** Online sorting (e.g., optical sorters removing defective almonds), grading lines in pack-houses, ripeness prediction in cold storage, internal defect detection in potato chips and apples.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hardness and Cohesiveness from TPA Curve

The TPA test produces a typical **two-bite force–time curve**. Let:

* $F_1$ = Peak force of **first compression** (Hardness, in N)
* $F_2$ = Peak force of **second compression** (Hardness after first bite)
* $A_1$ = Area under the **first compression curve** (positive work of first bite)
* $A_2$ = Area under the **second compression curve** (positive work of second bite)
* $A_3$ = Negative area between cycles (adhesive work)
* $T_1$ = Time at start of first compression
* $T_2$ = Time at start of second compression

### Step 1 — Define Hardness

$$\text{Hardness} = F_{1}$$

This is simply the maximum force recorded during the first compression stroke. It is the **first peak** of the force–time curve.

### Step 2 — Define Cohesiveness

Cohesiveness is the ratio of the **work of the second compression** to that of the **first compression**. It describes how well the sample holds together under repeated stress.

$$\text{Cohesiveness} = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$$

* Value = 1 → perfectly cohesive (gel-like)
* Value → 0 → completely brittle / crumbly

### Step 3 — Define Springiness

Springiness is the ratio of the **time interval** between the start of the second compression and the start of the first compression, divided by the time of the first compression.

$$\text{Springiness} = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{end} - T_{1}}$$

A perfectly elastic sample recovers fully → Springiness → 1.0.

### Step 4 — Define Chewiness

Chewiness is the energy required to masticate a solid food until it is ready for swallowing.

$$\text{Chewiness} = \text{Hardness} \times \text{Cohesiveness} \times \text{Springiness}$$

with units in **N**.

### Step 5 — Define Gumminess

For semi-solids (no springiness):

$$\text{Gumminess} = \text{Hardness} \times \text{Cohesiveness}$$

with units in **N**.

### Step 6 — Derived Validation

For a perfectly elastic solid, $\text{Springiness} \to 1$, $\text{Cohesiveness} \to 1$, $\text{Chewiness} = \text{Hardness}$. This is consistent with physical intuition: a hard, fully elastic solid would require sustained chewing effort equal to its breaking force.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Beer-Lambert Law for NIR Spectroscopy

When a beam of monochromatic radiation of intensity $I_0$ passes through a food sample of thickness $l$ (cm) and concentration $c$ (mol/L) of an absorbing species, the transmitted intensity $I$ is given by:

### Step 1 — Differential Equation of Absorption

Consider an infinitesimally thin slice of thickness $dx$. The fraction of light absorbed is proportional to the intensity, the concentration, and the thickness:

$$dI = -\alpha \cdot c \cdot I \cdot dx$$

where $\alpha$ is the **molar absorptivity** (L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹).

### Step 2 — Separation of Variables

$$\dfrac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \cdot c \cdot dx$$

### Step 3 — Integration Across the Path

Integrating from $I_0$ at $x = 0$ to $I$ at $x = l$:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \dfrac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \cdot c \cdot \int_{0}^{l} dx$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\alpha \cdot c \cdot l$$

### Step 4 — Convert Logarithm Base to Base 10

Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \cdot \log_{10}(x)$:

$$\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot c \cdot l}{2.303}$$

### Step 5 — Define Absorbance

$$A = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

where $\varepsilon = \alpha / 2.303$ is the **molar extinction coefficient**.

> This is the **Beer-Lambert Law**. It is the foundational equation for all spectroscopic non-destructive methods (NIR, MIR, UV-Vis).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Acoustic Wave Velocity in Foods

Acoustic methods rely on the relationship between wave speed and material stiffness.

### Step 1 — One-Dimensional Wave Equation

For a longitudinal wave traveling through a homogeneous food medium:

$$\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = v^2 \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$

where $u$ is the particle displacement and $v$ is the longitudinal wave speed.

### Step 2 — Solution Form

Assuming a harmonic wave $u(x,t) = U_0 e^{i(kx - \omega t)}$ and substituting back:

$$-\omega^2 U_0 = -v^2 k^2 U_0$$

$$v = \dfrac{\omega}{k} = f \cdot \lambda$$

### Step 3 — Link to Material Properties

For a thin rod (Poisson ratio effect ignored):

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$$

where $E$ is the Young's modulus (Pa) and $\rho$ is the density (kg/m³).

### Step 4 — Engineering Application

The measured time-of-flight (TOF) of an ultrasonic pulse across a known thickness $d$ gives:

$$v = \dfrac{d}{TOF}$$

From $v$ and the known density $\rho$, we determine the apparent stiffness of the fruit/vegetable, which correlates with firmness and ripeness.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DT vs. NDT

| Attribute | Destructive Test (DT) | Non-Destructive Test (NDT) |
|---|---|---|
| Sample integrity | **Destroyed** | **Preserved** |
| Throughput | Low (5–30 samples/hour) | High (1000s of samples/hour) |
| Accuracy | Very high (gold standard) | Moderate–High (R² = 0.85–0.98) |
| Cost per test | Low–Medium | High initial, low marginal |
| Skill required | Skilled lab technician | Trained operator + software |
| Real-time capability | No | **Yes (online grading)** |
| Regulatory acceptance | Universally accepted | Increasingly accepted |
| Data type | Scalar / discrete | Multivariate spectra / images |
| Waste generation | Yes | No |
| Use in industry | R&D, QC lab | Sorting line, R&D, QC lab |
