# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between destructive and non-destructive testing methods used in post-harvest quality evaluation of food materials.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Destructive Test | Non-Destructive Test |
|---|---|---|
| Sample Status | Sample is destroyed / consumed | Sample remains intact |
| Speed | Slow, lab-based | Fast, often online |
| Examples | TPA, Proximate analysis, pH | NIR, Computer Vision, Ultrasonic |
| Accuracy | Very high (gold standard) | High (model-dependent) |
| Use | R&D, regulatory compliance | Online grading, sorting |

Destructive methods (e.g., Texture Profile Analysis, proximate analysis, HPLC) alter the food permanently and provide highly accurate reference data. Non-destructive methods (e.g., NIR spectroscopy, computer vision, ultrasonic) preserve the sample and enable high-throughput online grading. Both are complementary: NDT models are **calibrated** against DT reference values.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the Beer-Lambert Law and explain its relevance in NIR spectroscopy of foods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Beer-Lambert Law states:

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

where $A$ is absorbance (AU), $\varepsilon$ is the molar extinction coefficient (L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹), $c$ is the concentration (mol/L) and $l$ is the optical path length (cm).

**Relevance (2 marks):** In NIR spectroscopy of foods, this law is the **physical foundation** that allows prediction of chemical composition (moisture, sugar, fat, protein). Specific molecular bonds (O-H, C-H, N-H) absorb characteristic NIR wavelengths, producing a **spectral fingerprint**. By correlating absorbance at multiple wavelengths with reference values (from destructive chemical analysis) using chemometric models (PLSR), we build a **non-destructive prediction model** for rapid online quality control.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Choice Format

---

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) — Destructive Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** *(Choice A)*
*(a)* Explain in detail the principle, instrumentation, and procedure of **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)**. Derive the formulas for **Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, and Chewiness**. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* Discuss **proximate analysis** of food materials with its significance in post-harvest quality assurance. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution (a) — TPA (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):**
TPA is a double-compression test that imitates the action of human mastication. A cylindrical probe descends at constant speed to compress the food sample twice, generating a force-time curve. From this curve, multiple textural parameters are mathematically extracted.

**Instrumentation (2 marks):**
1. **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** or **Texture Analyzer** (e.g., TA-XT2, Instron).
2. **Load Cell** for force measurement (range 0–500 N typical).
3. **Stepper Motor Drive** for controlled probe movement.
4. **Probe:** cylindrical, typically 50 mm diameter for fruits.
5. **Data Acquisition System** + Software (e.g., Texture Expert, Exponent).

**Procedure (1 mark):**
* Calibrate load cell with standard weights.
* Place sample centrally on the platform.
* Compress to 30 % (or 50 %) strain at 1 mm/s.
* Return probe to original position, wait 5 s.
* Repeat second compression cycle.

**Derivations (2 marks):**
$$\text{Hardness} = F_1$$
$$\text{Cohesiveness} = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$$
$$\text{Springiness} = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{end} - T_1}$$
$$\text{Chewiness} = \text{Hardness} \times \text{Cohesiveness} \times \text{Springiness}$$

**Engineering Utility (Bonus):** TPA is the **gold standard** for shelf-life studies, ripening studies, and reformulation of processed foods.

---

#### Solution (b) — Proximate Analysis (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Proximate analysis is a systematic chemical procedure to determine the major constituents of a food — moisture, ash, crude protein, crude fat (ether extract), and carbohydrate (by difference) — as defined by the **AOAC International** methods.

**Components & Methods (4 marks):**

| Component | Standard Method | Principle |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture | Hot air oven, 105 °C, 5 h | Gravimetric water loss |
| Ash | Muffle furnace, 550 °C, 6 h | Gravimetric residue after combustion |
| Crude Protein | Kjeldahl / Dumas | N × 6.25 conversion |
| Crude Fat | Soxhlet extraction | Ether-soluble extract |
| Carbohydrate | By difference | 100 − (Moisture + Ash + Protein + Fat) |

**Post-Harvest Significance (2 marks):**
* Determines **nutritional labeling** compliance (FSSAI, Codex).
* Tracks **moisture loss** during storage (a key quality decay indicator).
* Detects **adulteration** (e.g., dilution with starch).
* Supports **shelf-life prediction** via sorption isotherms.

---

### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) — Non-Destructive Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** *(Choice B)*
*(a)* Describe the **principle, instrumentation, and applications of Near-Infrared (NIR) spectroscopy** in non-destructive quality evaluation of food materials. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* Explain **computer vision systems** for non-destructive sorting and grading of fruits and vegetables. Include a block diagram. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution (a) — NIR Spectroscopy (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):**
NIR spectroscopy measures the absorption of electromagnetic radiation in the **700–2500 nm** range. When NIR light interacts with food molecules, overtone and combination vibrations of **O-H, C-H, and N-H** bonds absorb specific wavelengths. By analyzing these absorption patterns, the chemical composition (moisture, protein, fat, sugar) can be predicted.

**Beer-Lambert Law:**
$$A = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

**Instrumentation (2 marks):**
1. **Light Source:** Tungsten-Halogen lamp (covers full NIR range).
2. **Monochromator:** Diffraction grating or FT-NIR (Michelson interferometer).
3. **Sample Presentation:** Integrating sphere, fiber optic probe, or transmission cell.
4. **Detector:** InGaAs or PbS photodetector.
5. **Chemometric Software:** PCA + PLSR for calibration.

**Procedure (1 mark):**
* Acquire absorbance spectrum of sample (A vs. λ).
* Preprocess spectra (SNV, MSC, 2nd derivative).
* Apply pre-built PLSR calibration model.
* Predict attribute values in real time.

**Applications (2 marks):**
* **Moisture** measurement in grains, oilseeds, spices.
* **Brix / TSS** in fruits (apple, mango).
* **Fat** content in milk, cheese, meat.
* **Adulteration** detection in honey and milk powder.

---

#### Solution (b) — Computer Vision (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):**
A camera captures an image of the food sample under controlled illumination. Image processing algorithms extract **size, shape, color, and surface defects**. The features are fed into a classifier (e.g., SVM, ANN) that sorts the sample into quality grades — all without physical contact.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
[Sample] → [Conveyor] → [Lighting Chamber] → [CCD/CMOS Camera] → [Frame Grabber] → [Image Processing (Edge Detection, Color Space L*a*b*)] → [Feature Extraction] → [Classifier] → [Grade Bin / Reject Bin]

**Components (1 mark):**
* **Illumination:** Diffuse LED ring or dome (eliminates shadows).
* **Camera:** Industrial RGB camera (3-CCD preferred).
* **Processing Unit:** Industrial PC with OpenCV / MATLAB.
* **Actuator:** Pneumatic ejector or robotic arm for sorting.

**Applications (1 mark):**
* Apple grading by size, color, blush, defects.
* Tomato sorting by redness uniformity.
* Cashew kernel color sorting.
* Almond shell-piece separation.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Common Mistake Penalty Zone:**
> 1. **Confusing NDT with "less accurate" methods** — NDT can match DT accuracy (R² > 0.95) when properly calibrated. Marks are deducted if students write that NDT is "less scientific." Always emphasize calibration against DT reference values.
> 2. **Forgetting to state units** in TPA derivations — Hardness must be in **N**, Cohesiveness is **dimensionless**. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 3. **Writing Beer-Lambert as $A = \varepsilon c l$ without defining all symbols** — at least $\varepsilon$, $c$, $l$ must be defined; missing one = –1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing "Computer Vision" with "Machine Learning"** — CV is the imaging pipeline; ML is the classifier. KTU expects both to be mentioned separately.
> 5. **Listing tests without classifying them as DT or NDT** — categorization carries marks in Part B.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DT** = destroys sample; **NDT** = preserves sample. The 2024 scheme emphasizes NDT for **online grading**.
- **TPA** derives **Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Chewiness, Gumminess** from a single force-time curve.
- **Beer-Lambert Law** $A = \varepsilon c l$ is the foundation of all absorption spectroscopy (NIR, MIR, UV-Vis).
- **NIR** predicts moisture, sugar, fat, protein via **PLSR chemometric models**.
- **Ultrasonic testing** uses $v = \sqrt{E/\rho}$ to measure firmness non-destructively.
- **Computer Vision** uses RGB cameras + image processing for size, shape, color, and defect sorting.
- **Proximate Analysis** (AOAC) = Moisture + Ash + Protein + Fat + Carbohydrate = 100 %.
- **Hyperspectral Imaging** combines spectroscopy + imaging — gives both chemical and spatial maps.
- **E-nose and E-tongue** mimic human smell/taste using sensor arrays and pattern recognition.
- **X-ray CT** detects internal defects (hollow heart, weevil infestation, pit fragments) without cutting.
- **Dielectric spectroscopy** uses $\tan \delta = \varepsilon''/\varepsilon'$ for moisture and composition sensing.
- Always cite the **unit** and the **standard method** (AOAC, ISO, ASTM) when applicable.
- For any KTU Part B question, **draw a labeled block diagram** — it always earns bonus marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical, Chemical, Biological, Visual Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT614 - POST HARVEST HANDLING OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Physical, Chemical, Biological, Visual Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Quality evaluation of post-harvest food materials is the systematic application of **Physical, Chemical, Biological, and Visual methods** to determine the compositional, structural, microbiological, and aesthetic attributes of agricultural produce. These methods form the backbone of grading, sorting, shelf-life prediction, and compliance testing in food processing industries.

## 1.1 What Are These "Quality Evaluation Methods"?

In the post-harvest handling chain — from the farm gate to the fork — every fruit, vegetable, grain, or processed food item must be checked for **quality, safety, and acceptability**. KTU groups these checks into four complementary domains:

| Domain | Core Question It Answers |
|---|---|
| **Physical Methods** | "What does it feel, weigh, or look like mechanically?" |
| **Chemical Methods** | "What is it made of, and how much of each component is present?" |
| **Biological Methods** | "Is it safe, alive, or how is it changing biologically?" |
| **Visual Methods** | "Does it look acceptable to the human eye or a machine vision system?" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Check-up" Analogy:**
> Imagine a freshly harvested mango arriving at a packing house. The **Physical methods** are like checking its weight, size, and firmness (the "vitals"). The **Chemical methods** are the blood test — measuring sugars (Brix), acidity (pH), and vitamin C. The **Biological methods** are the pathology tests — checking for microbial load and respiration rate. Finally, the **Visual methods** are the dermatologist's eye — assessing color, blemishes, and surface defects. Together they give a complete "health card" of the produce.

## 1.2 Why Are These Methods Critical in Post-Harvest Engineering?

- **Grading & Standardization**: AGMARK, BIS, Codex, and USDA grading all rely on these measurements.
- **Shelf-Life Prediction**: Respiration rate (biological) + moisture (physical) + microbial load (biological) = predicted shelf life.
- **Process Control**: Sorting machines use physical (size, weight) and visual (color, defects) parameters.
- **Consumer Acceptance**: A product with the "right chemistry" but the "wrong color" will be rejected by consumers.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: Food Safety and Standards Authority (FSSAI) mandates microbiological and chemical testing.

## 1.3 Classification Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics & Constants Used in This Module:**
> - **Brix (°Bx)** = % soluble solids (mainly sugars) — measured at **20 °C**.
> - **Water Activity (a_w)** — dimensionless, range **0 to 1**.
> - **Color values**: **L\*** (lightness, 0–100), **a\*** (redness/greenness, ±60), **b\*** (yellowness/blueness, ±60) as per **CIE Lab** system.
> - **Respiration rate** units: **mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹** or **mg CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹**.
> - Standard microbial plate count units: **CFU/g** (Colony Forming Units per gram).

---

### Module 4 Topic Roadmap

The following 4 main categories will be studied in this topic:

1. **Physical Methods** → Dimensional, mechanical, optical, thermal, electrical properties.
2. **Chemical Methods** → Compositional analysis, pH, acidity, sugars, pigments.
3. **Biological Methods** → Microbiological, enzymatic, respiration, sensory.
4. **Visual Methods** → Manual grading, color charts, computer vision, image processing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PHYSICAL METHODS — Detailed Breakdown

Physical methods are **instrument-based** assessments that quantify attributes without (mostly) altering the chemical composition of the food. They range from simple hand-held tools to advanced non-destructive sensors.

### A. Dimensional & Gravimetric Properties

1. **Size & Shape**
   - Measured using **vernier calipers**, **image analysis**, or **laser scanners**.
   - Indices used: **Diameter (mm)**, **Length–Diameter (L/D) ratio**, **Sphericity (φ)**.
   - **Why it matters**: Uniform size enables mechanical sorting, consistent packaging, and predictable heat transfer during processing.

2. **Mass & Density**
   - **True density (ρ_t)** = mass ÷ volume of the solid (excluding pores) — measured using **gas pycnometer**.
   - **Bulk density (ρ_b)** = mass ÷ volume including inter-particle voids.
   - **Apparent density (ρ_a)** = mass ÷ volume including internal pores.

3. **Specific Gravity**
   - Used in **fruit grading** — e.g., tomatoes with SG > 1.0 sink (good quality); lower SG fruits float (overripe or hollow).

### B. Mechanical & Textural Properties

> [!NOTE]
> **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** is the KTU-favorite topic under this section. It is a **two-bite compression test** that mimics chewing.

Key TPA parameters:

- **Hardness** = peak force of first compression (N).
- **Cohesiveness** = ratio of work in second bite ÷ work in first bite (dimensionless).
- **Springiness** = distance recovered after first bite ÷ original deformation.
- **Chewiness** = Hardness × Cohesiveness × Springiness.
- **Adhesiveness** = negative work area of first bite (N·s).
- **Resilience** = work of upstroke ÷ work of downstroke in first bite.

Instruments: **Texture Analyzer (TA-XT2, Stable Micro Systems)**, **Penetrometer (Magness-Taylor for fruits)**, **Firmtech** for firmness.

### C. Optical & Color Properties

- **Hunter Lab system (L, a, b)** and **CIE L\*a\*b\*** are universally used.
- **Colorimeter / Spectrophotometer** measures reflected light.
- **Whiteness Index (WI)** and **Browning Index (BI)** are computed for baked and processed foods.
- **Total Color Difference (ΔE)** quantifies deviation from a reference.

### D. Thermal Properties

| Property | Symbol | Unit | Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific heat | $c_p$ | kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹ | DSC, mixture method |
| Thermal conductivity | $k$ | W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹ | Line-source probe |
| Thermal diffusivity | $\alpha$ | m²·s⁻¹ | Calculated |
| Enthalpy of fusion | $\Delta H_f$ | kJ·kg⁻¹ | DSC |

### E. Electrical & Dielectric Properties

- Used in **dielectric heating** (microwave, RF).
- **Dielectric constant (ε')** and **loss factor (ε'')** depend on moisture and frequency.

### F. Acoustic & Vibration Methods

- Measure **stiffness factor** = $f^2 m^{2/3}$ where $f$ = resonant frequency and $m$ = mass.
- Used for sorting **kiwifruit, mangoes, apples** by firmness without damage.

### G. Non-Destructive Imaging Methods

- **X-ray imaging** → detects internal defects (hollow heart in potato).
- **MRI / NMR** → moisture distribution, oil content.
- **Near-Infrared Spectroscopy (NIR)** → rapid Brix, firmness prediction.
- **Computer Tomography (CT)** → internal structure of fruits and grains.

---

## 2.2 CHEMICAL METHODS — Detailed Breakdown

Chemical methods determine the **composition** of food — what is inside, in what quantity, and in what form.

### A. Moisture Content Determination

1. **Oven Drying Method (AOAC Official)**
   - Sample dried at **105 °C for 3 hours** (or 130 °C for 1 hour for cereals).
   - Most widely used; inexpensive; but **destroys volatile compounds**.
2. **Vacuum Oven Method** → for heat-sensitive foods (sugar-rich, oil-rich).
3. **Infrared Moisture Meter** → rapid, uses IR heating.
4. **Karl Fischer Titration** → for very low moisture (<1%) like spices, dried milk.
5. **Distillation Method (Toluene)** → for fats and oils (where water is hard to evaporate alone).

### B. Ash Content

- **Total ash** = residue after incineration at **550 °C**.
- **Acid-insoluble ash** = ash insoluble in dilute HCl — indicates soil/sand contamination.

### C. pH and Titratable Acidity (TA)

- **pH meter** (potentiometric method) — measures free H⁺ activity.
- **Titratable acidity** = mL of NaOH × N × acid factor × 100 ÷ sample weight.
- **Sugar-Acid Ratio** = TSS (°Bx) ÷ TA (%) — critical maturity index for citrus, grapes, mangoes.

### D. Total Soluble Solids (TSS / °Brix)

- Measured by **hand refractometer** or **digital refractometer**.
- Compensated to 20 °C.
- In fruits, °Brix correlates well with sugar content.

### E. Protein, Fat, Fiber (Proximate Analysis)

| Component | Method | Apparatus |
|---|---|---|
| Protein (N × 6.25) | Kjeldahl / Dumas | Kjeldahl apparatus |
| Fat | Soxhlet extraction | Soxhlet apparatus |
| Crude fiber | Acid-alkali digestion | Fibertec |
| Carbohydrate | By difference | — |

### F. Pigment & Phytochemical Assays

- **Chlorophyll** — measured at **645 nm** and **663 nm** (Arnon's equation).
- **Carotenoids** — measured at **480 nm**.
- **Anthocyanins** — pH-differential method at **520 nm** and **700 nm**.
- **Vitamin C (Ascorbic acid)** — 2,6-Dichlorophenol Indophenol titration.
- **Antioxidant capacity** — DPPH (517 nm), ABTS (734 nm), FRAP.

---

## 2.3 BIOLOGICAL METHODS — Detailed Breakdown

### A. Microbiological Analysis

- **Total Plate Count (TPC)** — Aerobic plate count at **30–37 °C for 48 h**.
- **Yeast & Mold Count** — Acidified PDA at **25 °C for 5 days**.
- **Coliforms** — MPN method or VRBA (Violet Red Bile Agar).
- **Specific pathogens** — E. coli O157:H7, Salmonella, Listeria — using selective media and ELISA/PCR.

### B. Respiration Rate Measurement

Two approaches:
1. **Closed system** — Produce sealed in container; measure O₂ depletion or CO₂ accumulation using **gas chromatograph** or **infrared CO₂ analyzer**.
2. **Flow-through system** — Continuous gas stream through produce; difference between inlet and outlet gives respiration rate.

### C. Ethylene Production Rate

- Measured by **gas chromatography (GC-FID)**.
- Critical for **climacteric fruits** (banana, mango, tomato).

### D. Enzyme Activity Assays

- **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)** — Browning potential.
- **Peroxidase (POD)** — Heat treatment adequacy indicator.
- **Pectic enzymes (PG, PME)** — Texture degradation in fruits.

### E. Shelf-Life Studies

- **Accelerated Shelf-Life Testing (ASLT)** at elevated temperatures.
- **$Q_{10}$** approach — rate acceleration factor for every 10 °C rise.

### F. Sensory Evaluation (Biological / Human Response)

- **Difference tests** — Triangle, duo-trio, paired comparison.
- **Descriptive analysis** — Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA), Flavor Profile.
- **Hedonic tests** — 9-point hedonic scale for consumer acceptance.
- **Panel types**: Trained (8–12 panelists) vs Consumer (≥ 60 panelists).

---

## 2.4 VISUAL METHODS — Detailed Breakdown

### A. Manual Visual Inspection

- Human inspectors grade on **color, size uniformity, blemishes, shape**.
- Subjective, slow, and prone to fatigue — but still widely used in **traditional markets**.

### B. Color Charts and Reference Standards

- **Royal Horticultural Society (RHS) Color Chart** for flowers/fruits.
- **Munsell Color System** — Hue, Value, Chroma.
- **AGMARK grade specifications** (India) — define color bands for each grade.

### C. Computer Vision Systems (CVS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic — Computer Vision for Food Quality**

Pipeline:
**Image Acquisition → Pre-processing → Segmentation → Feature Extraction → Classification → Decision**

- **Cameras**: CCD or CMOS, RGB or multispectral/hyperspectral.
- **Illumination**: Standardized lighting (D65, D50).
- **Features extracted**: Color histograms, texture (GLCM), shape descriptors (area, perimeter, eccentricity).
- **Classifiers**: ANN, SVM, Deep Learning (CNN — ResNet, VGG).

### D. Spectral Imaging

- **Hyperspectral imaging** captures reflectance at hundreds of wavelengths — used to detect **bruising, sugar content, moisture** non-destructively.

### E. Machine Vision Applications

- **Apple sorting** — color + defect detection.
- **Rice grading** — chalkiness, broken percentage.
- **Tomato maturity** — RGB to L\*a\*b\* conversion.

---

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (EXAM CHEAT SHEET)

> [!NOTE]
> **NOTE TO STUDENTS**: The pipe `|` symbol inside table cells is replaced with `\vert` to keep Markdown table syntax intact.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture Content (wet basis) | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_i - W_f}{W_i} \times 100$ | $W_i$ = initial mass, $W_f$ = final mass |
| 2 | Moisture Content (dry basis) | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_i - W_f}{W_f} \times 100$ | Used in drying calculations |
| 3 | Sphericity | $\phi = \dfrac{(L \cdot W \cdot T)^{1/3}}{L}$ | $\phi \le 1$; $L,W,T$ = longest, intermediate, shortest axes |
| 4 | Porosity | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Bulk Density | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_{bulk}}$ | kg·m⁻³ |
| 6 | True Density | $\rho_t = \dfrac{m}{V_{solid}}$ | kg·m⁻³ (gas pycnometer) |
| 7 | Sugar–Acid Ratio | $SAR = \dfrac{^\circ Brix}{TA\,(\%)}$ | Maturity index for fruits |
| 8 | Titratable Acidity | $TA(\%) = \dfrac{V_{NaOH} \times N \times E \times 100}{W_{sample}}$ | $E$ = equivalent weight of dominant acid |
| 9 | Total Color Difference (CIE) | $\Delta E = \sqrt{(L^*_2 - L^*_1)^2 + (a^*_2 - a^*_1)^2 + (b^*_2 - b^*_1)^2}$ | $\Delta E < 1$: not perceptible; $\Delta E > 3$: clearly different |
| 10 | Browning Index | $BI = \dfrac{100 \cdot (x - 0.31)}{0.17}$ where $x = \dfrac{a^* + 1.75 L^*}{5.645 L^* + a^* - 3.012 b^*}$ | Used for baked / fried foods |
| 11 | Chlorophyll a | $Chl_a = 12.7 A_{663} - 2.69 A_{645}$ | mg·L⁻¹ (Arnon equation) |
| 12 | Chlorophyll b | $Chl_b = 22.9 A_{645} - 4.68 A_{663}$ | mg·L⁻¹ |
| 13 | Total Chlorophyll | $Chl_{total} = 20.2 A_{645} + 8.02 A_{663}$ | mg·L⁻¹ |
| 14 | Vitamin C (Ascorbic acid) | $V_C = \dfrac{V_{DCPIP} \times 0.5}{W_{sample}} \times 100$ | mg per 100 g |
| 15 | Specific Heat (Siebel's eq.) | $c_p = 0.033 W + 0.837$ | kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹; $W$ = moisture fraction |
| 16 | Respiration Rate | $R_{CO_2} = \dfrac{(C_{out} - C_{in}) \times Q}{m}$ | mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹; $Q$ = flow rate |
| 17 | $Q_{10}$ Rule | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{10/(T_2 - T_1)}$ | Typically 2–3 for fresh produce |
| 18 | Kjeldahl Protein | $\text{Protein (\%)} = \%N \times F$ | $F$ = 6.25 (general), 5.70 (wheat), 6.38 (milk) |
| 19 | Water Activity | $a_w = \dfrac{P}{P_0} = \dfrac{ERH\,(\%)}{100}$ | $P$ = partial vapor pressure |
| 20 | Stiffness factor (acoustic) | $S = f^2 \cdot m^{2/3}$ | $f$ = resonance frequency (Hz) |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Mandate**: Every algebraic step is fully written out. No abbreviation. The derivations below are common KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Moisture Content (Wet & Dry Basis Interconversion)

**Problem Statement:**
A sample of **fresh mango pulp** weighs **250 g** before drying and **62.5 g** after oven drying at 105 °C for 3 h. Compute (a) Moisture content on wet basis, (b) Moisture content on dry basis, and (c) The mass of water removed per kg of original pulp.

### Given:
- $W_i = 250$ g (initial mass)
- $W_f = 62.5$ g (final mass after drying)

### Step 1 — Mass of Water Removed

$$
m_w = W_i - W_f
$$

$$
m_w = 250 - 62.5 = 187.5 \,\text{g}
$$

> **Interpretation:** 187.5 g of water evaporated during drying. This is the total moisture present in the original pulp.

### Step 2 — Moisture Content on Wet Basis (MC_wb)

$$
MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_i - W_f}{W_i} \times 100
$$

$$
MC_{wb} = \dfrac{250 - 62.5}{250} \times 100
$$

$$
MC_{wb} = \dfrac{187.5}{250} \times 100
$$

$$
MC_{wb} = 0.75 \times 100 = 75 \,\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** Mango pulp is **75 % water on a wet basis** — typical for ripe mango.

### Step 3 — Moisture Content on Dry Basis (MC_db)

$$
MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_i - W_f}{W_f} \times 100
$$

$$
MC_{db} = \dfrac{187.5}{62.5} \times 100
$$

$$
MC_{db} = 3.0 \times 100 = 300 \,\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** For every 1 kg of dry solids, there were 3 kg of water — a value > 100% is normal on a dry basis.

### Step 4 — Mass of Water per kg of Original Pulp

$$
m_w / \text{kg} = 187.5 \,\text{g per 250 g} = 750 \,\text{g/kg of fresh pulp}
$$

### Step 5 — Interconversion Formula Derivation

Starting from $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_i - W_f}{W_i}$ and $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_i - W_f}{W_f}$:

$$
MC_{wb} = \dfrac{MC_{db}}{100 + MC_{db}} \times 100
$$

Substitute $MC_{db} = 300\%$:

$$
MC_{wb} = \dfrac{300}{100 + 300} \times 100 = \dfrac{300}{400} \times 100 = 75 \,\%
$$

> **Verification:** Both methods yield the same answer. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sugar–Acid Ratio (Maturity Index)

**Problem Statement:**
The juice of a **grape** sample has TSS = **18 °Brix** and titratable acidity = **0.9 %** (as tartaric acid). Calculate the Sugar-Acid Ratio. Comment on maturity.

### Step 1 — Direct Substitution

$$
SAR = \dfrac{^\circ Brix}{TA\,(\%)}
$$

$$
SAR = \dfrac{18}{0.9} = 20
$$

### Step 2 — Interpretation

| SAR Range | Quality / Maturity |
|---|---|
| < 15 | Too acidic — immature |
| 15 – 25 | Balanced — table grape maturity |
| 25 – 35 | Sweet — wine grape (late harvest) |
| > 35 | Overripe — fermentation risk |

> **Result:** $SAR = 20$ → grape is at **ideal table-grape maturity**. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Color Difference (ΔE)

**Problem Statement:**
A fried snack before frying has $L^* = 75.2$, $a^* = +3.1$, $b^* = +25.4$. After frying, $L^* = 62.5$, $a^* = +9.8$, $b^* = +30.1$. Compute ΔE and interpret.

### Step 1 — Differences in Each Coordinate

$$
\Delta L^* = L^*_2 - L^*_1 = 62.5 - 75.2 = -12.7
$$

$$
\Delta a^* = a^*_2 - a^*_1 = 9.8 - 3.1 = +6.7
$$

$$
\Delta b^* = b^*_2 - b^*_1 = 30.1 - 25.4 = +4.7
$$

### Step 2 — Square Each Difference

$$
(\Delta L^*)^2 = (-12.7)^2 = 161.29
$$

$$
(\Delta a^*)^2 = (6.7)^2 = 44.89
$$

$$
(\Delta b^*)^2 = (4.7)^2 = 22.09
$$

### Step 3 — Sum

$$
\Sigma = 161.29 + 44.89 + 22.09 = 228.27
$$

### Step 4 — Square Root

$$
\Delta E = \sqrt{228.27}
$$

$$
\Delta E = 15.11
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation Table

| ΔE Value | Human Perception |
|---|---|
| 0 – 1 | Not perceptible |
| 1 – 2 | Perceptible on close inspection |
| 2 – 3 | Perceptible at a glance |
| 3 – 5 | Clearly different |
| > 5 | Strongly different (very obvious) |

> **Result:** $\Delta E = 15.11$ → snack is **strongly darker and more reddish** after frying, indicating severe Maillard browning. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Respiration Rate (Closed System)

**Problem Statement:**
A 2 kg batch of bananas is sealed in a 5 L container with initial CO₂ concentration 0.04 %. After 4 h, CO₂ rises to 0.42 %. Compute respiration rate.

### Given
- $m = 2$ kg (produce mass)
- $V = 5$ L (jar volume)
- $C_i = 0.04 \,\%$
- $C_f = 0.42 \,\%$
- $t = 4$ h

### Step 1 — Volume of CO₂ Produced

$$
\Delta C = C_f - C_i = 0.42 - 0.04 = 0.38 \,\%
$$

> Note: 0.38 % of 5 L container = volume of CO₂ produced.

$$
V_{CO_2} = \Delta C \times V = 0.0038 \times 5 = 0.019 \,\text{L} = 19 \,\text{mL}
$$

### Step 2 — Respiration Rate

$$
R_{CO_2} = \dfrac{V_{CO_2}}{m \cdot t}
$$

$$
R_{CO_2} = \dfrac{19}{2 \times 4} = \dfrac{19}{8}
$$

$$
R_{CO_2} = 2.375 \,\text{mL CO}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1}\cdot \text{h}^{-1}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Banana respiration rates typically range **15–30 mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹ at 20 °C** (climacteric peak). A low value here indicates pre-climacteric stage or low storage temperature. ✓

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Q₁₀ Acceleration Factor

**Problem Statement:**
Respiration rate of strawberries is 12 mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹ at 10 °C and 30 mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹ at 20 °C. Find Q₁₀.

### Step 1 — Apply Q₁₀ Formula

$$
Q_{10} = \left( \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} \right)^{10 / (T_2 - T_1)}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
Q_{10} = \left( \dfrac{30}{12} \right)^{10 / (20 - 10)}
$$

$$
Q_{10} = (2.5)^{10/10}
$$

$$
Q_{10} = 2.5^1 = 2.5
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

> **Result:** $Q_{10} = 2.5$ means every 10 °C rise **doubles** the respiration rate. This is the typical value for fresh produce (range 2–3). Useful for predicting shelf life at different storage temperatures. ✓

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Sphericity of a Mango

**Problem Statement:**
A mango has length **L = 12 cm**, width **W = 8 cm**, thickness **T = 7 cm**. Compute sphericity.

### Step 1 — Cube Root of Volume Approximation

$$
(L \cdot W \cdot T)^{1/3} = (12 \times 8 \times 7)^{1/3}
$$

$$
= (672)^{1/3}
$$

$$
= 8.75 \,\text{cm}
$$

### Step 2 — Sphericity Formula

$$
\phi = \dfrac{(L \cdot W \cdot T)^{1/3}}{L}
$$

$$
\phi = \dfrac{8.75}{12} = 0.729
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

| $\phi$ Value | Shape Description |
|---|---|
| > 0.9 | Spherical |
| 0.7 – 0.9 | Near-spherical (mango) |
| 0.5 – 0.7 | Oblong |
| < 0.5 | Highly elongated |

> **Result:** $\phi = 0.73$ → mango is **near-spherical**, suitable for packing in regular boxes. ✓

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Matrix for KTU Exam / Viva**

| Attribute to Measure | Best Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Firmness (whole fruit) | Magness-Taylor penetrometer | Industry standard |
| Internal browning | MRI / X-ray | Non-destructive |
| Sugar content (rapid) | Hand refractometer (°Brix) | Quick, portable |
| Vitamin C | DCPIP titration | Specific to ascorbic acid |
| Microbial load | TPC agar plate | Gold standard |
| Color uniformity | Hunter Lab colorimeter | Objective, repeatable |
| Off-aroma / off-flavor | Sensory panel | No instrument matches human nose |
| Bruise detection | Hyperspectral imaging | Detects sub-surface damage |
| Maturity index | Sugar–Acid ratio | Industry index for citrus, grapes |
| Fat content | Soxhlet extraction | Reference method |
| Protein (general) | Kjeldahl | Universal N-protien method |
| Crispness (chips) | TPA (Three-point bend test) | Industry standard |

---

## 3.8 Numerical Practice Set (Model Solutions Provided)

### Problem A — Moisture Content
A sample of **wheat flour** has initial mass **120 g** and final mass after drying **102 g**. Compute:
(i) MC on wet basis
(ii) MC on dry basis

**Solution:**
- $m_w = 120 - 102 = 18$ g
- $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{18}{120} \times 100 = 15 \,\%$
- $MC_{db} = \dfrac{18}{102} \times 100 = 17.65 \,\%$

### Problem B — Titratable Acidity
5 g of orange juice is titrated with 0.1 N NaOH. The volume used is 7.5 mL. Calculate TA (as citric acid, equivalent weight = 64).

$$
TA(\%) = \dfrac{7.5 \times 0.1 \times 64 \times 100}{5 \times 1000} = \dfrac{4800}{5000} = 0.96 \,\%
$$

### Problem C — Vitamin C
10 g of amla juice requires 18.5 mL of 0.05 % DCPIP solution (1 mL DCPIP = 0.5 mg ascorbic acid). Compute vitamin C in mg/100 g.

$$
V_C = \dfrac{18.5 \times 0.5}{10} \times 100 = 92.5 \,\text{mg per 100 g}
$$

> **Result:** Amla is exceptionally rich in vitamin C — typical values 400–900 mg/100 g. The lower value suggests dilution or processing loss.

> **Diagram Interpretation:** All four quality domains converge on the same end-decision: **grading, sorting, acceptance, or rejection** of the food material. This convergence is the heart of post-harvest quality management.

## 4.2 Computer Vision Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Sample on Conveyor"] --> P2["Illumination Chamber D65"]
    P2 --> P3["RGB or Multispectral Camera"]
    P3 --> P4["Analog to Digital Converter"]
    P4 --> P5["Image Pre Processing\nNoise Removal and Calibration"]
    P5 --> P6["Image Segmentation\nThreshold or ML Based"]
    P6 --> P7["Feature Extraction\nColor Texture Shape"]
    P7 --> P8["Classifier\nSVM ANN CNN"]
    P8 --> P9["Decision Output\nGrade A B C or Reject"]
    P9 --> P10["Actuator\nPneumatic Diverter"]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** This is the canonical pipeline used in **automated sorting lines** for apples, mangoes, tomatoes, almonds, and rice. Each block corresponds to a unit operation in image-based quality assessment.

---

## 4.3 Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) Force-Time Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0["Probe Starts"] --> T1["Downstroke Begins"]
    T1 --> T2["Peak Force = Hardness"]
    T2 --> T3["Hold for Set Time"]
    T3 --> T4["Upstroke Begins\nAdhesiveness = Negative Area"]
    T4 --> T5["Wait Period = 5 Seconds"]
    T5 --> T6["Second Downstroke"]
    T6 --> T7["Second Peak Force"]
    T7 --> T8["Cohesiveness = Area2 divided by Area1"]
    T8 --> T9["Final Upstroke"]
    T9 --> T10["Springiness = Distance Recovery"]
    T10 --> T11["End of TPA Test"]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The TPA curve maps chewing mechanics onto instrument data. **Hardness**, **Cohesiveness**, **Springiness**, and **Chewiness** are calculated from this curve and reported in product specifications.

---

## 4.4 Respiration Pattern of Climacteric vs Non-Climacteric Fruits

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Harvest"] --> R2{"Climacteric Fruit"}
    R2 --> R3["Pre Climacteric Minimum"]
    R3 --> R4["Climacteric Peak\nCO2 and Ethylene Surge"]
    R4 --> R5["Post Climacteric Decline"]
    R5 --> R6["Senescence"]

    R1 --> R7{"Non Climacteric Fruit"}
    R7 --> R8["Gradual Decline in CO2"]
    R8 --> R9["Slow Senescence"]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** Climacteric fruits (banana, mango, tomato, apple) have a **respiratory burst** accompanied by ethylene, while non-climacteric fruits (citrus, grape, strawberry) decline gradually. This dictates storage strategy — climacteric fruits benefit from **1-MCP / controlled ripening**, non-climacteric from **rapid cooling**.

---

## 4.5 Proximate Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Food Sample"] --> S2["Weigh Initial Mass W1"]
    S2 --> S3["Oven Dry at 105 C to Constant Mass"]
    S3 --> S4["Weigh Final Mass W2"]
    S4 --> S5["Moisture = W1 minus W2"]
    S5 --> S6["Dry Residue = W2"]
    S6 --> S7["Incinerate at 550 C for Ash"]
    S7 --> S8["Ash = Mass after incineration"]
    S8 --> S9["Organic Matter = W2 minus Ash"]
    S9 --> S10["Kjeldahl for Nitrogen and Protein"]
    S9 --> S11["Soxhlet for Fat"]
    S10 --> S12["Final Proximate Composition Report"]
    S11 --> S12
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** This is the **classical Weende proximate analysis** used in every food testing lab. The carbohydrate content is then computed **by difference**: 100 − (moisture + ash + protein + fat).

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between wet-basis and dry-basis moisture content. A sample of paddy weighing 100 g contains 14 % moisture (wet basis). Find its dry-basis moisture content.**

**Model Answer:**
Wet basis moisture = mass of water ÷ mass of wet sample × 100.
Dry basis moisture = mass of water ÷ mass of dry sample × 100.

$$
MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}} \times 100
$$

$$
MC_{db} = \dfrac{14}{100 - 14} \times 100 = \dfrac{14}{86} \times 100 = 16.28 \,\%
$$

> **Answer:** **16.28 % (db)**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is Sugar–Acid Ratio? Why is it used as a maturity index in fruits?**

**Model Answer:**
Sugar–Acid Ratio (SAR) is defined as the ratio of Total Soluble Solids (°Brix) to Titratable Acidity (%) of fruit juice.

$$
SAR = \dfrac{^\circ Brix}{TA\,(\%)}
$$

**Why it works:**
- As fruits ripen, sugars accumulate (°Brix rises) and organic acids are respired (TA falls).
- The ratio therefore **monotonically increases** during maturation.
- Consumers perceive a balanced sweet-sour taste when SAR is in the optimal range (15–25 for table grapes, 30–40 for mango).
- It is **simple, cheap, and reliable** — needing only a refractometer and a titration setup.

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### ✍️ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the various **physical methods** used for quality evaluation of post-harvest food materials. Discuss dimensional, gravimetric, mechanical, and optical methods in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A sample of fresh tomato weighs **180 g** before drying and **22.5 g** after drying in a hot air oven. Compute:
1. Moisture content on wet basis
2. Moisture content on dry basis
3. The mass of water removed per kg of fresh tomato. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Physical Methods — Detailed Answer:**

**1. Dimensional Methods:**
Size (length, width, thickness) measured by vernier caliper; shape by sphericity index $\phi = (LWT)^{1/3}/L$; volume by water displacement or gas pycnometer.

**2. Gravimetric Methods:**
Mass by electronic balance; bulk density $\rho_b$ by filling a known-volume container; true density $\rho_t$ by pycnometer; porosity $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_t$.

**3. Mechanical / Textural Methods:**
Firmness by Magness-Taylor penetrometer; comprehensive texture by Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) giving hardness, cohesiveness, springiness, chewiness, adhesiveness. Puncture and shear tests for cereals and nuts.

**4. Optical / Color Methods:**
Color by Hunter Lab or CIE L\*a\*b\* colorimeter; lightness (L\*), redness/greenness (a\*), yellowness (b\*). Total color difference $\Delta E$ compares two samples. Whiteness index for flour; Browning Index for fried foods.

**5. Thermal / Electrical / Acoustic Methods:**
Specific heat by DSC; thermal conductivity by line probe; dielectric constant by LCR meter; acoustic resonance for firmness (stiffness factor $S = f^2 m^{2/3}$).

**6. Non-Destructive Imaging:**
NIR for sugar/firmness; X-ray for internal defects; MRI for moisture maps; hyperspectral imaging for chemical composition maps.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $W_i = 180$ g, $W_f = 22.5$ g

**(1) Moisture on Wet Basis:**
$$
MC_{wb} = \dfrac{180 - 22.5}{180} \times 100 = \dfrac{157.5}{180} \times 100 = 87.5 \,\%
$$

**(2) Moisture on Dry Basis:**
$$
MC_{db} = \dfrac{157.5}{22.5} \times 100 = 700 \,\%
$$

**(3) Water Removed per kg:**
$$
m_w / \text{kg} = \dfrac{157.5}{180} \times 1000 = 875 \,\text{g/kg}
$$

> **Result:** Tomato is **87.5 % water (wb)** — typical for fresh ripe tomato.

### ✍️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Describe in detail the **chemical methods** of food quality evaluation with special reference to pH, titratable acidity, TSS, and proximate composition. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fruit juice sample of **10 g** required **8.5 mL of 0.1 N NaOH** for titration. Calculate the titratable acidity as citric acid (equivalent weight = 64). If the juice has a TSS of **12 °Brix**, calculate the Sugar–Acid Ratio. Comment on the fruit's maturity and possible quality. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Chemical Methods — Detailed Answer:**

**1. Moisture Content:**
Oven drying (105 °C), Karl Fischer titration (low-moisture), toluene distillation (oils). Expressed as wet or dry basis.

**2. pH:**
Potentiometric method using glass electrode + reference electrode. Measures free H⁺ activity. Important in fermentation control, fruit juice quality, dairy.

**3. Titratable Acidity (TA):**
Titrating against standardized NaOH to endpoint pH 8.1 (phenolphthalein). Expressed as % dominant acid — citric for citrus/mango, malic for apple, tartaric for grapes.

**4. Total Soluble Solids (TSS / °Brix):**
Refractometric method using Brix hydrometer or digital refractometer at 20 °C. Correlates strongly with sugar content in fruits.

**5. Proximate Composition:**
Weende scheme: Moisture, Ash, Protein (Kjeldahl: N × 6.25), Fat (Soxhlet), Crude Fiber (acid-alkali), Carbohydrate (by difference).

**6. Pigments & Phytochemicals:**
Chlorophyll (acetone extraction + spectrophotometry at 663/645 nm), Carotenoids (480 nm), Anthocyanins (pH differential), Vitamin C (DCPIP titration), Antioxidants (DPPH/ABTS).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $V_{NaOH} = 8.5$ mL
- $N = 0.1$
- $W = 10$ g
- Equivalent weight of citric acid = 64
- TSS = 12 °Brix

**(1) Titratable Acidity:**
$$
TA(\%) = \dfrac{V \times N \times E \times 100}{W \times 1000}
$$

Substitute:
$$
TA(\%) = \dfrac{8.5 \times 0.1 \times 64 \times 100}{10 \times 1000}
$$

$$
TA(\%) = \dfrac{5440}{10000} = 0.544 \,\%
$$

**(2) Sugar–Acid Ratio:**
$$
SAR = \dfrac{^\circ Brix}{TA\,(\%)} = \dfrac{12}{0.544} = 22.06
$$

**(3) Interpretation:**
SAR = 22 indicates the fruit is in the **good table-quality range** (15–25). The fruit is mature, with a balanced sweet–sour taste suitable for fresh consumption.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**

> **Common mistake 1:** Students confuse **wet basis** and **dry basis** moisture content and lose 2–3 marks per question. **Always re-read** the question stem.
>
> **Common mistake 2:** In TA calculations, students **forget the factor of 1000** in the denominator (because weight is taken in grams and normality is in meq/mL). Marks are docked if not applied.
>
> **Common mistake 3:** In TPA, students mix up the **order of TPA parameters** — hardness comes first, not chewiness. Chewiness = Hardness × Cohesiveness × Springiness.
>
> **Common mistake 4:** Confusing **Hunter Lab** with **CIE L\*a\*b\***. Hunter Lab uses lowercase (L, a, b); CIE uses starred (L*, a*, b*). They are mathematically different.
>
> **Common mistake 5:** In respiration calculations, students forget that CO₂ concentration must be converted from **% to fraction** before multiplying by jar volume. Otherwise answer is off by **100×**.

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

### ✅ PHYSICAL METHODS
- [ ] Sphericity: $\phi = (LWT)^{1/3}/L$
- [ ] Porosity: $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_t$
- [ ] TPA parameters: Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Chewiness, Adhesiveness, Resilience
- [ ] $\Delta E < 1$ = no perceptible color difference
- [ ] Stiffness factor $S = f^2 m^{2/3}$ — acoustic firmness
- [ ] NIR, MRI, X-ray, Hyperspectral = non-destructive imaging

### ✅ CHEMICAL METHODS
- [ ] MC (wb) vs MC (db) — know both formulas
- [ ] $MC_{db} = MC_{wb} / (1 - MC_{wb}/100)$
- [ ] TA(%) formula: remember the **× 100 / × 1000** trap
- [ ] °Brix measured at **20 °C** on refractometer
- [ ] SAR = °Brix ÷ TA(%)
- [ ] Protein = %N × 6.25 (general factor)
- [ ] Chlorophyll a = 12.7 A₆₆₃ − 2.69 A₆₄₅
- [ ] Vitamin C by DCPIP titration — **1 mL DCPIP = 0.5 mg ascorbic acid**

### ✅ BIOLOGICAL METHODS
- [ ] TPC units = **CFU/g** at 30–37 °C / 48 h
- [ ] Respiration units = **mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹**
- [ ] Climacteric fruits (banana, mango, tomato, apple) have ethylene surge
- [ ] Non-climacteric (citrus, grape, strawberry) — no ethylene burst
- [ ] $Q_{10}$ typically 2–3 for fresh produce
- [ ] Sensory: 9-point hedonic scale, Triangle test for difference

### ✅ VISUAL METHODS
- [ ] Computer vision pipeline: Acquire → Pre-process → Segment → Extract → Classify → Decide
- [ ] CIE L\*a\*b\* — light source D65 standard
- [ ] Munsell: Hue, Value, Chroma
- [ ] ΔE: < 1 imperceptible, > 5 strongly different
- [ ] Hyperspectral imaging = hundreds of wavelengths
- [ ] RGB cameras for visible defects; multispectral for sub-surface

### 🔁 Quick-Recall Conversions
- 1 ppm = 1 mg/kg
- °Brix ≈ % TSS (sucrose equivalent)
- 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar
- 1 cal = 4.184 J
- 1 BTU = 1055 J
